process: Discrimination Training, Differential Reinforcement, Precision of Performance, Supplementary Reinforcement
abstract: Children learned to respond differentially to four tones when correct responses were indicatedby re-sounding the tone and flashing a signal lamp (simple knowledge of results). Correct re-sponses were then made to advance a display counter. With this procedure, rate of responseincreased, but accuracy decreased. Mild punishment-points subtracted from the count-wasthen arranged for incorrect responses. Accuracy returned to or above its previous level.Response rate tended to remain above its initial level. These results indicate that when sup-plementary reinforcers are employed, precise contingencies must be arranged to ensure de-sired behavior.

title: Punishment Shock Intensity and Basal Skin Resistance
process: Supressive Effects of Shock Punishment, Schedules of Reinforcement: Continuous Reinforcement, Operant Conditioning, Respondent Conditioning
abstract: The relationship between punishment shock intensity and basal skin resistance (BSR) wasinvestigated in two sessions with human females selected for their ability to maintain afairly substantial operant rate under a wide range of shock intensities. In both sessionseach button-pressing response was reinforced with a counter tally. Subjects were paid onecent for each 20 counts. In session 1, punishment followed each response during alternate4-min periods; in session 2 punishment was programmed in all 4-min periods. Shock in-tensities were presented randomly among the 4-min shock periods, with the restrictionthat the first three presentations occurred in ascending order. Operant responding showedsome suppression at higher shock intensities in session 1, with substantial recovery in mostsubjects during session 2. Respondent behavior was characterized by greater activity atsuccessively higher intensities, with recovery at all shock levels, especially the lowest levels,apparent during the second session.

title: Yoked Control without a Duplicate Apparatus: A Response Record-Playback System
process: Operant Conditioning, Negative Reinforcement, Stimulus Control
abstract: This text describes the yoked control experimental design, a methodology in which each experimental subject is paired with a control subject whose stimuli are directly determined by the experimental subject’s behavior. In the context of a Skinner box shock-escape experiment, this design ensures that each control subject experiences the same temporal pattern of shock-off periods as its paired experimental counterpart, regardless of its own behavior. The yoked control technique is used to analyze the effects of response–event contingencies on behavior and has been a standard method in behavioral research. To reduce apparatus costs, the text proposes an alternative setup using a single experimental chamber coupled with a response record–playback system based on a magnetic tape recorder. The system records behavioral responses, such as bar presses, as bursts of 1000-cycle sine wave voltage signals, which can later be replayed to replicate the experimental conditions for control sessions. This approach enables efficient and automated operation, minimizing equipment expenses while maintaining experimental accuracy.

title: Classical Conditioning of Aggressive Display in Siamese Fighting Fish
process: Respondent Conditioning, Classical Conditioning, Elicited Aggression, Extinction
abstract: Unconditioned aggressive-display behavior elicited by the mirror image of a male SiameseFighting Fish was brought under the control of a previously ineffective stimulus by classicalconditioning. A stimulus light repeatedly paired with mirror presentation came to elicit thecomplex aggressive-behavior sequence. Relative rates of acquisition of four components ofthe display were compared. Fin erection and undulating movements were acquired mostrapidly while gill-cover erection and frontal approach were acquired most slowly. A discrimi-native conditioning procedure revealed that the response was specifically elicited by theconditioned stimulus, and not a sensitization artifact.

title: Operant Discrimination of an Interoceptive Stimulus in Rhesus Monkeys
authors: Slucki, Henry, Adam, Gyorgi, Porter, Robert W.
process: Operant Discrimination, Stimulus Control, Operant Conditioning, Visceral Stimulation, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Ratio
abstract: Five rhesus macaques monkeys surgically prepared with Thiry small intestinal (jejunum)loops and implanted brain electrodes were restrained in primate chairs and kept on 23-hrdeprivation-feeding cycle. After being trained to press a lever for sugar pills on an FR 25schedule of reinforcement, a discrimination training procedure was established. Lever presseswere reinforced during the SD-a non-aversive mechanical stimulus applied to the internalwalls of the Thiry loop by rhythmic inflation-deflation of a small latex balloon by air at therate of one cycle per sec at 100 mm Hg pressure. The SA was the absence of the visceral stim-ulation. The monkeys successfully discriminated between presence and absence of the internalstimulus. A discrimination reversal was attempted and completed on one monkey. The re-sults clearly show operant discrimination based on an interoceptive stimulus. Cortical andsubcortical EEG records reflected the onset but not termination of the visceral stimulation.

title: A Transient Suppression Guide
static-equation-definitions: C: the capacitance in microfarads, R: resistance in ohms, I: current, E: voltage or electromotive force.
abstract: When the current in an inductive circuit is suddenly interrupted, a transient self-induced voltage arises due to the rapid decay of the magnetic field. The magnitude of this transient depends on the field’s strength, its rate of decay, and the circuit resistance. In the absence of suppression networks, such transients can produce destructive arcing across relay contacts or semiconductor junctions, leading to material erosion, contact welding, or component failure. Arc suppression circuits are designed to mitigate these effects by providing an alternate discharge path, thereby protecting relay contacts, safeguarding electronic devices, and preventing false triggering in circuits. Various suppression methods are reviewed, including resistor-capacitor (RC) networks, reverse-biased diodes, neon bulbs, zener diodes, and varistors. The limitations of neon lamps and varistors are discussed, and practical recommendations are made for the use of diode and RC suppression circuits in both conventional electromechanical relays and modern hybrid systems. The analysis emphasizes the physical principles governing transient suppression and offers guidance for effective protection of inductive loads such as relays, solenoids, motors, and stepping switches.

title: A Note on Time Out from Avoidance with the Chimpanzee
process: Fixed-Interval Responding, Shock Avoidance Schedules, Extinction
abstract: Fixed-interval responding which produced time out from shock avoidance schedules was estab-lished in a chimpanzee. Two widely differing discriminated avoidance schedules were employedin a multiple schedule arrangement. Differences in fixed interval rate were found to be relatedboth to the schedule from which the subject was escaping and to the amoung of training.

title: Experimental Control during an Earthquake
authors: Sloane, Howard N. Jr., Harper, James Larry
process: Differential Reinforcement, Conditional Discrimination, Time-Out, Punishment, Token Economy
abstract: This study examined performance under a simultaneous matching-to-sample procedure using an apparatus that delivered differential consequences for correct and incorrect responses. Each trial began with the presentation of a sample stimulus; touching the sample terminated it and produced 1–5 comparison stimuli. Correct selections produced a chime, brief illumination, and delivery of a bead that could later be exchanged for candy. Incorrect selections produced a buzzer and a 5.8-s blackout, after which the same sample was re-presented. Responses to the sample lasting longer than 0.3 s were required for reinforcement, and premature or sustained responding to comparison windows delayed stimulus presentation. Sessions continued until all 80 stimuli were correctly matched, with slide order reorganized every five sessions to prevent serial learning. During the 35th session, an unexpected 30–45-s earthquake (Richter scale magnitude 7) occurred, providing a naturally occurring disruption to ongoing performance. The procedural details and unplanned environmental event highlight considerations for stimulus control, response requirements, and session integrity in laboratory-based matching-to-sample research.

title: The Effect of Multiple S-delta Periods on Responding on a Fixed-Interval Schedule:III. Effect of Changes in Pattern of Interruptions, Parameters and Stimuli
process: Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed-Interval, Discrimination Training, Differential Reinforcement, Scheudules of Reinforcment: Fixed-Rate

title: A Simple Chronic Cortical Electrode for the Monkey
authors: Macchitelli, Frank J., Montanarelli, Nicholas Jr.
process: Respondent Conditioning, Reflexing Responses, Behavioral Stability for Recording
abstract: A method is described for recording cerebral cortical activity in semi-restrained monkeys using permanently implanted extradural Nu-Way snap studs as electrodes. The technique provides a secure, low-profile alternative to bulky external connectors and facilitates routine electroencephalographic (EEG) monitoring. Implantation is straightforward and requires no specialized tools. Following anesthesia and stereotaxic positioning, holes are drilled at predetermined cranial locations, tapped, and fitted with snap-stud electrodes seated to the level of the dura. Dental acrylic is used to affix the studs, and the scalp is repositioned and incised to allow each stud to protrude externally before final suturing. Antibiotics are administered postoperatively. The complete surgical procedure requires approximately 60 minutes and yields a durable, easily accessible recording interface suitable for repeated cortical monitoring in behavioral research.

title: A Multiple Stimulus Programming System
abstract: The described system enables the automatic and economical presentation of multiple visual stimuli using a recycling 35-mm slide projector in combination with visual readout devices and relay–photocell circuitry. By projecting opaque slides containing coded holes onto an array of photoconductive cells—each paired with an independent relay—the system selectively activates lamps in a visual display unit. Illumination of a photocell decreases its resistance, allowing current to trigger the corresponding relay, which then controls the appropriate display lamp. This design replicates functions typically achieved through solid-state control while offering lower cost, broader switching capacity, and compatibility with both relay-based and solid-state programming equipment.

title: Effects of Delayed Reinforcement in a Concurrent Situation
process: Delayed Reinforcement, Operant Conditioning, Schedules of Reinfrocment: Variable Interval, Exponential Function
abstract: Pigeons were trained to peck either of two response keys for food reinforcement on equatedaperiodic schedules. Delays of reinforcement for pecks at one key reduced the relative frequencyof pecking exponentially as a function of the delay interval. Similar functions were obtainedwhen other dependent variables were plotted against the delay interval.

title: The Silicon Controlled Rectifier as a Switching Device in Operant-Conditioning Experiments
process: Operant Conditioning Apparatus, High Voltage Device Control
abstract: The silicon-controlled rectifier (SCR), a four-layer semiconductor device, demonstrates strong potential as a switching component in operant conditioning equipment, especially where low-voltage programming circuits must control high-voltage or high-current loads. Because many operant conditioning tasks require reliable control of AC power—such as for flicker-free stimulus lights, motors, or solenoid feeders—the SCR offers a feasible, economical, and safe switching method. The described apparatus was developed for pigeon visual discrimination experiments but is adaptable to a wide range of situations requiring AC power switching. In operation, the SCR remains nonconductive in a DC circuit until a small gate potential is applied, after which it becomes conductive and remains so until current is externally interrupted.

title: A Central Probability- Generating Station
process: Probabilistic Control of Vatiables, Input Pulse Shaping, Multiple Probability Outputs
abstract: Some experimental procedures require independent variables to vary probabilistically around an expected mean rather than being fixed at a constant value. Although several laboratory devices exist for producing random variation, most generate only a single fixed probability at any given setting, limiting their usefulness to one experiment at a time. The probability generator described here overcomes this limitation by providing multiple, simultaneously available probability outputs. The system uses a clock-driven series of flip-flops whose outputs feed AND gates; these gates also receive brief, shaped input pulses produced by a one-shot circuit. Each AND gate then controls a power-handling device, such as a relay driver, producing probabilistic output events based on the state of the flip-flop and the timing of the shaped input pulse. This configuration allows several distinct probabilities to be generated concurrently using a single piece of equipment.

title: Some Discriminative Properties of Fixed Ratio Performance in the Pigeon
process: Discrimination Training, Conditional Discrimination, Differential Reinforcement, Extinction, Schedules of Reinforcement: Mixed, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Ratio, Reinforcement Pause
abstract: The discriminative control over a spatial choice response exercised by prior behavior was studied using a procedure involving discrete exposures to a two-member chained schedule. The initial member (red key) was either a smaller or larger fixed ratio (Mix FR:FR), the completion of which produced, after a 1-sec delay, two white response keys. If the larger FR had been completed as the initial chain member, a single peck on the right white key was reinforced; after the smaller FR, a peck on the left white key was reinforced. Frequencies of unreinforced responses (SΔ responses) were determined with several pairs of red-key FRs: 95-5, 75-25, 65-35, 60-40, 58-42 and 50-50. The SΔ response frequencies were low through the FR pair 65-35; sharp increases were obtained with pairs 60-40 and 58-42. Later, curves analogous to stimulus generalization functions were obtained using a probe procedure. Finally, the delay interval between completion of a red-key FR and the white-key choice response was manipulated: results were variable, but SΔ response frequencies tended to increase with increasing delays.

title: Interocular Generalization: A Study of Mirror-Image Reversal Following Monocular Discrimination Training in the Pigeon
process: Generalization, Discrimination Training, Schedules of Reinforcement: Differential Reinforcement of Other Behavior, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval, Stimulus Control, Generalization Gradient, Interoculur Generalization
abstract: Generalization gradients along a continuum of angular orientation were obtained from four pigeons, following monocular training on a discrimination between a 45° oblique line (S+) and a 135° oblique line (S–). All pigeons were trained on a chain DRO VI 1 schedule of reinforcement. Generalization gradients obtained with the trained and untrained eye were compared. All pigeons responded maximally to the 45° line (S+) when tested with only the trained eye open. During generalization tests of interocular transfer with only the untrained eye open, three pigeons responded maximally to S– (135°), the mirror-image of the stimulus associated with reinforcement during training (45°). The other pigeon failed to show interocular transfer of the discrimination. Interocular reversal of left-right mirror-image stimuli has not been reported for any other species.

title: An Easily-Constructed Cable Swivel Device for Brain Stimulation Experiments
authors: Johnson, Roger N., Trehub, Arnold, Pietskowski, Edward
abstract: Electrophysiological stimulation studies with freely moving animals are frequently hindered by lead twisting, and existing commercial slip-ring systems are either costly or impractical for small species. Prior solutions—such as multi-conductor mercury bath couplers or more complex swivel devices—are often unnecessary for experiments requiring only two conductors. The apparatus described here provides a low-cost, easily constructed alternative based on two high-quality metallic ball-bearing assemblies that enable stable electrical continuity while allowing unrestricted rotation. The outer rings of the bearings are rigidly mounted, while the inner rings rotate freely and carry the electrode leads; current is transmitted from fixed terminals on the outer rings, through the bearings, to the rotating inner rings. A simple Plexiglas mount and metal socket wafer secure the structure without compromising bearing integrity

title: The Motivational Properties of Schedule-Induced Polydipsia
process: SCheduled of Reinforcement: Continuous Reinforcement, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval, Schedules of Reinforement: Fixed Ratio, Motivating Operations: Establishing Operation
abstract: Schedule-induced polydipsia occurred during initial magazine training to Noyes pellets (45 mg), disappeared when lever-pressing was acquired on a continuous reinforcement schedule (CRF), and reappeared when the food contingency was changed to a 1-min variable interval schedule (VI 1 min). Polydipsia also developed under a VI 1 min food schedule when water was concurrently available on various fixed ratios (FR), rather than being freely available. The level of the polydipsia and its motivating properties allow it to be classified as a form of adjunctive behavior.

title: A Pulse Former for Counter-Printers
process: Measurement System, Measurement Reliability, Response Effort, Stimulus Control
abstract: Counter-printers have become standard in programming apparatus due to their ability to produce data in precise, digital printed form. Although the Sodeco ITPB3 and comparable units from Texas Instruments operate reliably on 24-V DC, their design incorporates a telephone-switching configuration in which the positive line serves as the operate pulse. These units are typically supplied without pulse formers for essential functions such as counting, printing, and resetting. Using independent commercial pulse formers for each function results in bulky, high-cost systems that provide insufficient protection for the sensitive internal coils. Additionally, commercially available formers do not adequately prevent smeared recordings caused by simultaneous counting and printing. The need for a streamlined, protective, and behaviorally reliable pulse-forming system is therefore essential to ensure accurate printed outputs in experimental programming apparatus.

title: Threshold for Conditioned Supression using X-Rays as the Pre-Aversive Stimulus
process: Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval, Punishment: Positive, Conditioned Supression, Operant Condiitioning
abstract: Four male, 12-week-old Sprague-Dawley rats were used to determine the threshold for behavioral response to X-irradiation using the conditioned suppression technique. They were maintained at 80 per cent body weight and initially trained to stable performance on a VI 1 min schedule with 16 per cent sucrose solution as reinforcement. After a stable baseline was obtained, animals were placed in the instrumental conditioning box beneath the X-ray machine for a half-hour session each day. While subjects were actively pressing the lever for reinforcement, 15-sec X-ray exposures of 0.5 R/sec were administered, followed immediately by electric shock. After all animals had exhibited conditioned suppression, the dose-rate was decreased in subsequent sessions in an effort to establish threshold. The results indicate that X-rays at a dose-rate as low as 0.004 R/sec can be an effective pre-aversive stimulus for the rat.

title: An Inexpensive Automatically Programmed Monochromator
process: Stimulus Control, Discrimination Training, Behavior Momentum, Response Allocation, Reinforcement: Negative
abstract: Recent investigations of stimulus control often require systems capable of projecting narrow-band monochromatic light onto response keys. Traditional methods typically involve passing white light through either monochromators or fixed interference filters. Although commercial monochromators offer continuous wavelength selection and can be automatically programmed for rapid stimulus changes, their high cost (over $1,000 prior to automation) and insufficient light intensity limit their practicality. The monochromator described here provides a cost-effective alternative that produces 15 high-intensity, narrow-band stimuli that can be projected instantaneously onto a translucent response key. Constructed for approximately $400, the system has demonstrated nearly two years of uninterrupted operation. The device uses a continuous interference filter mounted within a metal frame, transmitting wavelengths that vary linearly along the filter’s length. Calibration with a Bausch and Lomb spectrometer confirmed manufacturer specifications regarding wavelength linearity, half-width (approximately 12 mµ), and transmission levels (26–33.5%). This system improves accessibility to reliable monochromatic stimuli for stimulus-control research without compromising intensity or performance.

title: Schedule Induced Polydipsoa as a Function of Fixed Interval Length
process: Operant Condition, Reinforcement: Positive, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Interval
abstract: Rats were trained to bar-press for Noyes pellets on an FI schedule which was increased serially through several values from 2 sec to as high as 300 sec. Concurrently, water was freely available. As FI length was increased, the degree of polydipsia increased linearly to a maximum value.

title: A Treatment for Chronic Respiratory Disease (Snuffles) in the Rat
abstract: During shipment from vendors, rats maysometimes contact colds, "red nose", chronicrespiratory disease (CRD or snuffles), middleear disease or other ailments. New animals caneasily be replaced, but such infectious diseasesalso crop up in well-tended animals and mayprecipitate an epidemic. When snuffles spreadthrough our colony, some animals were iso-lated in an attempt to find a treatment.Bacterial cultures of lung tissue were negative.Heavy doses of penicillin (Crystifor 400, E. R.Squibb and Sons), oxytetracycline HC1 (Terra-mycin, Chas. Pfizer and -Co.) administered indrinking water or intraperitoneally to differ-ent groups of animals did not effect a cure.But chloramphenicol (Chloromycetin, Park,Davis and Co.) in very heavy doses was ef-fective.

title: A Comparison of Two Types of Extinction Following Fixed Ration Training
authors: Weissman, Norman W., Crossman, Edward, K.
process: Reinforcement: Positive, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Ratio, Exinction
abstract: Five groups of pigeons were food reinforced on various schedules. Half of each group were extinguished in the normal manner; the others were presented with a stimulus change, previously paired with reinforcement, each time they completed their respective fixed ratios. Response rate in training was an increasing negatively accelerated function of the FR. Increasing the FR produced transitory rate changes, the amount of which yielded a quantitative index of ratio strain. Cumulative records of extinction performance revealed that the stimulus change exerted discriminative control by maintaining the cohesiveness of FR response units. Nevertheless, neither the absolute number of extinction responses nor extinction response units differed appreciably for the two extinction procedures.

title: Three Manipulanda for the Study of Human Infants Operant Play
A Rapid Method for making 2 by 2 IN. Slides
abstract: The Kodak Carousel projector has beenused with increasing frequency for presentingvisual stimuli in research with humans(Hively, 1962; Hively, 1964; Siegel, 1964).Some disadvantages of the conventional pho-tographic procedure of making slides, par-ticularly during the developmental stages oftraining programs, are the expense and delayinvolved in the photographic process. Amethod for making 2 by 2 in. slides has beendeveloped which avoids these problems, andis satisfactory for a variety of experimentalapplications.The best background for the slide is a me-dium opacity tracing paper (trade name Kovel)which sells for $28.20 per thousand 8½2 by11 in. sheets. The paper was purchased locallyfrom Charles Bruning, 2546 Broadway, KansasCity, Missouri. The slides were made for athree-choice matching-to-sample task presentedby rear projection onto a panel similar to thatdescribed by Hively (1964). The projector islocated 17 in. behind the response panelswhich results in an enlargement of 1:6 witha 3-in. projector lens.

title: A Mercury Switch Grid Scrambler for Aversive Conditioning
authors: Owen, John E. Jr., Kellermeier, A.P.
abstract: The grid scrambler described here has beenproven reliable and silent in over 1000 hrof operation on the shock titration schedule.As shown schematically in Fig. 1, it is a motor-driven cam shaft which rocks single pole, dou-ble throw mercury switches, A (Mercoid mer-cury switch No. 9-64R at $2.70). The polarityof the current to any grid of the experimentalcage is determined by the relative positionsof the cam, F, and switch for that grid. Twoswitches are operated by each cam, one atthe top and the other at the bottom of thecam.

title: Punishment of an Extinguishing Shock Avoidance Response by Time Out from Positive Reinforcement
process: Punishment: Negative, Reinforcement: Positive, Extinction, Discriminated Shock Avoidance, Schedules of Reinforcement: Concurrent, Noncontingent Time Out, Punishment: Positive, Reinforcement: Negative, Discrimination Training
abstract: Two experiments were conducted to determine the effects of punishment by time-out from positive reinforcement on the extinction of discriminated shock-avoidance responding. Subjects were trained initially to bar press for food on an intermittent schedule of reinforcement and, concurrently, to avoid shock at the onset of a warning signal. Experiment I compared avoidance extinction performance under no punishment and when avoidance responding resulted in a 30-sec TO from reinforced appetitive responding. In Exp II, the contingent use of TO punishment was compared with its random, or noncontingent use. The results of both experiments showed that in the absence of punishment, avoidance extinction was characterized by short latencies and nearly 100% avoidance responding. Avoidance responding in extinction was little affected by noncontingent TO punishment. When TO was made contingent upon avoidance responding, however, avoidance latencies immediately increased and the frequency of avoidance responses subsequently decreased to zero.

title: A Method to Encourage Extensive Study of Animal Hypnotic Behavior
process: Respondent Conditioning, Stimulus Control of Induction, Habituation
abstract: Since its first description in 1646, the phenomenon commonly termed “animal hypnosis” (also known as tonic immobility, catalepsy, entrancement, and other labels) has been discussed widely but lacks a comprehensive behavioral description to accompany known electrophysiological findings. Interpretation of animal hypnosis has often been confounded by comparisons to human psychiatric states such as catatonia, despite insufficient empirical parallels. Rigorous study has been limited by the brief and unstable nature of the trance: many species, including rabbits and guinea pigs, spontaneously recover within seconds to a few minutes, and even minimal sensory stimulation (e.g., touch or sound) terminates the state prematurely. Attempts to increase trance duration through daily repeated inductions have required extensive training periods, while tranquilizing drugs enhance both duration and depth but introduce interpretive complications. Electrical stimulation across the head or subcortical structures has also been shown to prolong and deepen the trance. A coherent behavioral account requires systematic analysis of observable behavior, environmental conditions, and controlling variables to clarify the mechanisms underlying induction, maintenance, and termination of tonic immobility.

title: Discrimination and Emission of Temporal Intervals by Pigeons
process: Interresponse Time, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval, Operant Conditioning, Reinforcement: Positive
abstract: Because the frequency distribution of IRTs showed little or no control by a DRL schedule, the schedule was modified so that the pigeon's behavior after each IRT would indicate whether or not it had discriminated the duration of the IRT. After every two pecks on a red key, the key changed to blue for 30 sec. Then it automatically became red again. Pecks on the blue key were reinforced with food on a VI schedule only when the preceding IRT on the red key had been longer than 18 sec. The birds did not selectively emit longer IRTs on the red key: the value of IRTs/op did not increase with IRT duration. However, they did discriminate the duration of the IRT emitted on the red key: the rate of pecking on the blue key was an increasing function of the duration of the preceding IRT on the red key.

title: Operant Conditioning of a Small Scale Muscle Response
process: Reinforcement: Positive, Respondent Conditioning, Operant Conditioning
abstract: Six naive male subjects were reinforced for responses in the 20–30 microvolt range, and two each for those in 10–20 and 30–40 microvolt ranges. Records were taken of 15 min of “settling down,” 15 min of initial operant level responding, 30 min of conditioning, and 45 min of extinction, 30 min with light present and 15 min without. The results were: (1) small-scale muscle potentials from the thenar eminence, in the 10–20, 20–30, and 30–40 microvolt ranges, were conditioned, using pointer movement as the reinforcement; (2) the response rate in adjacent ranges of greater and lesser amplitude also increased during such conditioning and decreased during the subsequent extinction; (3) during conditioning the frequency of response distribution shifted toward the reinforced range; (4) subjects were unaware of the reinforced response; (5) observing a noncontingent moving pointer increased the response rate in the 20–30 microvolt range, but did not lead to conditioning.

title: Probability of Response and Probability of Reinforcement in a Response-Defined Analogue of an Interval Schedule
process: Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval, Differential Reinforcement, Interresponse Time, Behavior Chain, Reinforcement: Positive, Operant Conditioning
abstract: Variable interval (VI) responding was hypothesized to be a function of differential reinforcement susceptibilities of various unspecified behavior chains that mediate interresponse times (IRTs). To test this hypothesis, probabilities of reinforcement were regulated for the lengths of chains of key pecking responses of pigeons, analogous to the way that VI regulates probabilities of reinforcement for IRTs. This procedure generated a number of VI-like effects, supporting the notion that VI behavior can be construed as a special case of an interaction between the organism's function relating reinforcement susceptibilities to chain length and the experimenter's function relating probabilities of reinforcement to chain length.

title: Operant Conditioning in the Newly Hatched Chicken
process: Opernat Coniditoning, Reinforcement:Positive, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Interval, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Ratio, Shaping
abstract: Techniques are described for conditioning key-pecking reinforced with food and for recording cheeping in newly hatched chickens. A mirror in the test box is essential when conditioning isolated chickens up to five or more days old. Conditioning proceeds more rapidly when frequently pecked objects and materials that move when scratched are not present. Stimulus control over key-pecking is present in the three-day-old chicken and multiple fixed-ratio, fixed-interval schedule control develops in succeeding days. In young chickens, pecking and cheeping are inversely related. The newly hatched chicken is useful for pharmacological studies and appears to offer other advantages for behavioral studies.

title: Fixed Ratio and Extinction Performance of Infans in the Second Year of Life
process: Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Ratio, Reinforcement: Positive, Operant Conditioning, Post-Reinforcement Pause
abstract: Five 14½- to 19½-month-old infants were trained to lever press for snacks on small fixed ratio schedules of reinforcement. Within four to nine sessions, responding under FR 10 was established for four subjects and FR 15 for the other. Each subject's last session revealed behavioral patterns similar to animal and human FR trained subjects—a high and constant ratio rate, mixed with a zero rate following reinforcements. Deviations were mostly in the form of prolonged and variable post-reinforcement pauses. These and other irregularities were probably due to the limited deprivation conditions and improper training procedures in which the ratio (for two subjects) was ascended too early and too quickly. Extinction was instituted during the last session. The degree to which extinction performance matched that of other organisms depended upon how stable and “ratio-like” performance was during conditioning.

title: Analysis of Water and NaCL Solution Acceptance by Schedule-Induced Polydipsia
process: Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval, Reinforcement: Positive, Operant Conditioning,
abstract: Animals were trained on a VI 1-min schedule for food pellets, and concurrent water intake was measured. The polydipsia induced was analyzed in terms of the frequency distribution of post-pellet licking burst sizes and the trend of polydipsia throughout the session. An ascending series of NaCl solutions was presented consecutively over daily sessions and a typical NaCl acceptance-rejection intake function was generated. Beginning in the 0.9-1.2% NaCl range, the animals drank less often during the session but took larger drinks when they did drink. Neither the frequency of drinks nor the mean licking burst size were simply related to the volumes of NaCl solution consumed. The NaCl acceptance-rejection function cannot be explained in terms of water repletion factors alone.

title: A Flexible Apparatus for Presenting Pairs of Visual Stimuli to Children
process: Measurement: Duration, Operant Conditioning, Stimulus Control, Choice Behavior
abstract: Research on preferential choice behavior toward visual stimuli often relies on measuring the duration of observing responses, yet obtaining reliable measures is challenging—particularly with children. Traditional methods, such as allowing subjects to advance slides at will (Berlyne & Lewis, 1963), provide little assurance of actual stimulus fixation. Prior attempts to increase control have included requiring repeated button presses to maintain stimulus presentation (Smock & Holt, 1962), working in darkness to restrict visual orientation (Holland, 1957, 1958), or using head-mounted illumination devices (Blair, 1958). Although partially effective, these approaches are often impractical or unsuitable for young children. More recent techniques, such as Cantor, Cantor, and Ditrichs’s (1963) forehead-activated light boxes, offer improved control but impose substantial physical demands that limit rapid stimulus presentation. The present paper describes a novel apparatus designed specifically to facilitate controlled presentation of paired visual stimuli to children while ensuring active, stimulus-directed responding. Constructed from soft pine and plywood and integrated with a dual-slide projector, the apparatus enables children to select and display either of two slides by performing a simple, orientation-dependent response. This design provides improved control over observing behavior without requiring darkness or excessive bodily movement, thereby offering a practical method for studying visual preference in young populations.

title: Operant Behavior and the Galvanic Skin Potential under DRL Schedule
authors: Meurice, Emile, Weiner, Harold, Sloboda, Walter
process: Galvanic Skin Potential, Operant Reconditioning, Operant Conditioning, Schedules of Reinforcement:DRL, Extinction
abstract: Motor and galvanic skin potential (GSP) activity were investigated during the conditioning, extinction, and reconditioning of motor responses under a differential reinforcement of low rate (DRL) schedule of reinforcement. Interresponse time (IRT) distributions for motor responses during conditioning and reconditioning gradually stabilized at a peak just beyond the minimal IRT required for reinforcement. Few unreinforced motor responses and “bursts” of motor responses were observed during conditioning and reconditioning. Relative to conditioning and reconditioning, extinction effected larger IRTs and smaller GSP amplitudes. GSP amplitudes were greater for unreinforced than for reinforced motor responses during conditioning and reconditioning. However, GSP amplitudes associated with the unreinforced extinction responses were smaller than either the reinforced or unreinforced responses during conditioning and reconditioning.

title: The Post-Reinforcement Pause
process: Post-Reinforcement Pause, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Rate, Reinforcement: Positive
abstract: Measurements of the post-reinforcement pause and response rate were obtained from four birds on a range of fixed ratio schedules from 25 to 150. The results indicated a consistent increase in the length of the pause as the ratio was increased. Response rate tended to decrease, but these data were less consistent and some reversals were apparent.

title: Auditory Reaction Time and the Depreivation of Equal Loudness Contours for the Monkey
process: Measuresment: Latency of Response, Operant Conditioning, Reinforcement: Positive
abstract: Monkeys were trained to release a telegraph key at the onset of a pure tone. Latency of the response was measured over a 70-db range of sound pressure (re 0.0002 dyn/cm2) at six frequencies (250 to 15,000 cps). Latency was found to be an inverse exponential function of intensity at all frequencies. Equal loudness was inferred from the equal latency contours which were constructed from the latency-intensity functions at each frequency. These data indicate peak auditory sensitivity for the monkey near 1000 cps. At the frequencies above and below 1000 cps consistently more sound energy was required for equal latency.

title: A Device for the Partial Restraint of Rats in Operant Conditioning Studies
authors: Hall, Robert D., Clayton, Richard J., Mark, Roger G.
process: Operant Conditioning, Stimulus Control, Device: Restraint
abstract: In research on operant behavior, it is often necessary to restrict an animal’s movement to prevent interference with the acquisition or interpretation of physiological measures. Although many rat restraining devices have been described, relatively few are suitable for operant studies. The apparatus presented here, used consistently for over three years, provides a lever-pressing environment in which only the rat’s head and one foreleg retain substantial freedom of movement. Its simple, rigid construction allows for easy placement of animals, supports prolonged restraint, and offers excellent control over operant responding. Additionally, because most of the animal’s body—particularly the head—remains accessible, the device is well suited for experiments involving brain electrical activity, intracranial stimulation, or chemical and electrical interventions requiring unobstructed access.

title: A Suspended Platform for use in Chronic Restraints of Monkeys
authors: Thompson, Travis, Seal, Donald, Bloom, William
abstract: Extended restraint of rhesus monkeys in standard commercially available primate chairs often results in significant physical complications, including neck and mandibular lesions as well as deterioration of ischial callosities. Earlier work by Findley (1959) demonstrated that an intermediate restraining device, which allows greater postural freedom and shifts body support to the feet, can reduce these adverse effects, though it is unsuitable for chronic procedures that require more rigid restraint. The present arrangement modifies the conventional primate chair by replacing the typical two-bar seat with a suspended 9 × 9 in. brass-rod platform supported by adjustable steel sash chains. This configuration provides sufficient restraint for chronic experimental needs while enabling the animal to redistribute pressure across its body, thus preventing the development of ischial callosity breakdown and neck or mandibular lesions. The design offers an effective compromise between necessary immobilization and the animal’s ability to engage in limited postural adjustments that protect vulnerable body regions.

title: The Effect of Multiple S-Delts Periods on Responding on a Fixed-Interval Schedule: IV. Effect of Continuous S-Delta with only Short Sd Probes
process: Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed-Ratio, Disrimination Training, Reinforcement: Postiive, Operant Conditioning, Time-out
abstract: Pigeons were studied under FI 500 sec in which an SΔ was present throughout the interval except during the terminal 50-sec segment and one earlier 50-sec segment. Very little responding occurred during the presence of SΔ. The rate of responding in the earlier 50-sec SD segments was lower than in the terminal SD segment. There was a clear trend for the rate of responding in the earlier SD segment to be progressively higher the later it occurred in the course of the FI 500 sec. This trend was shown roughly to parallel the increasing rate of responding in a conventional FI 500 sec with no interruption by SΔ. Since the changing tendency to respond through the FI survives massive disruption by SΔ, it is concluded that the control of responding through the FI does not require continuous mediating behavior. It is suggested that it is the decaying retroactive influence of the reinforcer on responses that occurred longer and longer before the reinforcer occurred which produces the familiar scalloped pattern of responding under FI schedules.

title: A Simple Method of Obtaining a Scatter Distribution of Off-Key Pigeon Pecking
abstract: There are occasions, such as in shaping and in preference studies (with two keys and a space between them), when it may be of value to have a scatter distribution of the location of pigeon pecks not on the key. The key pecks will be recorded automatically, but off-key pecks will not. A simple and inexpensive method for recording such a scatter distribution is to use a Ditto master (such as a 'Dittmark' master) through which a hole is cut for the key. The Ditto master, cut down, is taped to the key wall of the experimental box and the hole for the key is strengthened from the rear with Scotch tape to minimize tearing from pecks. Each peck, in a fashion similar to a typed mark, leaves an impression on the Ditto master and can be recorded for later analysis.

title: A Further Improvement in the Automatic Dispensing of Live Food for Fish
authors: Haralson, John V., Ralph, George S.
abstract: Previous studies have shown limitations in live food dispensing systems for experimental reinforcement with small worms. The device developed by Hogan and Rozin (1961), later evaluated by Longo and Bitterman (1963), was found to deliver food inconsistently, while the Longo–Bitterman modification introduced additional reliability issues stemming from the use of medicine droppers. These droppers allow worms to escape delivery when surface tension between the upper and lower menisci is insufficient to contain their movement, or when improper air gaps permit worms to gain traction on the dropper tip. Furthermore, operational inefficiency results from the need to manually place and remove numerous individual droppers during experimentation. Based on several years of practical experience with live worm reinforcement techniques, the present apparatus resolves these shortcomings by employing a mechanically controlled delivery system driven by a 1 cps Bodine motor. Activation of the motor advances a slotted disc mechanism that precisely regulates a single delivery cycle, terminating automatically via a braking circuit triggered by a microswitch. This design improves reliability, prevents worm escape, and streamlines the delivery process, offering significant advantages over earlier live food dispensing methods.

title: Two Types of Behavioral Contrast in Discrmination Learning
process: Behavioral Contrast, Discrimination Training, Extinction, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval, Punishment, Multiple Scheules of Reinforcement, Schedules of Reinforcement: Differential Reinforcement of Other Behavior
abstract: Two groups of pigeons received daily discrimination training at two values on a line-tilt continuum. S+ (VI 1) and S– (EXT) intervals alternated, and a 30-sec criterion of no responding to S– was required before S+ returned. Rates of responding to S+ showed two separate contrast effects: at an intermediate stage of training a high peak rate appeared which declined, later in training, to a stable level still in excess of the VI baseline rate. The peak rate was correlated with the total number of responses to S–, while the final rate was not; suggesting that the peak rate and final rate may not be functions of the same variable. These results were compared with performance on a red-green discrimination where the two stages were not so clear. A line-tilt discrimination was repeated with fixed length S– intervals terminated by TO, and showed the same contrast magnitude in the final rate without any peak. The peak rate was interpreted as an effect of the ‘punishment’ contingency where responding to S– prolongs S– for 30 sec, while the final rate was taken to be analogous to previous demonstrations of contrast.

title: A Computer-Oriented System for High-Speed Recording of Operant Behavior
authors: Barry, Herbert III, Kinnard, William J. Jr., Watzman, Nathan, Buckley, Joseph P.
process: Operant Conditioning, Measurement: Operant Behavior, Punch Programming
abstract: A method is described by which large quantities of data, generated at high and variable rates from a large number of test boxes, are recorded on a single eight-channel punched paper tape. The data, which include a record of the occurrence time of each event in 1/10-sec units are in a compact form, suitable for conversion to standard Hollerith punched card codes and for decoding and summarizing by a large digital computer. Experience with the system has demonstrated a high degree of accuracy and reliability, and low operating cost.

title: A Relay-Transistor Sequential Grid Scrambler
process: Aversive Control: Electric Shock, Device: Relay Transistor
abstract: Effective implementation of electric shock in behavioral experiments often requires rapid and reliable polarity changes across grid floors. Although numerous grid-scrambling devices have been developed, selecting an appropriate system depends on experimental demands, the number of chambers requiring scrambled shock, cost constraints, and available equipment that can be adapted. Early motor-driven scramblers, such as those designed by Skinner and Campbell (1947) and by Wycoff and Page (1954), varied shock patterns through mechanical switching but suffered from limited switch lifespan and the financial burden of supplying separate scramblers for multiple chambers. More recent proposals, including mercury-switch scramblers (Owen & Kellermeier, in press), offer improved durability and noise reduction but remain unsuitable for procedures requiring shock durations under one second. The present work addresses these limitations by emphasizing the need for a shock-scrambling approach that supports rapid polarity changes, accommodates diverse experimental protocols, and increases system longevity while reducing operational costs.

title: Behavior Control by an Imprinted Stimulus
authors: Hoffman, Howard S., Searle, John L., Toffey, Sharon, Kozma, Frederick Jr.
process: Imprinting Procedures, Operant Conditioning, Extinction, Temporal Relations, Respondent Conditioning
abstract: Newly hatched ducklings were exposed to imprinting procedures and subsequently trained to peck a key by presenting the imprinting stimulus as the reinforcing (response contingent) event. It was found that the key peck was learned only when imprinting procedures were initiated during the first 6 to 8 hr after hatch. Additional studies revealed that: (1) the duckling's distress vocalizations were reduced in the presence of the imprinting stimulus and enhanced in its absence; (2) when the ducklings had constant access to the imprinted stimulus (via a key peck), pecking responses occurred in bursts and relatively few distress vocalizations occurred; (3) the initial effect of extinction procedures was an increase in key peck rate. When, however, repeated key pecks failed to produce the imprinted stimulus, distress vocalization ensued and peck rate declined; (4) both the presentation of an unfamiliar mechanical figure and delivery of electrical shock enhanced distress vocalization and key pecks; (5) for some ducklings, certain familiar objects in the environment influenced distress calls in a manner comparable to the imprinted stimulus in that distress calls increased when these objects were removed.

title: Extinction-Induced Aggression
authors: Azrin, N.H., Hutchinson, R.R., Hake, D.F.
process: Extinction, Aggression, Extinction-Induced Regression, Reinforcement: Positive, Unconditioned Aversive Stimuli, Schedules of Reinforcement: Continous Reinforcement, Signaled Extinction, Unsignaled Extinction
abstract: Pigeons were conditioned to peck a response key under a procedure that alternated periods of food reinforcement with periods of extinction. The pigeons attacked a nearby pigeon at the onset of extinction. Some also attacked a stuffed model of a pigeon. The duration of attack was an inverse function of the time since the last food reinforcement and a direct function of the number of reinforcements. The pigeons attacked after the last food delivery whether or not the conditioned pecking response was required and whether or not the extinction period was signaled. The food had to be eaten; the mere sight and sound of food being delivered did not produce attack. Prior satiation reduced attack. The phenomenon was not attributable to a past history of competition between pigeons since socially deprived pigeons also attacked. Superstitious reinforcement of attack was not found to be a factor. The results indicated that the transition from food reinforcement to extinction was an aversive event that produced aggression.

title: Conditioned Acceleration and Conditioned Suppression in Pigeons
process: Punishment: Positive, Discrimination Training, Operant Conditioning, Conditioned Acceleration, Conditioned Suppression, Conditioned Supression, Reinforcement: Positive, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval
abstract: Two experiments were performed to investigate the effects on pigeons' keypecking behavior of stimuli that signal different kinds of aversive events: time-out from positive reinforcement, electric shock, loud noise, and loud tone. Behavior maintained by a variable-interval schedule of reinforcement was suppressed by a stimulus before shock, was accelerated by a stimulus before time-out from positive reinforcement, and was unchanged by a stimulus before loud noise or a stimulus before loud tone. Conditioned acceleration with time-out from positive reinforcement and conditioned suppression with shock were obtained regardless of whether a response contingent or response-independent procedure was employed.

title: What is the Experimental Analysis of Behavior
abstract: The experimental analysis of behavior is the study of the behavior of animals in the laboratory. It is a branch of psychology that is concerned with the study of the behavior of animals in the laboratory. It is a branch of psychology that is concerned with the study of the behavior of animals in the laboratory.

title: Discrimination Learning as Function of Stimulus Location Along an Auditory Intensity Continuum
process: Discrimination Learning, Stimulus Location, Auditory Intensity Continuum, Reinforcement: Positive, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Ratio, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval
static-equation-definitions: DI: discrimination index, R^{SD}: response rate for the SD stimulus, R^{SDelta}: response rate for the SDelta stimulus
abstract: Eight groups of rats were trained on an auditory intensity discrimination in which the discriminative stimuli were separated by 10 decibels (db). Four pairs of stimuli were selected from different regions along a 60–100 db (SPL) intensity continuum. Counterpart groups were trained on each stimulus pair, with the relative intensity positions of the reinforced stimulus (SD) and the non-reinforced stimulus (SΔ) reversed for the two groups. Discrimination acquisition curves were compared to determine whether stimuli separated by equal logarithmic units were of comparable 'difficulty', and to determine the relative effectiveness of an SD serving as the more versus less intense member of a stimulus pair. It was concluded that: (1) When SD is the more intense, auditory intensities of constant logarithmic separation are graded in 'difficulty' along the intensity continuum; high intensity discriminative stimuli are most readily discriminated. When SΔ is the more intense, this graded effect is not evident. (2) For a given continuum location, discrimination is inferior when SΔ is the more intense. This effect is most pronounced at the high intensity end of the continuum and is chiefly attributable to differences in the rate of SΔ responding.

title: Concurrent Fixed-Ratio and Avoidance Responding in the Squirrel Monkey
authors: Catania, Charles A., Deegan, John F., Cook, Leonard
abstract: Squirrel monkeys maintained concurrent performances appropriate to a fixed-ratio schedule of food reinforcement on one lever and an avoidance schedule on a second lever. The overall rate of responding maintained by either schedule was not systematically affected when the other schedule was discontinued and its lever removed.

title: An Automatic Method for the Study of Aggression in Squirrel Monkeys
process: Aggression, Experimental Analysis of Behavior, Animal Behavior, Squirrel Monkey, Biting Behavior, Electric Shock, Reinforcement: Positive, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Ratio, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval
abstract: Research on aggression in infrahuman species has progressed from naturalistic observation to controlled laboratory analysis, with increasing emphasis on experimentally elicited aggression. Despite these advances, the objective definition and measurement of aggressive behavior have remained problematic. Earlier methods relied on visual observation or broad rating scales, which were time consuming, imprecise, and vulnerable to observer bias. Subsequent laboratory techniques demonstrated that electric shock could reliably elicit aggressive responses toward inanimate objects, permitting the study of aggression in a restricted, nonsocial environment. However, existing recording methods lacked reliability and sensitivity, often registering responses that did not correspond to actual biting behavior and failing to measure key dimensions such as frequency, duration, and intensity. The present paper describes the development of an improved apparatus for the direct measurement of biting attack in squirrel monkeys. Using a pneumatic bite sensor and transducer system integrated into a restraining chair arrangement, this method provides reliable, continuous, and objective recording of aggressive responses, allowing precise quantification of the frequency, duration, and intensity of elicited biting behavior.

title: A Comparison of Different Measures of Response Strength in the Study of Stimulus Generalization
authors: Thomas, David R., Konick, Dorothy S.
process: Variable Interval Training, Stimulus Generalization, Extinction, Response Rate, Response Strength, Response Rate Gradient, Response Rate Gradient Correction
abstract: In Experiment 1, three pigeons were given variable interval training to peck at a light of 550 mμ and then were tested for stimulus generalization in extinction to several different wavelengths. A gradient was obtained for latency of the first response in each test period, for the number of test periods in which responding occurred, and for the measure of response rate. When the response rate gradient was corrected for differences in initial latency and in number of responded trials, the change was minimal, indicating that the major component of response rate as usually measured is rate of responding having once responded. In Experiment 2, three other pigeons were trained to respond to 550 mμ (for variable interval reinforcement) and not to 570 mμ (extinguished). Analysis of generalization gradients dictated the same conclusion as that reported for generalization following single stimulus training.

title: A Suggestion Concerning the Anesthetization of the Rat
abstract: Pentobarbital sodium and chloral hydrate have been widely used as anesthetic agents for small animals, but each presents significant limitations when administered alone. Pentobarbital sodium often fails to maintain adequate surgical anesthesia in rats for extended periods without repeated dosing, while chloral hydrate carries substantial risk due to pronounced and sustained respiratory depression. Although combined administration of these agents has been shown to reduce mortality, complications such as pulmonary fluid accumulation may still occur. This paper reports the use of chlorprothixene as a pre-anesthetic in combination with pentobarbital sodium, a procedure that markedly improves the stability and safety of anesthesia. The combined regimen minimizes respiratory complications and provides reliable, prolonged surgical anesthesia, thereby reducing anesthetic risk and improving experimental outcomes in small-animal surgery.

title: Conditioned Suppression and Variable Ratio Reinforcement
process: Conditioned Suppression, Response Rate, Deprivation,Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable-Ratio, Extinction, Operant Conditioning, Cumulative Recording
abstract: Conditioned suppression is a decrease in response rate during a relatively short duration stimulus that terminates independently of the animal's behavior and coincidentally with a brief unavoidable shock. The degree of conditioned suppression was measured for each of three birds on three variable ratio schedules; that is, the number of responses required for food reinforcement was varied around a mean of 50, 100, or 200. The results indicated a slight and possibly negligible decrease in the degree of suppression as the mean number of responses required on the schedule was increased from 50, to 100, and 200. In general, it was found that all of the variable ratio schedules tested were quite insensitive to the conditioned suppression procedure, although almost complete suppression was obtained on a few occasions. Since the reinforcement was contingent upon the emission of responses, the birds typically displayed a high rate of response during the pre-shock stimulus on all schedules. In addition, the rate during the pre-shock stimulus often changed abruptly independent of the presentation of a reinforcement. As a result of the high rate of response and the abrupt changes in rate, the degree of suppression from trial to trial was quite variable. A clear analysis of an experimental variable on this baseline is thus difficult.

title: Automatic Stimulus Attenuator for Rapid Discrimination Training
authors: Luschei, Erich S., Saslow, Carol A.
abstract: Efficient training of animals to perform complex discriminations can be substantially improved by introducing task difficulty gradually rather than presenting the final problem immediately. Previous research has demonstrated that shaping performance through successive approximations reduces errors and enhances later accuracy across species and tasks. The present note describes a simple, reliable, and inexpensive device designed to automate this process by adjusting stimulus parameters contingent upon the animal’s responses. The apparatus employs a bidirectional, gear-reduced motor that drives ganged rheostats under relay control, allowing systematic increases in task difficulty following correct responses and decreases following errors. The rheostats may be incorporated into a variety of circuits to modify stimulus intensity, temporal delays, or other experimental variables. This device provides a flexible means of implementing error-reducing training procedures and facilitates the efficient acquisition of complex discriminations.

title: Recovery of Responses during Mild Punishment
process: Punishment: Positive, Punishment: Electric Shock, Schedules of Reinforcement: Multiple Schedule, Extinction, Reinforcement: Positive, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Interval, Extinction with Shock, Extinction without Shock, Operant Conditioning, Discrimination Training
abstract: Pigeons were punished with mild shock for pecking during one of two components of a multiple schedule. They eventually recovered so that they pecked at the same rate during both components. In one experiment they were extinguished after recovering. When punishment, was maintained during extinction, they extinguished faster during the punished, than during the unpunished component. When punishment was stopped during extinction, they extinguished faster during the unpunished than during the previously punished component. In another experiment, punishment was programmed first during neither of the two components, then during one, then during both, and finally during the other component. The extent of recovery decreased with each successive cycle. It is concluded that, if transient emotional states are ignored, reward and punishment are symmetrical in their effects.

title: An Electromechanical Drinkometer
abstract: A simple, low-cost drinkometer is described that can be constructed with minimal tools and materials while providing reliable performance for behavioral research. The device operates without passing electrical current through the animal, ensuring experimental safety. It has been successfully employed in a large-scale study requiring the simultaneous operation of 24 drinkometers, a context in which alternative devices proved prohibitively expensive. Performance validation was conducted through direct comparison with an electronic drinkometer connected to the same drinking tube, with both devices registering identical lick counts. The apparatus consists of a lightweight conductive leaf pivoted at one end and fitted with a small aperture at the other; commercially available relay leaves with contacts were found to be suitable. The leaf is electrically grounded through its pivot, enabling accurate detection of drinking behavior

title: A Sensitive Electronic Drinkometer
process: Reinforcement: Positive , Measurement: Rate of Drinking
abstract: This paper describes a device of simple de-sign which allows rates of drinking in animalsto be measured while passing the minimum ofcurrent through the subject. The drinkometeremploys a gas filled tetrode (2D21) and astandard relay.

title: Schedules using Noxious Stimuli L Multiple Fixed-Ratio and Fixed-Interval Termination of Schedule Complexes
process: Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed-Interval, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed-Ratio, Schedules of Reinforcement: Multiple Schedules, Punishment: Electric Shocks, Schedule-Complex Termination, Shock-Schedule
abstract: The termination of a schedule complex, comprising a stimulus in the presence of which brief presentations of electric shocks are scheduled, is a reinforcer. Conditions were studied under which schedule-controlled patterns of responding characteristic of fixed-interval, fixed-ratio, and multiple fixed-interval fixed-ratio schedules can be maintained in the squirrel monkey by terminating a schedule complex. The schedule of shock presentation was a critical determinant of the patterns of responding, especially under fixed-interval schedules of termination. The rates and patterns of responding under various schedules of termination of schedule complexes were generally akin to those maintained under comparable schedules of food presentation. The findings suggest a general similarity in the dynamic aspects of performances under schedules of schedule-complex termination and comparable schedules of food presentation. The schedule of reinforcement is more important than the nature of the reinforcer in the control of behavior.

title: Micro-Minuature Connectors used as Permanent Electrode Holders in the Rat
abstract: The main requisites for a technique of per-manent electrode implantation are: (1) easy ac-cess for connecting the animal to the stimula-tion or recording apparatus, (2) permanence,(3) electrically good contacts, (4) minimal dis-comfort to the animal, and (5) preferably, theuse of commercially available materials. Con-nectors have met these demands in cats(Bradley and Elkes 1953) and monkeys(Nicholson 1964) and also fulfil these cri-teria in rats. The method of implantation isdescribed here.

title: Discriminative and Reinforcing Properties of Two Types of Food Pellets
authors: Cruse, Daniel B., Vitulli, William, Dertke,Max
process: Discrimination Learning, Discrimination Training, Schedules of Reinforcement: Multiple, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed-Ratio, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Interval, Reinforcement: Positive, Priming Stimulus
abstract: In Experiment I some discriminative functions of food pellets were studied by developing a multiple schedule of reinforcement (mult FR 30 FI 3) in which the delivery of a standard laboratory food pellet as a reinforcer set the occasion for reinforcement on every 30th response (FR 30), and the delivery of a sucrose food pellet as a reinforcer set the occasion for reinforcement after a 3-min interval (FI 3). Discriminative stimulus control by the type of pellet was also demonstrated by reversing the operant discrimination and having the standard pellet control the FI 3 and the sucrose pellet control the FR 30. In Experiment II a mult FR 30 FR 30 with two bars was developed; a standard food pellet was followed by an FR 30 on Bar 1 and extinction (ext) on Bar 2, while a sucrose pellet was followed by an FR 30 on Bar 2 and ext on Bar 1. A control rat was placed, for comparison, on a mixed (mix) FR 30 FR 30 schedule with two bars, but neither bar correlated with the type of food pellet. In Experiments I and II the similarity between pellet controlled multiple schedules and multiple primed schedules was discussed, as was the comparability of transitions and effectiveness of control between pellet controlled multiple schedules and multiple schedules providing continuous exteroceptive stimuli.

title: An Analysis of Contrast Effects in Multiple Schedules
authors: Nevin, John A., Shettleworth, Sara J.
process: Behavioral Contrast, Schedules of Reinforcement: Multiple Schedules, Sucessive Schedule Cycles, Extinction, Contrast Effects, Operant Contrasts, Schedules of Reinforcement:Fixed-Interval, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable-Interval, Time-Out, Positive Induction
abstract: Some phenomena of behavioral contrast in multiple schedules are reviewed, and three accounts of contrast are considered. Rate changes within a constant schedule component (transient contrasts) are distinguished from rate changes across successive schedule cycles (sustained contrasts). Pigeons were exposed to a three-component multiple schedule, in which a stimulus correlated with a constant variable interval schedule of reinforcement was preceded by a stimulus correlated with more frequent variable interval reinforcement, or by an extinction stimulus. If the preceding stimulus was correlated with more frequent reinforcement, the response rate in the constant component was low and increased with time. If the preceding stimulus was correlated with extinction, the rate in the constant component was high and decreased with time. Similar transient contrasts were observed in a two-component multiple schedule with different variable interval schedules in the two components. The transient contrast effects in the three-component schedule were shown to depend on differential reinforcement frequency rather than differential response rate in the preceding component. Such transient contrasts were not sufficient to account for sustained contrast effects observed in these experiments. The relation of these findings to the concepts of excitation and inhibition is discussed.

title: Concurrent Schedules of Interresponse Time Reinforcement: Probability of Reinforcement and the Lower Bounds of the Reinforced Interresponse Time Intervals
authors: Malott, Richard W., Cumming, William W.
process: Interreponse Times, Operant Conditioning, Temporal Intervals, Reinforcement: Positive
abstract: Data were obtained with rats on the effects of interresponse time contingent reinforcement of the lever press response using schedules in which interresponse times falling within either of two temporal intervals could be reinforced. Some of the findings were (a) the mode of the interresponse time distribution generally occurred near the first lower bound when the maximum reinforcement rate for the two lower bounds was equal; this also frequently occurred even when the reinforcement rate was less for the first lower bound; (b) as is the case with schedules using a single interval of reinforced interresponse times the values of the lower bounds partially determined the location and spread of the distributions; but the particular pair of values used did not seem to influence the effects of the probabilities of reinforcement; (c) although the modal interresponse time was usually at the lower bound of one of the two intervals of reinforced interresponse times, no simple relation existed between either the probability or rate of reinforcement of interresponse times in these two intervals and the location of this mode.

title: Multiple Punishment Schedule
process: Concurrent Schedules of Punishment, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed Ratio, Operant Conditioning, Continuous Punishment, Multi-element Baseline, Discrimination Training
abstract: Concurrent schedules of punishment and fixed-ratio reinforcement were studied in six white Carneaux pigeons. Two stimuli were alternated on the translucent key which served as the response mechanism. The pigeons were exposed to continuous punishment in one stimulus and first response punishment in the other. It was found that continuous punishment suppressed the fixed-ratio performance more than did punishment of the first response. Typically, a ratio run of responses was completed in spite of the onset of continuous punishment. Responding was then reduced for the remainder of the continuous punishment stimulus, except for those times when responding was initiated near the end of the stimulus. The results indicate that a multiple schedule can be used to compare different schedules of punishment in the same organism.

title: A Device for Rapid Presentation of Monochromatic Stimuli
abstract: A simple device constructed to insert 2-in.square interference filters into a light path hasbeen used for several months. A section of theunit is shown in the left of Fig. 1. Within thefilter box, several frame filter holders aremounted so that each may slide vertically inindividual grooves.

title: Observing Behavior during Interval Schedules
process: Chained Interval, Schedules of Reinforcement: Continuous Reinforcement, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed-Interval, Operant Conditioning, Schedules of Reinforcemetn: Variable Interval, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Ratio , Discrimination Training, Schedules of Reinforcement: Concurrent
abstract: Experiment 1 showed that the three stimuli associated with three chained fixed-interval links could be used to maintain observing behavior. Experiment 2 showed that three stimuli correlated with the passage of time since the last reinforcement in a fixed-interval schedule could be used to maintain observing behavior. In both experiments most observing responses occurred midway between reinforcements. Few occurred just before or just after reinforcement. Experiment 3 showed that the decline in the rate of observing behavior just before reinforcement was reduced when more stimuli could be observed. The relatively high terminal rate of observing behavior that resulted was maintained even when at least 4 sec intervened between the reinforcement and the last observed stimulus.

title: A Method for Automatic Programming of Spectral Stimuli using a Monochromator
The Stability and Control of Conditioned Noise Aversion in the Tilt Cage
process: Noise Contingencies, Negative Reinforcement, Multiple-Reversal Method, Aversive Stimuli
abstract: Twenty-six rats were used to study the development of stable conditioned responding to terminate white noise (100 or 105 db) in a tilt cage apparatus. Preliminary work, with one end of the cage consistently associated with silence, demonstrated stability at a high level of cumulative noise-off time per session. Time spent at the noise-off cage end was unaffected by lay-off and showed adaptation to reversal of the cage end functions. Variability in reversal acquisition, insensitivity to stimulus intensity changes and persistence in remaining at one end of the cage in the absence of noise-termination contingencies indicated the need for more precise behavioral control. A schedule programming several reversals of the no-noise end at variable intervals within each session was developed. Performance on the latter schedule was characterized by stability of high noise-off times and also proved more sensitive than the preliminary method to stimulus intensity changes (55, 70, 100 db) as measured by both cumulated noise-off time and cage crossing rates. The ease of training combined with sensitivity to stimulus change and the lack of highly specific motor response requirements make this technique promising for use with animals potentially debilitated by drugs or surgical aftereffects.

title: Varying Temporal Placement of an Added Stimulus in a Fixed-Interval Schedule
process: Steady State Experiments, Temporal Relations, Parametric Conditions, Operant Conditioning, Schedules of Reinforcement: Fixed-Interval, Scheules of Reinforcment: Multiple, Extinction, Discrimination Training
abstract: One paradigm for exploring stimulus effects on behavior is defined for steady state experiments. The paradigm is illustrated by a 60-sec fixed-interval reinforcement schedule wherein a 6-sec light is introduced into each interval. The temporal relation of this stimulus to the reinforcer is the independent variable that is systematically explored. Two experiments studied this temporal relation under two parametric conditions: (a) when the 6-sec light occurs once in each 60-sec interval, (b) when the 6-sec light occurs twice in each interval, the second time always during the 6 sec immediately preceding the reinforcer. Functions are presented showing the effect of the 6-sec light on responding at all points in the fixed-interval.

title: The Role of Discrimination Training in the Generalization of Punishment
process: Discrimination Training, Punishment, Generalization Gradients, Schedules of Reinforcement: Variable Intercal, Aversive Stimuli: Electric Shock
abstract: Pigeons were trained to respond equally to various orientations of three parallel lines projected on a response key. One group was then punished for responding to the vertical lines, but not punished in a line-absent condition. Two other groups were also punished but had no opportunity to make such a discrimination. Orderly generalization gradients were obtained from the discrimination group during recovery from punishment, with least responding to the vertical lines and higher rates to other orientations. Gradients obtained from the non-discrimination groups were flat. A discrimination of punishment contingencies appears to be necessary for a stimulus correlated with punishment to acquire control over its reductive effects.

title: A Method for Chronic Intravenous Infusing in Freely Moving Rats
abstract: This report describes a system for infusingfluids either on a self administered or pro-grammed basis into the blood stream of freelymoving, unanesthetized rats. The method hasproved to be successful for studying barbitu-rate and morphine addiction in rats (Davisand Miller, 1963; Weeks, 1960), and can beused whether drugs are to be given periodi-cally or chronically 24 hr a day for weeks at atime. The system has been in use off and onfor about a year with rats being infused on a24 hr basis. The only major difficulty has beenthat occasionally rats will roll over on thelead-in tube. This may bend the tube or bringthe swivel joint inside the animal's cage whereit can be chewed. This can be prevented bylengthening the lead-in tube or lowering theceiling of the enclosure.

title: Some Effects of Methylphenidate on Stimulus Control of Ratio Avoidance Behavior in the Rat
authors: Stretch, Roger, Blackman, Derek, Alexander , Daphne
abstract: After exposure to an avoidance schedule which included a warning signal, a rat was placed on a multiple schedule in which the first component was the same as before, i.e., a single response reset the response-shock interval, delaying shock, and the second component differed only in that four bar-presses were required to postpone shock. A fixed ratio requirement of four responses (FR 4) generated behavior resembling a fixed ratio requirement of one response (FR 1) since responding was controlled by the warning signal but more shocks were received. At a dosage of 2 mg/kg, methylphenidate given intraperitoneally decreased shock frequency during FR 4 periods while FR 1 behavior was not affected; at 4 mg/kg, stimulus control of avoidance responding was impaired during both components. Results at 4 mg/kg were partially confirmed by two animals exposed to an FR 4 avoidance schedule which included a warning signal but with different parameters. Response distributions showed that methylphenidate increased response rates in the absence of the warning signal, i.e., stimulus control of ratio-avoidance behavior was impaired although the increased response rates reduced shock frequency.
