= \tfrac{1}{2} \log\!\left( \frac{P_w}{P_x} \cdot \frac{P_y}{P_z} \right)", "\log d = \tfrac{1}{2} \log\!\left( \frac{P_w}{P_x} \cdot \frac{P_z}{P_y} \right)
abstract:
Using an operant analogue of the yes-no detection task, six pigeons were trained to detect
luminance changes under two different reinforcement-scheduling procedures. In the first,
an uncontrolled reinforcement-ratio procedure, the relative frequency of food reinforcers
obtained for correct detections was free to vary with the birds' behavior as luminance
levels were changed. In the second, a controlled reinforcement-ratio procedure, changes in
preference could not alter the relative distribution of food reinforcers between the two
response alternatives. Extreme response biases developed as luminance was decreased to
threshold in the uncontrolled procedure. No progressive changes in response bias as a
function of decreasing luminance were seen in the controlled procedure. Absolute thresh-
olds for light intensity were lower in the controlled than in the uncontrolled procedure.

title: Signal Detection and Matching: Analyzing Choice on Concurrent Variable-Interval Schedules
abstract:
Pigeons' pecks on a red key and a green key were followed by access to grain according to
pairs of concurrent independent variable-interval schedules in a combined signal detection/
matching law paradigm. Pecks on the red key were reinforced by the richer variable-interval
schedule if a short-duration tone had been presented; pecks on the green key were rein-
forced by the richer variable-interval schedule if a long-duration tone had been presented.
Pecks on the green key given a short-duration tone, or on the red key given a long-duration
tone, were reinforced by the leaner variable-interval schedule. The data were analyzed ac-
cording to both signal detection's and the matching law's separate measures of, first, the
discrimination of the choices and, second, the bias to make one response or another. Increas-
ing the difficulty of the tone-duration discrimination decreased both methods' measures of
the discrimination of the choices and did not change both methods' measures of the bias
to make one response or another. Changing the leaner variable-interval schedule so that
it approached the richer variable-interval schedule decreased signal detection's measure of
discrimination but left its measure of response bias and the matching law measures un-
changed. Data collected only until a subject's first changeover response following presenta-
tion of a long or a short tone showed higher values for both methods' measures of discrimi-
nation, no change in signal detection's measure of response bias, and lower values for the
matching law's measure of response bias. Relationships between the matching law's and
signal detection's methods of analyzing choice are discussed. It is concluded that a signal
detection analysis is more efficient for examining changes in the difficulty of a discrimina-
tion, whereas a matching law analysis is more effective for examining the effects of changes
in relative reinforcer frequency.

title: Free-Operant Forgetting: Delayed Stimulus Control of Multiple-Schedule Performance
abstract:
Pigeons' responses were reinforced in two components of several multiple variable-interval
variable-interval schedules of food reinforcement. In one component, the key was illumi-
nated green for 15 seconds and white for 45 seconds. In the other component, the key was
illuminated red for 15 seconds and white for 45 seconds. Values for the exponent of the
power functions relating response ratios to reinforcement ratios were higher in the pres-
ence of the discriminative stimuli (green or red) than in the presence of white. Sensitivity
of response ratios to changes in reinforcement ratios provided an index of the extent to
which responding was under delayed stimulus control by prior discriminative stimuli

title: A DEVICE FOR MEASURING AND PROGRAMMINGTHE SPEED OF EXECUTION OF A SINGLE RESPONSE
abstract: his technical note describes an electrical circuit for measuring and controlling the speed or force of an operant response involving rotary motion. A small direct-current generator attached to a rotating lever or wheel produces an electrical output proportional to the subject’s response speed or mechanical energy. This signal biases a transistor–thyratron relay system that triggers when the generator output exceeds a preset criterion, thereby enabling the experimenter to program reinforcement or record responses based on movement velocity. Adjustable resistors permit calibration of response thresholds, while a smoothing capacitor stabilizes relay operation. The system offers a simple and inexpensive means to study quantitative response characteristics—such as vigor, force, or speed—in addition to response frequency, broadening the measurement of operant performance.

title: DIAGRAMMING SCHEDULES OF REINFORCEMENT
abstract: This brief methodological note introduces a graphical system for representing reinforcement schedules using cumulative-response coordinates, with number of responses on the y-axis and elapsed time on the x-axis. Skinner shows that each basic schedule—fixed ratio (FR), variable ratio (VR), fixed interval (FI), variable interval (VI), extinction, and continuous reinforcement—can be visualized as a line or region on this graph, where a response is reinforced when the cumulative-response curve contacts the line. The method extends naturally to more complex arrangements such as alternative, conjunctive, interlocking, tandem, chained, and multiple schedules, allowing their defining contingencies to be depicted as geometric relations between response number and time. Skinner suggests that such diagrams clarify relations among schedule types and may support future notational systems for escape, avoidance, or punishment schedules.

title: SHOCK INTENSITY IN VARIABLE-INTERVALESCAPE SCHEDULES
process: Aversive control via negative reinforcement (escape) on variable-interval schedules; stimulus-intensity–response functions
abstract: Rats pressed a lever to terminate electric shock on VI escape schedules (typical mean: 30 s shock on, 120 s shock off). Using ascending and descending intensity series (0–400 µA) within single sessions, response rate shifted immediately with each intensity change. Rates were roughly proportional to current and showed positive acceleration from ~50 to 400 µA, with a “break” below 50 µA. Between-session drifts led the authors to estimate full intensity functions within single sessions. With precautions to prevent unintended avoidance, stable orderly performances were obtained; animals could be conditioned de novo at 50 µA and maintained on VI 30 s.

title: SOME NOTES ON "BURSTS"IN FREE-OPERANT AVOIDANCE EXPERIMENTS
process: Free-operant avoidance and conditioned suppression bursts
abstract: Sidman analyzed the occurrence of rapid, closely spaced response “bursts” in free-operant avoidance procedures where each lever press postponed shock for a fixed interval. Data from rats and monkeys showed that bursts often followed shocks or responses that terminated warning stimuli, though they also appeared after near-miss pauses that would have produced shocks. Early in training, bursts alternated with long pauses, resembling ratio-like performance patterns. Sidman proposed that bursts arise from the abrupt relief following aversive stimulation or warning termination, possibly through temporary broadening of stimulus generalization gradients. These observations suggested that shock onset, offset, and even its avoidance can all evoke short-lived increases in response probability.

title: OPERANT STUTTERING:THE CONTROLOF STUTTERING BEHAVIOR THROUGHRESPONSE- CONTINGENT CONSEQUENCES
process: Operant control of stuttering behavior — differential effects of punishment and escape (negative reinforcement) contingencies on speech disfluencies.
abstract: Three male stutterers (ages 15, 22, and 37) read aloud while every stutter produced either (a) a 1-second aversive tone (response-contingent punishment) or (b) 5 seconds of relief from a continuous tone (response-contingent escape). Stuttering increased when escape was contingent on stuttering and decreased—often to complete suppression—when tone onset followed stuttering. After aversive contingencies ended, compensatory bursts of stuttering occurred. Reading rate was largely unaffected, indicating that stuttering could be independently controlled as an operant distinct from speech rate. The findings supported an operant model of stuttering, in which reinforcement and punishment contingencies systematically modulate response frequency.

title: COUNTING BY HUMANS ON A FIXED-RATIOSCHEDULES OF REINFORCEMENT
process: Human fixed-ratio performance and self-generated discriminative control
abstract: "Human subjects were asked to detect pointer deflections on a dial visible only when a key press produced a brief flash of light. Deflections were programmed on fixed-ratio (FR) schedules, and subjects’ light-flashing responses were recorded. One subject began counting the number of responses per ratio, producing an inverted-S pattern: high response rate post-reinforcement, a mid-ratio lull, and acceleration approaching reinforcement. When counting was explicitly instructed, similar positive acceleration near reinforcement was observed, confirming that verbal counting acted as a discriminative stimulus controlling response pacing. These findings show that human verbal behavior can serve as an internal “counter,” functionally analogous to external stimulus counters in animal FR performance."]

title: SOME EFFECTS OF NOISE ON HUMAN BEHAVIOR
process: Aversive control and discriminative control in humans — effects of response-contingent (escape/avoidance) versus noncontingent noise on operant behavior.
abstract: This study examined how intense noise affects human behavior under different contingencies. Participants performed fixed-interval and fixed-ratio observing tasks while noise was presented (a) noncontingently, (b) contingent on reinforcement, or (c) contingent on responses. Noncontingent noise produced only brief, transient disruptions, while response-contingent noise (as punishment or escape) reliably and stably modified performance—reducing, eliminating, or reorganizing response patterns. Discriminative use of noise (e.g., as a signal) also controlled responding independently of aversiveness. The results demonstrated that the behavioral impact of noise depends on its contingency relationship to responses or reinforcers, not on intensity alone, and that prior null findings likely stemmed from noncontingent stimulus presentations.

title: THE EFFECT OF METHAMPHETAMINE ON OPERANT LEVELAND A VOIDANCE BEHA VIOR
process: Aversive control / avoidance conditioning with pharmacological modulation (psychopharmacology of operant behavior)
abstract: Across seven experiments with rats, methamphetamine reliably increased avoidance responding on Sidman-type schedules once the avoidance contingency had begun to control behavior; the drug did not comparably elevate unconditioned (operant-level) lever pressing. Dose–response data showed large transient rate increases at moderate doses (≈1–3 mg/kg), with temporary response suppression or cessation at higher doses (≥3–4 mg/kg) and evidence of short-term tolerance. Timing of administration (after avoidance came under control) was critical to observing rate increases. Control procedures (unavoidable shocks + time-out) indicated post–time-out rate surges after overdoses were drug-related rather than shock-induced. Findings dissociate stimulant effects on avoidance performance from general activity and highlight schedule parameters and response topography effec"This study examined how intense noise affects human behavior under different contingencies. Participants performed fixed-interval and fixed-ratio observing tasks while noise was presented (a) noncontingently, (b) contingent on reinforcement, or (c) contingent on responses. Noncontingent noise produced only brief, transient disruptions, while response-contingent noise (as punishment or escape) reliably and stably modified performance—reducing, eliminating, or reorganizing response patterns. Discriminative use of noise (e.g., as a signal) also controlled responding independently of aversiveness. The results demonstrated that the behavioral impact of noise depends on its contingency relationship to responses or reinforcers, not on intensity alone, and that prior null findings likely stemmed from noncontingent stimulus presentations.

title: THE EFFECT OF DEPRIVATION AND FREQUENCYOF REINFORCEMENT ON VARIABLE-INTERVAL RESPONDING
process: Motivational control (food deprivation) interacting with operant performance under interval schedules (schedule control)
abstract: Nine male rats pressed a lever for 0.045-g pellets on VI 1-min, VI 2-min, or VI 3-min schedules while daily deprivation was manipulated by testing at seven times since feeding (1–23 h). Stable performance was first established, then response rates were measured across deprivation times. Both deprivation and reinforcement frequency increased rate, with a significant interaction: equal increases in deprivation produced larger rate increases on richer VI schedules. Across deprivation levels, rates under different VIs were approximately proportional (e.g., r(VI-1) ≈ 1.35·r(VI-2); r(VI-1) ≈ 2.43·r(VI-3)), suggesting a multiplicative relation between deprivation effects and schedule value.

title: SEQUENTIAL DEPENDENCIES OF THE LENGTHS OFCONSECUTIVE RESPONSE RUNS
process: Response patterning and sequential dependency under ratio-like contingencies; extinction-induced variability
abstract: Three water-deprived rats responded on two levers where a minimum of eight consecutive responses on lever A followed by one on lever B produced water. Successive run lengths (number of A presses per run) were analyzed for sequential dependencies. The length of a given run covaried positively with the length of the preceding run, indicating first-order sequential effects. Visual inspection of run-by-run records also suggested cyclic fluctuations in run lengths across time. During extinction, variability in run length increased sharply, consistent with prior findings on behavioral variability during nonreinforcement. The results show short-term dependencies and increased response variability when reinforcement ceases.

title: SUSTAINED PERFORMANCE DURING VERY LONGEXPERIMENTAL SESSIONS
process: multiple schedule with DRL, FI, FR components; induction and satiation effects
abstract: Two pigeons were maintained in continuous experimental sessions lasting up to 28 days under a multiple schedule: DRL 1-min (white key), FI 10-min (green), and FR 50 (red). The DRL component sustained low, steady response rates, while FI and FR components alternated hourly to maintain overall responding and body weight. Over days and weeks, pigeons exhibited stable lever-pecking patterns similar to those in conventional daily sessions. Performance decrements occurred only with satiation or minor loss of stimulus control. Induction among schedule components occasionally produced cross-schedule rate changes. The study demonstrated the feasibility of continuous, around-the-clock operant performance, providing a baseline for long-term behavioral and physiological research.

title: BEHAVIOR UNDER EXTENDED EXPOSURE TO A HIGH-VALUEFIXED INTERVAL REINFORCEMENT SCHEDULE
process: Fixed-interval performance and temporal patterning under extended exposure
abstract: Three White Carneaux pigeons were exposed to a fixed-interval (FI) 30-minute schedule for over 2300 total hours (up to 146 sixteen-hour sessions). Extended observation revealed a rich progression of fixed-interval patterns beyond early “scalloping.” With continued training, birds displayed multiple forms of double and triple scallops, sustained “break-and-run” patterns, negative acceleration before reinforcement, and occasional scallops occurring independently of reinforcement delivery. These phenomena extended and refined earlier analyses by Ferster and Skinner, demonstrating that long-term FI exposure produces increasingly complex temporal structures in responding. One subject died near the end of the study, with its final session suggesting physiological deterioration. The findings highlight behavioral stability and emergent temporal organization under extreme duration FI conditions.

title: BY-PRODUCTS OFA VERSIVE CONTROL
process: Aversive control and conditioned suppression/facilitation; adventitious chaining between concurrent operants
abstract: Sidman examined how aversive control spills over onto “irrelevant” behavior in rhesus monkeys responding on concurrent schedules. A food-maintained operant (Lever A or chain pull; typically VI 4-min, later FR) ran alongside a Sidman avoidance operant (Lever B; R–S = 20 s). A warning stimulus periodically preceded unavoidable (“free”) shock. Initially, the pre-shock stimulus facilitated both responses, contradicting simple conditioned suppression. Sequence analyses showed frequent A→B alternations, implying adventitious chaining whereby the food operant acquired avoidance functions. Replacing A with a chain and, critically, switching A to fixed ratio broke A→B chaining, yielding the canonical pattern: suppression of the food operant during the pre-shock stimulus while the avoidance operant was facilitated. Results highlight superstitious linkage across response classes as a by-product of aversive control.

title: FIXED-RA TIO SCHEDULES OF CONDITIONEDREINFORCEMENT WITH CHIMPANZEES1
process: ["Conditioned reinforcement; fixed-ratio performance (post-reinforcement pause & running rate); discriminative chaining across conditioned reinforcers"
abstract: Two 7-year-old chimpanzees earned poker chips (conditioned reinforcers) on fixed-ratio (FR) schedules by pressing a key under a white light; chips could be exchanged for food only when a red light appeared (session end). After FR 30 baselines, the ratio was raised (to FR 100; later FR 125), with sessions up to 8 h or 60 chips. Performance showed classic FR structure: post-reinforcement pauses and high, stable running rates that increased with ratio size. At high FR values, very long initial pauses dominated early session segments, yet once running began, rates were sustained (≈2–4 resp/s). Extinction produced bursts at prior running rates. Providing 50 chips at session start abolished the initial pause, supporting a chained-schedule/discriminative control interpretation of conditioned reinforcement."Sidman examined how aversive control spills over onto “irrelevant” behavior in rhesus monkeys responding on concurrent schedules. A food-maintained operant (Lever A or chain pull; typically VI 4-min, later FR) ran alongside a Sidman avoidance operant (Lever B; R–S = 20 s). A warning stimulus periodically preceded unavoidable (“free”) shock. Initially, the pre-shock stimulus facilitated both responses, contradicting simple conditioned suppression. Sequence analyses showed frequent A→B alternations, implying adventitious chaining whereby the food operant acquired avoidance functions. Replacing A with a chain and, critically, switching A to fixed ratio broke A→B chaining, yielding the canonical pattern: suppression of the food operant during the pre-shock stimulus while the avoidance operant was facilitated. Results highlight superstitious linkage across response classes as a by-product of aversive control.

title: INTERACTION AMONG COMPONENTS OF AMULTIPLE SCHEDULE
abstract: Two male albino rats worked on a 4-component multiple schedule cycling every 5 min: FI 5-min (food; limited hold 2 min), SΔ1 (extinction), Sidman free-operant avoidance (tone), and SΔ2 (clicker). Across phases the avoidance interval (S-S and R-S) was 20 s, 5 s, avoidance extinction, then 10 s. Shortening the avoidance interval increased avoidance responding and elevated response rates during both SΔ components—demonstrating cross-component interaction. FI rates were idiosyncratic (one decreased, one unchanged). An immediate shift to 5 s produced a transient loss of stimulus control with near-equal rates across components; with continued exposure, typical FI scallops re-emerged and SΔ responding showed temporal patterning (late in SΔ1, early in SΔ2). During avoidance extinction, SΔ responding quickly declined despite initially high avoidance rates."Two 7-year-old chimpanzees earned poker chips (conditioned reinforcers) on fixed-ratio (FR) schedules by pressing a key under a white light; chips could be exchanged for food only when a red light appeared (session end). After FR 30 baselines, the ratio was raised (to FR 100; later FR 125), with sessions up to 8 h or 60 chips. Performance showed classic FR structure: post-reinforcement pauses and high, stable running rates that increased with ratio size. At high FR values, very long initial pauses dominated early session segments, yet once running began, rates were sustained (≈2–4 resp/s). Extinction produced bursts at prior running rates. Providing 50 chips at session start abolished the initial pause, supporting a chained-schedule/discriminative control interpretation of conditioned reinforcement."Sidman examined how aversive control spills over onto “irrelevant” behavior in rhesus monkeys responding on concurrent schedules. A food-maintained operant (Lever A or chain pull; typically VI 4-min, later FR) ran alongside a Sidman avoidance operant (Lever B; R–S = 20 s). A warning stimulus periodically preceded unavoidable (“free”) shock. Initially, the pre-shock stimulus facilitated both responses, contradicting simple conditioned suppression. Sequence analyses showed frequent A→B alternations, implying adventitious chaining whereby the food operant acquired avoidance functions. Replacing A with a chain and, critically, switching A to fixed ratio broke A→B chaining, yielding the canonical pattern: suppression of the food operant during the pre-shock stimulus while the avoidance operant was facilitated. Results highlight superstitious linkage across response classes as a by-product of aversive control.

title: GENERALIZA TION GRADIENTFOR AUDITORY INTENSITY IN THE RAT
process: Stimulus generalization under operant discrimination (auditory intensity); gradient sharpening with discrimination; extinction generalization
abstract: Male albino rats lever-pressed under an auditory intensity discrimination using a 4-kHz tone. After establishing SD–SΔ differing by 40 dB, Phase II introduced four nonreinforced test intensities (SΔ and +/–10, +/–20, +/–30 dB toward SD) while SD continued on a VI-like geometric schedule (mean 59 s). Generalization gradients (responses per 7.5 min) across three 6-day blocks were concave/decelerated and well fit by hyperbolae; slopes sharpened with continued discrimination training (greater suppression at SΔ-proximal values). In extinction (no reinforcement for any intensity, 12 days), overall rates dropped and gradients flattened, yet graded responding persisted until near zero. Gradients were governed by distance from SD, not direction (attenuation vs increase). Results mirror classic generalization findings across operant/respondent paradigms.

title: INTERMITTENT REINFORCEMENT OF OPERANTBEHA VIOR IN CHILDREN'
authors: Long, E.R.; Hammack, J.T.; May, F.; Campbell, B.J.
abstract: ~200 children (ages 4–8) responded on keys/manipulanda in 20–30 min sessions (typically weekly) under FR, FI, and VI schedules with tangible reinforcers (trinkets, pennies) and sometimes projected pictures. Basic schedule effects resembled those in nonhuman labs (e.g., VI steady rates; FR ratio-run; FI scallop initially), but FI scalloping in children was often transient, giving way to steady or irregular rates. Performance was highly sensitive to reinforcer value and schedule programming: changing trinket type/amount, adding pictures at reinforcement, swapping VI interval sets (“tapes”), lengthening intersession intervals, or seeding brief FRs before FI could elevate rates and reduce pausing/grain. Social/contextual factors and weak deprivation constrained control. Overall, standard schedules can reliably organize children’s free-operant behavior, with notable reinforcer- and context-dependencies

title: RESPONSE TO DIFFERENT DEGREES OF NOVELTY:THE INCIDENCE OF VARIOUS ACTIVITIES'
abstract: Thirty male hooded rats were observed for 15 min in one of three test cages that differed in degree of novelty relative to home cages: N-1 (striped walls), N-2 (striped walls + wire-mesh floor), N-3 (N-2 + ~73 dB white noise). Using a 6-s time-sample, observers coded sniffing (S), locomotion (W), grooming (G), freezing (F), lying (L), and miscellaneous (M). As novelty increased, sniffing decreased and grooming increased; locomotion declined over time in all groups; freezing tended to rise later in sessions for a subset of rats. Patterns evolved across five 3-min blocks, with stronger progressive changes under higher novelty. Results challenge “exploratory” vs. “fear” drive accounts and suggest analyzing novelty effects via stimulus cues, arousal, and existing response strengths.

title: EFFECTS OF SCOPOLAMINE ON A MULTIPLESCHEDULE
abstract: Three food-deprived male albino rats performed on multiple schedules while receiving intraperitoneal scopolamine hydrobromide (0–1.6 mg/kg). Two rats worked on a 4-ply multiple: FI 5-min (limited hold 2 min) food, SΔ (no programmed consequence), Sidman avoidance (R-S = S-S = 20 s, 0.4-s, 2-mA shock), SΔ. A third rat worked on a 2-ply (FI vs SΔ). Scopolamine decreased FI response rate and reduced FI quarter-life toward ~1.25 min (flattened scallop), increased SΔ responding (discrimination disruption), and in avoidance raised shock frequency: low doses increased both avoidance rate and shocks (poorer temporal control), high doses depressed avoidance below baseline. One rat showed tolerance across repeated 0.1 mg/kg doses. Authors interpret effects as combined depressant action plus loss of stimulus control consistent with “amnestic-like” disruption in rats.

title: SOME OBSER VA TIONS ON AN OPERANT IN HUMAN SUBJECTS ANDITS MODIFICA TION BY DEXTRO A MPHETA MINE'
process: Schedule-controlled human operant behavior; drug effects on temporal control (DRL-like timing) and response rate/distribution
abstract: Seven male medical students pressed a telegraph key for nickels under a compound contingency combining fixed ratio and DRL: reinforcement occurred on the nth response only if each counted response followed the prior response by ≥ x s (non-qualifying responses reset the IRt timer). Two parameter sets were tested: FR100@DRL 2.5 s and FR10@DRL 25 s. Inter-response-time (IRT) distributions peaked just above the minimum delay; subjects were more efficient and steadier at 25 s than 2.5 s. After 5 mg dextro-amphetamine (vs placebo), IRT distributions shifted left (shorter IRTs) with little change in total reinforcers earned. Results replicate classic amphetamine effects seen in animals—tendency toward shorter IRTs—within a verbally instructed human operant preparation.

title: THE A CCENTUA TION OF A RATE DIFFERENCEDURING EXTINCTION
process: Resistance to extinction; differential persistence of responding under multiple schedules
abstract: Four male pigeons were trained under a multiple VI 3-min VI 9-min schedule with distinct keylight colors signaling each component. Two deprivation levels (≈75% and ≈90–95% body weight) were alternated across days. Under reinforcement, rates were typical for VI performance and slightly higher in VI 3 than VI 9, with deprivation increasing overall rates but not the ratio between components. During extinction, responding declined smoothly but persisted longer and at higher initial rates in the stimulus previously correlated with VI 3, producing a marked accentuation of the prior rate difference (e.g., VI3/VI9 ratios ≈1.2 under reinforcement vs. up to 3–4 during extinction). Experienced pigeons showed greater persistence. The findings illustrate stimulus–schedule interaction effects revealed in extinction.

title: FIXED-INTERVAL REINFORCEMENT OF RUNNINC IN A WHEEL
process: Fixed-interval schedule performance; temporal patterning of continuous operant behavior
abstract: Two rats were reinforced for running in a wheel on a fixed-interval 5-minute (FI 5) schedule, with a 6-watt light as the discriminative stimulus. Each session included pre- and post-interval periods of unreinforced running in darkness to compare spontaneous and schedule-controlled activity. Initially, running showed brief bursts and erratic pauses, but stable FI scallops later emerged—characterized by postreinforcement pauses followed by acceleration in running and sometimes deceleration near reinforcement. Increasing wheel friction (20–100 g) reduced overall rates, lengthened pauses, and eliminated late-interval deceleration, likely reflecting reduced magazine checking. Extinction revealed orderly decline in running and reappearance of unreinforced patterns. The study demonstrates that continuous responses like running can form fixed-interval patterns analogous to discrete operants such as lever pressing.

title: SOME TIME-CORRELATED REINFORCEMENT SCHED ULESAND THEIR EFFECTS ON BEHAVIOR
process: ["Schedule control; transition from interval-like to ratio-like performance via temporally defined contingencies]
abstract:
Key-pecking by pigeons was reinforced under time-correlated (T) schedules in which a cycle alternated a reinforcement-available window (tᴰ) and a no-reinforcement window (tᴬ); only the first response in tᴰ was reinforced, otherwise the reinforcer was missed. With cycle lengths of 2 min and 10 min, T = tᴰ/(tᴰ+tᴬ) was decreased systematically. As T decreased, behavior shifted from interval-like (scalloped) to ratio-like: overall rate and short IRTs increased, initial–terminal rate differences shrank, and extinction resembled that after ratio schedules. Very small T values produced missed reinforcers and rate drop. Rate’s SD scaled linearly with mean rate. Prefeeding reduced output in a stepwise manner (ratio-like). Switching from a matched T to VR yielded no discernible change. A power function

title: THE MAINTENANCE OF BEHA VIOR BY THE TERMINA TIONAND ONSET OF INTENSE NOISE
process: ["Negative reinforcement; stimulus control by aversive-stimulus offset/onset; schedule interactions]
abstract: Rats pressed and released a lever under two aversive-sound procedures: (1) Escape (Keller)—a press (Rᴾ) during 108–116 dB broadband noise terminated the noise and produced a silent period (T₀), after which noise returned; presses during T₀ had no effect. (2) Termination–Onset (Hefferline)—Rᴾ terminated noise and a lever release (Rᴿ) turned it back on. Abrupt noise offset maintained higher escape rates than slow offset. Varying T₀ from 2–20 s had little effect on overall escape rate (except very short values), though responses clustered early in T₀. Amphetamine (2 mg/kg) markedly increased both Rᴾ and Rᴿ rates. Long layoffs (days–years) transiently elevated escape rates. In the Hefferline schedule, Rᴿ showed a warm-up; Rᴾ did not—supporting partly independent control of the two operants.

title: SOME NEURAL AND BEHAVIORAL CORRELATES OF ELECTRICALSELF-STIMULATION OF THE LIMBIC SYSTEM
authors: Porter, R.W.; Conrad, D.G.; Brady, J.V.
abstract: Nine rhesus monkeys received chronically implanted bipolar electrodes in limbic and related areas (septum, hypothalamus, anterior thalamus, hippocampus, amygdala, median forebrain bundle, etc.). After lever-press training, animals earned brief brain shocks (self-stimulation) while concurrent EEGs were recorded. Self-stimulation of septal/anterior hypothalamic/anterior thalamic sites reliably produced spike-and-slow-wave complexes (often septal) correlated with high lever-press rates. Hippocampal stimulation often evoked focal seizure discharges: sessions showed bursts of pressing followed by EEG seizure “breaks”; as seizure duration waned within a session, pressing declined until seizures reappeared. Amygdala stimulation at higher intensities (≈15–20 mA) induced seizures that suppressed responding for long periods. Results link site-specific EEG patterns to maintenance—or suppression—of self-stimulation behavior.

title: SOME OBSERVATIONS ON AN OPERANT IN THE OCTOPUS
abstract: Three Octopus vulgaris were shaped to pull a vertical lever that turned on a light; food (sardine pieces) was delivered at the opposite end of the tank contingent on lever operation. After stepwise shaping (approach → contact → lever operation), two animals showed reliable lever-pulling under continuous reinforcement across multiple sessions; fixed-ratio probes (FR 2–3) were briefly tolerated but increased latencies and produced breakdowns. When reinforcement was discontinued, responding declined with long pauses and session cessation—clear operant extinction; some spontaneous recovery occurred. A third animal exhibited competing behaviors (lamp grabbing, directed water jets) that interfered with stable responding. Results indicate the law of effect operates in O. vulgaris, suggesting wide phylogenetic generality of operant principles.

title: A COMPLEX CONCURRENT SCHEDULE OF REINFORCEMENT
process: FI) schedule performance; schedule interaction/induction; response allocation"
abstract: Two adult male chimpanzees worked on two keys concurrently: a right-key multiple schedule (FR→FI components) and a left-key VI schedule. With conc(VI 4, later VI 10; mult FR–FI) the animals often responded on both keys simultaneously (left hand on VI, right hand on mult). During transition to mult FR–FI, the FI component initially showed low, constant rates (little scalloping) while FR runs were typical. Extinguishing the left-key VI produced normal FI scallops on the right key; reinstating VI thereafter did not disrupt the established FI pattern. Very high FRs (up to FR 475) were sustained with brief pauses. Overall, the VI performance interfered with the development but not the maintenance of FI control; total reinforcement rate influenced right-key responding.

title: ONE BAR-PRESS PER DA Y:ACQUISITION AND EXTINCTION
abstract: Female hooded rats learned a single daily bar-press for food with the lever retracted immediately after each press. Two groups received 1 reinforced trial/day for 10 (E-10) or 50 (E-50) days, then 1 extinction trial/day for up to 75 days. A control group received only daily nonreinforced trials. E-50 reached a low, stable latency by extinction and showed no extinction across daily trials; E-10 largely matched this pattern (1 rat extinguished). In a second experiment, rats trained to criterion with 5 trials/day (≥5-min ITI) all extinguished within 60 extinction trials. Results show that immediate lever retraction alone does not prevent extinction; high trial spacing (≈1/day) is critical for maintaining responding without reinforcement.

title: SOME DATA ON BEHAVIOR REVERSIBILITYIN A STEADY STATE EXPERIMENT'
process: Steady-state response rate and behavioral reversibility
abstract: Three pigeons alternated repeatedly between two time-correlated reinforcement schedules differing in cycle length (tD + tA = 30.0 s vs. 0.94 s) while maintaining a constant proportion of deprivation (T = 0.05). Each schedule was continued until stable response rates were achieved, after which birds were switched to the alternate schedule—completing five full alternations. Response rates increased with shorter cycle lengths and remained roughly proportional across alternations. Although relative rates (ratios) stabilized after the first transition (≈0.65–0.84), absolute rates gradually increased across alternations, indicating partial but not complete reversibility of steady-state behavior. The findings suggest that steady-state response measures can be relatively recoverable across conditions, though subject to upward drift with repeated alternations.

title: INTER-RESPONSE TIME DISTRIBUTION AS A FUNCTION OFDIFFERENTIAL REINFORCEMENT OF TEMPORALLYSPA CED RESPONSES
authors: Kelleher, Roger, T.; Fry, W.; Cook, L.
process: Temporal discrimination and pacing under DRL / DRL-limited-hold schedules
abstract: Albino rats worked on DRL and DRL–limited-hold (DRL LH) schedules to study temporal discrimination via inter-response time (IRT) distributions and response-probability functions. Under DRL 20 LH 5, response probability stayed near zero for ~15 s after a press, then rose sharply around the reinforced window and declined beyond it; performances were precise and often produced long runs of consecutively reinforced responses. Removing response-produced auditory feedback slightly increased early IRTs but did not disrupt timing. Tightening the hold (DRL 20 LH 1) reduced percent reinforced yet maintained behavior. With DRL 20 (no LH, no feedback), timing was initially absent but developed across sessions. Similar precision emerged on DRL 18 LH 3, regardless of CRF or FR histories. Bursts and interim chains were largely absent."Three pigeons alternated repeatedly between two time-correlated reinforcement schedules differing in cycle length (tD + tA = 30.0 s vs. 0.94 s) while maintaining a constant proportion of deprivation (T = 0.05). Each schedule was continued until stable response rates were achieved, after which birds were switched to the alternate schedule—completing five full alternations. Response rates increased with shorter cycle lengths and remained roughly proportional across alternations. Although relative rates (ratios) stabilized after the first transition (≈0.65–0.84), absolute rates gradually increased across alternations, indicating partial but not complete reversibility of steady-state behavior. The findings suggest that steady-state response measures can be relatively recoverable across conditions, though subject to upward drift with repeated alternations.

title: THE ACTION OF A TROPINE, BENA CTYZINE, AND SCOPOLA MINEUPON FIXED-INTERVAL AND FIXED-RATIO BEHAVIOR
abstract: Two water-deprived rats worked on a multiple schedule alternating FR 26 (clicking) and FI 4-min (tone). After stable baseline, rats received i.p. injections of atropine, benactyzine, or scopolamine (various doses). Cumulative records and average rates (per half-session) were compared to multi-session controls via drug/control ratios. High doses of all three anticholinergics disrupted temporal patterning: FI scallops disappeared (steady, moderate responding) and FR pauses/runs degraded; overall FR rates fell, whereas FI mean rate sometimes did not decrease because drugs reduced the usual post-reinforcement pause despite lower terminal rates. Medium doses often altered FI with little FR change (differential sensitivity). Low doses produced slight rate increases without pattern loss. Benactyzine effects were shorter-lived; scopolamine low doses could elevate FI rate. Authors note the multiple performance was partly governed by sequence rather than pure stimulus control.

title: THE EFFECT OF SECOBARBITAL ON A MULTIPLESCHEDULE IN THE MONKE
process: ["Schedule-controlled performance under multiple FI/FR; drug effects on temporal patterning"
abstract: Two adult male rhesus monkeys responded on a multiple FI 2-min / FR 40 schedule for water (0.5 cc) with component-correlated lights. After stable baseline, secobarbital (e.g., 30 mg/kg, s.c.) produced three phases: (1) a brief stimulant period (FI pause loss; high, erratic rates), (2) an overall depressant phase (near-abolition of FI; FR broken into high-rate bursts separated by pauses), and (3) a post-depression excitatory phase where FR normalized sooner, while FI remained disrupted (little pausing, rough grain, late return of scallops). Larger doses lengthened/intensified these phases. Results replicate schedule-dependent barbiturate sensitivity: FI is more affected than FR. Verhave notes that FI disruption in the late phase could reflect impaired discrimination, tempering claims that barbiturates neatly “fractionate” tandem FI-FR components."Two water-deprived rats worked on a multiple schedule alternating FR 26 (clicking) and FI 4-min (tone). After stable baseline, rats received i.p. injections of atropine, benactyzine, or scopolamine (various doses). Cumulative records and average rates (per half-session) were compared to multi-session controls via drug/control ratios. High doses of all three anticholinergics disrupted temporal patterning: FI scallops disappeared (steady, moderate responding) and FR pauses/runs degraded; overall FR rates fell, whereas FI mean rate sometimes did not decrease because drugs reduced the usual post-reinforcement pause despite lower terminal rates. Medium doses often altered FI with little FR change (differential sensitivity). Low doses produced slight rate increases without pattern loss. Benactyzine effects were shorter-lived; scopolamine low doses could elevate FI rate. Authors note the multiple performance was partly governed by sequence rather than pure stimulus control.

title: AN INTERMEDIA TE RESTRAINING DEVICE FORWORKING WITH MONKEYS'
abstract: Findley describes a restraining and harness system that provides a practical compromise between complete restraint and freedom for monkeys in operant research. The device consists of adjustable neck and waist collars attached to a pivoted harness, allowing limited movement (leaning, slight rotation, partial standing) while maintaining stable positioning in either a home stand or working chair. The working chair design also enables reliable electric shock delivery via conductive studs in the waist collar and an electrically isolated pivot rod, ensuring consistent contact through hair without tight fitting. The system allows for rapid transfer (~2 minutes) between stands, single-operator handling, secure drug injection, weighing, and maintenance of multiple monkeys efficiently."Two adult male rhesus monkeys responded on a multiple FI 2-min / FR 40 schedule for water (0.5 cc) with component-correlated lights. After stable baseline, secobarbital (e.g., 30 mg/kg, s.c.) produced three phases: (1) a brief stimulant period (FI pause loss; high, erratic rates), (2) an overall depressant phase (near-abolition of FI; FR broken into high-rate bursts separated by pauses), and (3) a post-depression excitatory phase where FR normalized sooner, while FI remained disrupted (little pausing, rough grain, late return of scallops). Larger doses lengthened/intensified these phases. Results replicate schedule-dependent barbiturate sensitivity: FI is more affected than FR. Verhave notes that FI disruption in the late phase could reflect impaired discrimination, tempering claims that barbiturates neatly “fractionate” tandem FI-FR components."Two water-deprived rats worked on a multiple schedule alternating FR 26 (clicking) and FI 4-min (tone). After stable baseline, rats received i.p. injections of atropine, benactyzine, or scopolamine (various doses). Cumulative records and average rates (per half-session) were compared to multi-session controls via drug/control ratios. High doses of all three anticholinergics disrupted temporal patterning: FI scallops disappeared (steady, moderate responding) and FR pauses/runs degraded; overall FR rates fell, whereas FI mean rate sometimes did not decrease because drugs reduced the usual post-reinforcement pause despite lower terminal rates. Medium doses often altered FI with little FR change (differential sensitivity). Low doses produced slight rate increases without pattern loss. Benactyzine effects were shorter-lived; scopolamine low doses could elevate FI rate. Authors note the multiple performance was partly governed by sequence rather than pure stimulus control.

title: OPERANT-LEVEL LEVER PRESSING BY A MONKEY AS A FUNCTIONOF INTER TEST INTERVAL
abstract: A naïve Cebus monkey was tested for spontaneous (“operant-level”) lever pressing across 252 half-hour sessions under varying intertest intervals of 15, 39, and 63 hours. Lever presses produced no programmed consequences. Responding decreased with repeated daily tests but recovered as the intertest interval increased, indicating a time-dependent recovery of baseline operant activity. Across four replications, lever-press frequency was consistently highest at the 63-hour interval. Analyses revealed significant effects of both intertest interval and replication, suggesting that nonreinforced lever pressing in monkeys may recover with rest rather than extinction reversal. The pattern was consistent with temporal spacing effects previously observed in rats.

title: A NOTATION SYSTEM FOR THE DESCRIPTIONOF BEHAVIORAL PROCEDURES'
process: Formalization and symbolic representation of reinforcement contingencies and behavioral schedules

abstract: Mechner proposes a formal symbolic notation for describing behavioral procedures (schedules of reinforcement, contingencies, etc.) in a concise, logical, and diagrammatic format. The system integrates conventions from psychological paradigms, Boolean algebra, flow-chart notation, and mathematics to express stimulus–response–time relationships unambiguously. Examples include standard schedules (FI, DRL, Sidman avoidance, counting, conjunctive, interlocking) and complex procedures involving time intervals, response magnitude, and mathematical functions. Mechner demonstrates that the notation can facilitate classification of behavioral procedures and reveal formal parallels (e.g., DRL vs. counting schedules). The paper concludes that a standardized symbolic system could advance behavioral science by enhancing precision, communication, and theoretical analysis.

title: DELAYED MATCHING IN THE PIGEON
abstract: Pigeons performed delayed matching-to-sample (DMS) with a 1-s sample (flickering vs. steady white light), followed by a delay (0–10 s), then two keys (one flicker, one steady). A single grain delivery followed correct matches; correction trials repeated the same sequence but only first choices counted. In two birds, accuracy decreased monotonically with delay (~90–95% at 0 s to ~60–70% at 5 s). In two others, performance was higher but unstable—linked to stereotyped “mediating” behaviors during the delay that differed by sample type and appeared to guide choice. Birds lacking differentiated mediating chains showed orderly delay gradients. Results highlight the role of delay-period behavior in maintaining stimulus control over short delays.

title: A TECHNIQUE FOR DELIVERING SHOCK TO PIGEONS'
abstract: Azrin describes a method for delivering precise and reliable electric shock to pigeons via subcutaneous electrode implantation around the pubis bone. The electrodes connect to an external power source through a light harness and pulley system, allowing free movement without electrical variability. This approach eliminates problems associated with electrified grids (e.g., poor contact, escape responses, current inconsistency, feces shorting). Pigeons maintained normal performance under food-reinforced schedules, and response suppression occurred immediately when shock was delivered contingent on behavior. The technique provides consistent control of shock intensity and duration while preventing unintended escape responses, making it ideal for operant studies involving aversive control.

title: CONFIRMATION OF A POSITIVE RELATION BETWEEN DEPRIVATION ANDNUMBER OF RESPONSES EMITTED FOR LIGHT REINFORCEMENT
abstract: Segal tested whether food and water deprivation affected the number of responses rats emitted when lever presses produced brief light onset. Thirty-six male albino rats were divided into three deprivation levels (satiated, food-deprived, food + water-deprived). After 30 days of operant-level baseline sessions in darkness (no reinforcement), a single light-reinforcement session was introduced. While deprivation slightly decreased unreinforced (operant-level) bar pressing, it markedly increased responding when light onset was contingent on bar pressing, confirming Davis (1958)’s finding of a positive relation between deprivation and light-maintained responding. The results challenge interpretations based solely on general activity or drive and support the view that deprivation lowers thresholds to external stimulation.

title: INTERACTION BETWEEN THE COMPONENTS OF A CHAINED SCHEDULE
abstract: Hanson and Witoslawski examined how varying the fixed-ratio (FR) requirement in a chained fixed-interval fixed-ratio (FI FR) schedule affected responding during the FI component. Three food-deprived male Wistar rats pressed a lever for sweetened condensed milk under chain FI 4-min FR n schedules, where FR values were systematically changed (FR 5, 60, 120). As FR size increased, response rates during the FI component declined sharply and systematically, though their temporal distribution (scalloped pattern) was preserved. Total responses per reinforcement increased slightly with larger FRs, suggesting interaction beyond a simple inverse relation. The findings show that increasing response requirement in the terminal link reduces responding in the initial link, demonstrating strong interdependence between chained components.

title: AVOIDANCE RESPONDING AS A FUNCTION OF SIMULTANEOUSAND EQUAL CHANGES IN TWO TEMPORAL PARAMETERS
abstract: The nature of the relationship between rate of responding and the interval by whicheach response postpones an electric shock is reported. Throughout the experiment theshock-shock interval was varied simultaneously and kept equal to the response-shockinterval. Certain features of the inter-response-time distributions were also reported.

title: SOME BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS OF A TWO-VALUED,TEMPORALLY DEFINED REINFORCEMENT SCHED ULE
process: ["Variable-interval control under temporally defined (tD) two-valued schedules; schedule-controlled response rate and temporal patterning."
abstract: Eighteen pigeons (six groups of three) pecked under two-valued, temporally defined VI schedules in which cycles of 2.0 min and 0.5 min alternated randomly. Two parameters were manipulated: P (proportion of long cycles: 0.95, 0.90, 0.60, 0.40, 0.10, 0.05) and T (fraction of each cycle during which reinforcement could occur), with T stepped from 1.0 → 0.003125. Session rates (last 20 min of 30-min sessions) rose gradually across ~60–100 days and increased as T decreased, but the magnitude depended on P. Groups with P≈0.40 showed mixed-schedule-like temporal discriminations and large rate increases; P≈0.60 yielded low, linear rates resembling arithmetic VI with small T effects; extreme P’s behaved like fixed 2-min or 30-s schedules. Results clarify how simple two-interval mixtures can reproduce classic VI phenomena.

title: AN ANALYSIS OF THE BEHA VIOR OF RATS AND MONKEYSON CONCURRENT FIXED-RA TIO A VOIDANCE SCHED ULES
authors: Kelleher, Roger, T.; Fry, W.; Cook, L.
abstract: Rats (FR 50 or FR 200) and squirrel monkeys (FR 150) responded on concurrent schedules combining a fixed-ratio (FR) food program with Sidman (unsignaled) shock-avoidance (SS=RS=30 s; each response postponed shock 30 s). Control performance showed two alternating rates within each ratio: a low, stable “avoidance” rate immediately after reinforcement, then an abrupt shift to a high FR rate until food. To analyze component control and interaction, the authors ran avoidance extinction (no shock), FR extinction (no food), and prefeeding. Removing shock produced post-reinforcement pauses and predominantly high FR rates; removing food or prefeeding yielded predominant low avoidance rates. Monkeys’ avoidance was higher-rate and more extinction-resistant than rats’. Overall, components largely add without strong interaction.

title: A VERSIVE CONTROL WITH THE PIGEON'
abstract: The authors show that pigeons can acquire and maintain operant behavior under aversive control when reinforcement is shock termination/avoidance. After unreliable grid/foot methods, they introduce a robust wing-band electrode technique. Because shaping key pecks under shock was fragile (competing elicited reactions, immediate suppression when nonreinforced), they switch to a simpler, automatically shaped head-lifting response using a photocell beam: breaking the beam terminates pulsed shock (escape), and—with a 1-kHz tone CS 4 s before shock—can prevent shock (avoidance). Birds quickly acquire short-latency escape; avoidance emerges slowly across sessions but can reach high levels once criteria are incremented gradually. The paper provides a practical preparation for studying aversive reinforcement and avoidance in pigeons.

title: THE EFFECT OF RESERPINE ON THE CONDITIONEDEMOTIONAL RESPONSE IN THE GUINEA PIG
process: Conditioned emotional response (CER); suppression ratio as a function of aversive conditioning and pharmacological modulation (reserpine)
abstract: Twelve guinea pigs were trained to press a lever for water reinforcement on a variable-interval (VI) schedule and subsequently exposed to a conditioned emotional response (CER) procedure in which a clicker was followed by shock. The study examined behavioral suppression and the effects of reserpine at varying doses. Unlike rats, guinea pigs exhibited partial suppression of lever pressing during the conditioned stimulus and never drank during this period. Low doses of reserpine (0.003–0.004 mg/kg) markedly decreased overall responding and produced immobility during the clicker, while higher or chronic doses nearly eliminated lever pressing. The findings demonstrate both species-specific differences in CER behavior and the extreme sensitivity of guinea pigs to reserpine.

title: TITRATION BEHA VIOR ON VARIOUS FRACTIONAL ESCAPE PROGRAMS
abstract: Rats were trained to press a lever to produce fractional reductions in electric shock intensity under a “titration” schedule, in which shock intensity periodically increased in discrete steps. The study examined how response patterns varied with the rate of programmed shock increments and with ratio requirements for producing decrements. The median maintained shock level rose as the interval between increments shortened and as more responses were required per decrement, reflecting a higher response threshold and competing behavior under stronger aversive control. Wave-like oscillations in shock level and irregular responding emerged under certain parameters, suggesting analogies with psychophysical threshold behavior. The findings characterize titration schedules as a continuous, fractional form of escape responding.

title: DIFFERENTIAL DRUG EFFECTS UPON A THREE-PLYMULTIPLE SCHEDULE OF REINFORCEMENT
process: Multiple-schedule operant performance (food-maintained CRF vs. Sidman avoidance vs. extinction) under pharmacological modulation.
abstract: Four food-deprived rats worked on a three-component multiple schedule—Extinction (SA), Continuous Reinforcement (CRF with sweetened milk), and Sidman shock avoidance (RS=SS=10 s)—repeated in 9-min cycles (13/day). Drugs were administered i.p. to test differential effects across components. Morphine (≈5–15 mg/kg) disrupted CRF at lower doses than avoidance; amphetamine (0.5–2 mg/kg) markedly increased rates in all components, especially SA, with “drinkometer” evidence of response–consumption dissociation; pentobarbital (8–24 mg/kg) produced biphasic effects with transient depressions followed by elevated SA; chlorpromazine (≥2–3 mg/kg) selectively impaired avoidance while sparing CRF at intermediate doses; MAO inhibitors (iproniazid, nialamide; high/extended doses) produced prolonged CRF disruption (“loss of appetite”) with comparatively minor effects on avoidance/SA. The preparation cleanly revealed drug-specific behavioral profiles.

title: SCHEDULES OF REINFORCEMENT AND DISCRIMINA TION LEARNING'
authors: Green, E.J.; Sanders, R.M.; Squire, R.W.
process: Human operant discrimination learning under different schedules of reinforcement
abstract: Seventy college students were tested in a visual discrimination task (1-2-5 light pattern) under 13 reinforcement schedules: continuous, fixed ratio (FR 5–20), variable ratio (VR 5–20), fixed interval (FI 5–30 s), and variable interval (VI 5–30 s). Each 1-hour session included 60 presentations (30 SD, 30 SΔ), with lever-press responses earning points under schedule control. Discrimination indices (SD responses / total responses) revealed that shorter ratios and intervals produced more rapid and stable discriminations. Longer ratios led to “ratio strain” and higher variability; longer intervals yielded flatter SD–SΔ separation and signs of temporal discrimination. The results indicated that human discrimination performance reflects response differentiation shaped by reinforcement contingencies, paralleling nonhuman data but bounded by fixed stimulus intervals.

title: PUNISHMENT A ND RECO VER Y D URING FIXED-RA TIO PERFORMA NCE'
abstract: Six pigeons were trained on fixed-ratio (FR 10–50) schedules with food reinforcement, then exposed to immediate electric-shock punishment after each response. Shock intensity was systematically increased from 1 V to 120 V across 120 days. Punishment selectively lengthened postreinforcement pauses but did not reduce the local (terminal) response rate once responding began. Higher voltages prolonged pauses and reduced total responding, with recovery of performance occurring both within and across sessions. Even severe punishment (80–120 V) produced partial recovery, though early-session pauses remained extended. The results indicate that punishment in FR schedules primarily affects response initiation rather than rate, contrasting with generalized suppression seen in variable-interval schedules.

title: GENERA LIZA TION AND PREFERENCE ON ASTIMULUS-INTENSITY CONTINUUM
abstract: Pigeons pecked a small red key light while stimulus intensity varied over 11 steps (~3.6 log units). Experiment I (n=6): each bird was trained at a single intensity (continuous light), then tested without reinforcement as intensity swept up/down in 30-s steps. Generalization gradients were smooth, peaked near training intensity, sometimes asymmetric or slightly displaced; no evidence of a monotonic “brighter→faster” effect. Experiment II (n=4): birds were reinforced equally at all intensities (FI 1-min during test). A preference emerged for lower-middle intensities. The preference may partly explain asymmetries in single-intensity gradients; alternatively, summing identical gradients centered on each reinforced intensity approximated the observed preference.

title: A FLEXIBLE MODEL FOR GENERALIZATION GRADIENTS
abstract: Schlosberg describes a mechanical analog model of stimulus generalization that substitutes a physical apparatus for the behaving organism. The model uses movable ordinates (weighted bicycle spokes) on a graph representing stimulus values, with tensioned threads or wires forming a “generalization gradient.” Reinforcement and extinction are simulated by adding weights to specific ordinates. Adjusting the tension or stiffness of the connecting line reproduces empirical patterns reported in the literature—such as linear gradients (Solomon & Schlosberg, 1943), depressed gradients after repeated unreinforced tests (Pierrel, 1958), and curved, shifted peaks (Hanson, 1957). The model provides a flexible teaching and conceptual tool for exploring how reinforcement distribution and stimulus spacing determine generalization gradients.

title: THE PSYCHIATRIC NURSE AS A BEHAVIORAL ENGINEER'
process: extinction, DRA/DRI, FI limited-hold social reinforcement, escape/avoidance, and satiation
abstract: Across multiple psychiatric wards, nurses implemented operant programs to reduce disruptive behaviors (e.g., office-intrusion, psychotic talk, violence, hoarding) and to strengthen deficits (e.g., self-feeding). Using time-sample observations and simple contingencies—primarily extinction of attention, differential reinforcement of incompatible/alternative behavior (DRI/DRA), fixed-interval “limited-hold” social reinforcement, escape/avoidance via mild food-spilling, and stimulus satiation (abundant magazines)—patients’ target behaviors changed over 6–11 weeks with 24/7 coverage. Case data show selective decreases in problem behavior (often via withdrawing nurse attention) and increases in adaptive responses (self-feeding maintained >10 months). Practical constraints (staff turnover, “bootleg” reinforcement) and staff attitudes are discussed, framing nurses as day-to-day behavior engineers."Schlosberg describes a mechanical analog model of stimulus generalization that substitutes a physical apparatus for the behaving organism. The model uses movable ordinates (weighted bicycle spokes) on a graph representing stimulus values, with tensioned threads or wires forming a “generalization gradient.” Reinforcement and extinction are simulated by adding weights to specific ordinates. Adjusting the tension or stiffness of the connecting line reproduces empirical patterns reported in the literature—such as linear gradients (Solomon & Schlosberg, 1943), depressed gradients after repeated unreinforced tests (Pierrel, 1958), and curved, shifted peaks (Hanson, 1957). The model provides a flexible teaching and conceptual tool for exploring how reinforcement distribution and stimulus spacing determine generalization gradients.

title: THE EFFECT OF CHANGING THE SCHEDULE OF REINFORCEMENTUPON DURATION OF RESPONDING'
abstract: Schaefer and Steinhorst (1959) examined how changes in reinforcement schedules affect the duration of lever pressing (D) in rats. Eleven male rats were trained under continuous reinforcement (CRF) and later exposed to FR 5, FR 10, or shock-paired schedules. Lever press duration (D) was reliably measured using a microswitch system. Results showed that D was a stable, individual-specific characteristic under steady reinforcement but became unstable when the schedule changed (especially from CRF to FR 5). D increased immediately following schedule changes, often before rate changes appeared. Shock reduced both D and response output, while minor external disturbances had little effect. Findings highlight D as a sensitive microbehavioral index of reinforcement contingencies.

title: EFFECTS OF METHYLPHENIDYLACETATE AND CHLORPROMAZINEON CERTAIN COMPONENTS OF GENERAL ACTIVITY
process: Drug-induced modulation of operant and nonoperant behavioral components
abstract: Bindra and Baran (1959) analyzed how methylphenidylacetate (Ritalin) and chlorpromazine (Largactil) differentially affect specific components of rat general activity. Thirty-two male rats were observed after injections of three dosages of each drug, or saline control. Using timed observations, the researchers quantified sniffing, lying, grooming, and frequency of activity changes. Methylphenidylacetate increased sniffing and behavioral variability while decreasing lying; chlorpromazine produced the opposite pattern—reducing sniffing and activity changes but leaving lying and grooming largely unaffected. Effects peaked within 30–60 minutes and were dosage-dependent. The study demonstrated that analyzing discrete behavioral components yields greater sensitivity to pharmacological effects than gross activity measures.

title: THE RELATION OF AMOUNT OF REINFORCEMENT TO PERFORMANCEUNDER A FIXED-INTERVAL SCHEDULE
authors: Stebbins, W.C.; Mead, P.B.; Martin, J.M.
process: Effects of reinforcer magnitude on operant performance under fixed-interval schedules
abstract: Two food-deprived rats worked on an FI 2-min schedule while the amount of primary reinforcement was varied via sucrose concentration (5%, 12.7%, 32%, 50%; 0.02-ml dipper). After stable FI performance, each concentration was tested for 4 hours. Response rate increased with log sucrose concentration across the tested range: little change from 5%→12.7%, higher at 32%, and highest at 50% (contrary to prior VI findings of a downturn at high concentrations, likely due here to small reinforcer volume limiting satiation). Cumulative records and interval histograms showed concentration-related changes in within-interval distribution (“scallop”) with some evidence of sharper terminal responding at certain concentrations. Authors suggest an exponential rate-concentration relation under these parameters.

title: GENERA LIZA TION OF CONDITIONED SUPPRESSION'
abstract: Ray and Stein (1959) examined generalization of conditioned suppression along a tone-frequency continuum in rats. Lever pressing for milk on a VI 2-min schedule was suppressed by a 1800 Hz tone paired with shock (CS), while a 200 Hz tone (SA) signaled safety. Once discrimination stabilized, intermediate tones (560, 980, 1120, 1500 Hz) were presented during test sessions. Suppression ratios decreased systematically with increasing similarity to the CS, forming smooth gradients for two of three subjects. Results confirm that conditioned aversive control follows the same generalization principles as appetitive conditioning, demonstrating stimulus control of fear responses across auditory frequencies.

title: A PRECISION LIQUID FEEDING SYSTEM CONTROLLEDBY LICKING BEHA VIOR'
abstract: Hulse (1959) describes a precision liquid-feeding apparatus for controlling fluid delivery contingent on a rat’s licking behavior. The system integrates a specially designed plexiglass drinking tube with a brass electrical contact, an electronic relay that detects each lick, and an infusion pump driven by a rotary solenoid to deliver precise drop volumes. A solenoid-driven lever retracts the fluid between licks to prevent unreinforced contact. The design allows fine control of liquid amount per lick, total consumption, and reinforcement schedules, enabling the use of licking as an operant response. The apparatus provides reliable, quantitative control over consummatory behavior for research applications in operant and taste conditioning.

title: A SIMPLE INTER VAL-PROGRAMMING CIRCUIT'
abstract: Clark and Caudill (1959) describe a low-cost interval-programming circuit for use with Gerbrands punched-tape or cam-timer systems. The design automates fixed-interval (FI), variable-interval (VI), and limited-hold schedules by coordinating relay-controlled lock-up and timing mechanisms. The circuit employs relays, resistors, capacitors, and diodes to control reinforcement availability and prevent premature responses, with adjustable parameters for timing reliability and discharge characteristics. The apparatus can also operate as a self-recycling or multiple-schedule timer. The authors provide detailed specifications for construction and calibration, emphasizing reliability, flexibility, and low expense (approx. $15–25 in 1959).

title: STIMULUS GENERALIZATION AS A FUNCTION OF THE TIMEBETWEEN TRAINING AND TESTING PROCEDURES
authors: Thomas, D.R.; Ost, J.; Thomas, D.
abstract: Thomas, Ost, and Thomas (1959) examined how time intervals between training and testing affect stimulus generalization in pigeons. After discrimination training with 550 nm (SD) and 570 nm (SΔ) lights, groups were tested 1, 7, or 21 days later. All groups showed peak responding at 540 nm, shifted away from SΔ, with gradients equally steep across time delays. Relearning sessions after 7 and 21 days revealed strong retention and minimal responding to SΔ. The results indicate that discrimination learning and the associated generalization gradient remain stable for at least 21 days without practice, contradicting prior findings that delay weakens gradients.

title: SOME ELECTRONIC CONTROL UNITS FOR OPERANT BEHAVIOR STUDIES:I. A RESPONSE AND REINFORCEMENT CONTINGENCY TRANSLATOR'
authors: Schoenfiled, W.N.; Cumming, W.W.; Hearst, E.; Waine, M.; Snapper, A.G.; Haas, P.
abstract: Schoenfeld, Cumming, Hearst, Waine, Snapper, and Haas (1959) describe an advanced electronic “translator” unit for controlling and recording operant behavior contingencies. The system electronically processes response pulses, controls reinforcement timing, and interfaces with programming systems for flexible experimental design. It accepts response input, outputs to counters or recorders, and manages reinforcement availability via pulse gates, flip-flops, and one-shot circuits. The unit handles extremely high response rates (up to 100 kHz) and allows precise timing and manual testing through control switches and indicators. Accompanying Schmitt trigger circuits ensure stable operation. The design offers reliability, modularity, and versatility for complex temporal reinforcement schedules.

title: DISCRIMINA TION OF CHA NGES IN HEA R T RA TE:TWO UNSUCCESSFUL A TTEMPTS
abstract: Mandler and Kahn (1959) conducted two experiments to determine whether verbal behavior could be conditioned to changes in heart rate. In the first study, a subject attempted to label cardiac acceleration or deceleration (“FAST” or “SLOW”) with reinforcement. Apparent success was later shown to be based on respiratory changes correlated with heart rate (sinus arrhythmia), not direct cardiac discrimination. A second subject, instructed only to “guess” which of two lights would appear while reinforcement coincided with cardiac changes, performed at chance across 4,675 trials. The findings indicate that adults cannot easily discriminate or verbally respond to discrete cardiac rate changes, suggesting that “emotional” verbal behavior may rely on broader visceral cues.

title: HUMAN OBSERVING BEHAVIOR AFTER SIGNAL DETECTION
process: discriminated operant control and postreinforcement stimulus control
abstract: Laties and Weiss (1959) examined postreset observing responses (PRORs) in human subjects performing a signal detection task under fixed-interval schedules. Participants monitored a meter pointer that occasionally deflected and pressed one key to reset it and another to illuminate the dial. PRORs—key presses made shortly after a reset—were analyzed under four manipulations: (1) reinforcing PRORs increased their frequency; (2) adding an auditory signal to the reset reduced PRORs; (3) suggesting the reset key was unreliable increased PRORs; and (4) requiring use of one hand for both keys had no effect. Results indicate PRORs function as discriminated operants under control of post-reinforcement stimuli, demonstrating how reinforcement distinctiveness and uncertainty shape human observing behavior.

title: THE AVERSIVE CONTROL OF AN OPERANT DISCRIMINATION'
abstract: Appel (1960) examined whether rats’ Sidman avoidance responding (RS 20 s, SS 2 s) could come under stimulus control. A tone signaled SD (avoidance active); in SΔ the avoidance contingency was removed while unavoidable shocks were delivered at 0, 0.20, or 0.50 shocks/min. With no shocks in SΔ, rats acquired the discrimination via extinction of SΔ responding while SD rates stayed higher. Introducing unavoidable SΔ shocks prevented or degraded discrimination: SΔ responding rose (and often SD rose modestly), keeping SD vs. SΔ rates similar; in some animals prior good discrimination was impaired when shocks (even 1 every 5 min) were added to SΔ. Thus, unavoidable shocks in SΔ sustain responding and block discrimination based on aversive control.

title: MULTIPLE SCHEDULES OF TIME-CORRELATEDREINFORCEMENT
process: Schedule control under time-correlated/limited-hold reinforcement showing a transition from interval-like to ratio-like performance
