title: OPERANT BEHAVIOR IN MENTAL DEFECTIVES:EXPLORATORY STUDIES'
authors: Ellis, N.R.; Barnett, C.D.; Pryer, M. W.:
abstract: Ellis, Barnett, and Pryer explored free-operant performance in institutionalized adults labeled “mentally defective” (historical term). Using a Lindsley manipulandum and candy (later cigarettes) as reinforcers with a 5-s time-out, Study 1 (N=12; IQ≤30) established lever pressing (CRF → FR 10) and compared FR 30, VI 1-min, FI 1-min, and extinction. Interval schedules produced erratic, pause-laden records; FR produced smooth, high, linear rates with pauses mainly post-reinforcement. Study 2 (N=26; IQ 30–70; MA 3–9 yr) escalated FR from 10 up to 1024: higher MA/CA predicted higher overall rates; many participants sustained very high ratios; reliability across days was good (odd–even ρ≈.95). Some “superstitious” adjunctive acts were noted. Authors suggest operant methods are feasible for training severely impaired populations.

title: BEHA VIOR STABILITY UNDER EXTENDED EXPOSURE TOA TIME-CORRELA TED REINFORCEMENT CONTINGENCY
abstract: Cumming & Schoenfeld examined how “steady state” behavior actually stabilizes under prolonged exposure to a time-correlated reinforcement schedule. Six pigeons (“birds”) worked for many months on a 30-s cycle with a limited-hold proportion T = 0.05. The authors tracked response rate day-by-day, evaluated common stability criteria (e.g., a 6-day ≤5% split-half rule), and compared early blocks to very long-run performance. They found sizeable within- and between-block variability, coefficients of variation ~19% across birds, and showed the 6-day rule often selects non-representative means. Typically 40–60 days were needed before block means bracketed the overall mean. After training, six 20-min extinction sessions produced bursts (“runs”) separated by pauses; extinction was relatively rapid. The paper argues for explicit, data-based stability standards in steady-state research.

title: INTERMITTENT REINFORCEMENT OF DISCRIMINA TIVEL Y CONTROLLEDRESPONSES AND RUNS OF RESPONSES'
abstract: This study examined the effects of intermittent reinforcement applied to discriminatively controlled responses in rats. Following stimulus onset (light + tone), only a subset of bar presses—or runs of bar presses—were reinforced. Despite reduced reinforcement frequency, animals maintained clear stimulus control, though response latency increased and became more variable. The study established that discrete discriminative stimuli can sustain operant behavior under sparse reinforcement, bridging partial reinforcement paradigms in runway studies with operant methods.

title: SOME EFFECTS OF BRAIN STIMULATIONON TIMING BEHA VIOR
abstract: This study examined how electrical brain stimulation as a reinforcer affects differential reinforcement of low rates (DRL) timing behavior in rhesus monkeys. Monkeys with chronically implanted electrodes received either food or intracranial stimulation in regions such as the globus pallidus, medial forebrain bundle, and anterior thalamus. Brain stimulation in the globus pallidus disrupted temporal discrimination—shortening interresponse times and increasing response rates—while stimulation in other areas did not. The pattern closely resembled that produced by amphetamine administration, suggesting region-specific neural mediation of timing processes.

title: SOME ELECTRONIC CONTROL UNITS FOR OPERANT BEHAVIORSTUDIES: II. A RANDOM RATIO GENERATOR'
authors: Schoenfiled, W.N.; Cumming, W.W.; Hearst, E.; Snapper, A.G.; Haas, P.
process: Random ratio (RR) reinforcement — probabilistic control of operant responding
abstract: This technical note describes an electronic circuit for generating random ratio reinforcement schedules. The device uses a high-frequency pulse divided and shaped through amplifiers and a phantastron delay circuit to produce brief discriminable reinforcement opportunities (tᴰ) separated by variable intercycle intervals (tᴵ). The system yields an equal and independent probability of reinforcement for each response, simulating random ratio contingencies in operant experiments. The authors also discuss calibration and substitution of commercial delay generators for precision control.

title: A CONTINUOUS STENCIL DUPLICATOR'
abstract: Ferster describes the design and operation of a low-cost, continuous stencil duplicator capable of producing long strips of printed material, such as teaching-machine tapes, beyond the size limits of commercial presses. The apparatus modifies a standard spirit duplicator with a roller-and-belt superstructure, enabling continuous stencil rotation and long-roll paper feed. With proper alignment, it can yield over 100 copies of six-foot tapes at minimal cost. The paper and stencil materials are commercially available, making the system practical for experimental or instructional applications.

title: SPEECH RA TE METER FOR VOCAL BEHA VIOR ANAL YSIS
process: Human verbal behavior measurement and analysis
abstract: Starkweather introduces an automated device for quantifying vocal behavior by recording the rate of speech from audio input. The “Speech Rate Meter” detects speech pulses corresponding to syllabic or emphasized vocal elements, converts them to uniform electrical pulses, and produces cumulative records representing word rate over time. Validation showed a strong correlation (r = 0.988) between instrument readings and manual word counts across interview samples. The system can also simultaneously record a conversational partner’s vocal activity to study listener reinforcement effects. The apparatus provides an objective, operant-compatible measure of human verbal behavior.

title: THE BEHA VIORAL EFFECTS OF REPEATED EXPOSURE TOTHREE MIXED EXTINCTION SCHEDULES
abstract: Weissman examined how rats respond under repeated extinction conditions embedded within mixed fixed-ratio (FR 24), fixed-interval (FI 2-min), and differential-reinforcement-of-low-rate (DRL 20-s) schedules. Across months of training, stable “trained extinction” patterns emerged that differed sharply from one-trial extinction. Under mixed FR schedules, extinction began with a brief “priming run” followed by little or no responding; under FI, extinction showed abrupt cutoffs after initial runs; and under DRL, extinction contained long pauses interspersed with occasional high-rate bursts absent during reinforcement. Despite comparable stability, rats under DRL emitted far more extinction responses than under FR or FI, indicating weaker discrimination of extinction onset.

title: EFFECTS OF PUNISHMENT INTENSITY DURINGVARIABLE-INTER VAL REINFORCEMENT
process: Punishment (response-contingent aversive stimulation) interacting with schedule-controlled behavior
abstract: Six pigeons responded on VI 1-min food schedules while every key-peck also produced electric shock via implanted electrodes. Adding punishment (and stepwise increases from 10→40–60 V) produced immediate, strong suppression followed by gradual recovery within and across sessions; higher intensities reduced or eliminated recovery. Decreasing intensity yielded slow, days-long increases in responding; removing punishment caused brief “overshoot” above baseline. Response rate under punishment remained orderly (uniform VI-like), scaled inversely with intensity and directly with deprivation, and re-suppressed after brief no-punishment timeouts.

title: SUPPRESSION OF A PERFORMANCE UNDER DIFFERENTIALREINFORCEMENT OF LOW RATES BY APRE-TIME-OUT STIMULUS
abstract: Two rhesus monkeys worked on VI schedules that were progressively made less favorable (VI 6→VI 20; added DRL 5–30 s). A pre-time-out (red-light) stimulus occurred every 18 min with a limited-hold: any response in the final 5 s produced a 60-min time out (all lights/feedback off). Despite leaner reinforcement (and added DRL), the pre-time-out stimulus reliably suppressed responding near its end and animals avoided triggering time outs. Thus, time out functioned aversively across conditions; no evidence that time out became positively reinforcing as the baseline schedule was degraded.

title: ESCAPE AND A VOIDANCE RESPONSE OF PRE-SCHOOL CHILDREN TOTWO SCHEDULES OF REINFORCEMENT WITHDRA WAL
process: Avoidance and escape behavior maintained by removal of positive reinforcement
abstract: Preschoolers viewed cartoons that periodically stopped (reinforcement withdrawal) unless they pressed a bar. Two contingencies were tested: (1) a Hefferline escape–avoidance schedule, where each bar press postponed the next interruption by n seconds, and (2) an “escalator” schedule, where each press added n seconds to the total delay before the next withdrawal. Both schedules established consistent responding: children rapidly learned to press to restore or maintain cartoons. Under the escalator schedule, bursts of responses alternated with plateaus, evolving into smooth avoidance patterns that minimized reinforcement loss. The study demonstrated that human avoidance responding could be maintained by the temporary loss of positive reinforcement.

title: OPERANT RESPONDING IN A HORSE UNDERSEVERAL SCHEDULES OF REINFORCEMENT
process: Operant conditioning under fixed-ratio and fixed-interval schedules of reinforcement
abstract: A 10-year-old horse was conditioned to nudge a lever with its snout to receive small grain reinforcers. After shaping under continuous reinforcement, the animal was exposed to fixed-ratio (FR 3–11) and fixed-interval (FI 1–3 min) schedules. The horse quickly acquired stable response patterns characteristic of other species—steady rates under ratio schedules and scalloped patterns under longer interval schedules. Despite no deprivation regimen, operant responding was maintained effectively, demonstrating that equine behavior could be systematically controlled and analyzed within a standard reinforcement framework.

title: TEMPORAL AND INTENSIVE PROPERTIES OF HUMAN VOCALRESPONDING UNDER A SCHEDULE OF REINFORCEMENT
abstract: Lane developed a method to measure human vocal operants (“oo” sounds) under differential reinforcement of low rates (DRL) schedules, recording both temporal and intensity properties. Subjects produced “oo” responses into a microphone that illuminated a viewing light when responses met DRL timing contingencies. Across sessions, response rates adjusted toward the rate that maximized reinforcement. Subjects showed either acceleration or deceleration in response rate depending on prior reinforcement history. Interresponse times shifted systematically, paralleling animal data, while response duration and intensity remained stable. This study demonstrated that human vocal behavior follows the same temporal dynamics as nonhuman operants under DRL control.

title: A MATHEMATICAL INDEX OF PERFORMANCE ONFIXED-INTERVAL SCHED ULES OF REINFORCEMENT
authors: Fry, W.; Kelleher, R.T.; Cook, L.
process: Fixed-Interval (FI) schedule performance and quantitative characterization of response acceleration
abstract:
This paper introduces a quantitative method—the index of curvature—for describing the characteristic shape of cumulative response curves under fixed-interval (FI) schedules. The index measures the deviation of the cumulative record from a straight line, capturing both the extent and direction of acceleration in responding. Derived mathematically by comparing the area under the response curve to that of an ideal constant-rate line, the index is expressed as:

Empirical examples with pigeons, rats, and squirrel monkeys show the index’s sensitivity to acquisition and drug effects (e.g., chlorpromazine reducing curvature). The measure provides a consistent, rate-independent metric of temporal patterning under FI control.

title: DECREMENT IN PERFORMANCE DURING PROLONGEDA VOIDANCE SESSIONS
process: Avoidance responding and performance degradation (fatigue) under prolonged exposure
abstract: Two rhesus monkeys were kept on a Sidman (unsignaled) avoidance schedule for seven consecutive days, pressing a lever to postpone electric shocks. Periodic fixed-interval (FI) 5-minute components were inserted to assess changes in temporal discrimination and operant control. Over the course of the week, avoidance response rates gradually declined and shock frequency increased, particularly after four days. Despite clear performance decrements, discrimination between avoidance and FI stimuli remained intact, and the typical FI scallop pattern persisted, though “flat” intervals (single or no responses) appeared more often. Observations suggested the monkeys could maintain minimal responding while dozing, indicating remarkable behavioral persistence despite fatigue and partial sleep deprivation.

title: SOME RELA TIONS BETWEEN THE A UDITOR Y SYSTEM OF THEMEDULLA AND AUDITORY-STIMULUS FUNCTIONS
authors: Harrison, J.M.; Abelson, R.M.; Fisher, G.L.
process: ["Auditory stimulus control and its disruption by neural lesions"
abstract: This study examined how lesions to specific medullary auditory pathways (ventral acoustic system and lateral lemniscus) affected operant performance in rats across several auditory and visual reinforcement schedules. Lesions reduced lever-pressing and increased lever-releasing under auditory aversive schedules but produced no impairment in comparable visual conditions. Control manipulations of stimulus intensity or noise removal did not replicate the lesion effects, indicating that the auditory pathways play a distinct role in mediating aversive stimulus control rather than simple sensory detection or intensity modulation.

title: FREE-OPERANT BEHAVIOR UNDER CONDITIONS OF DELA YEDREINFORCEMENT. I. CRF-TYPE SCHEDULES
abstract: Pigeons were studied under continuous reinforcement–type schedules in which reinforcement followed a response after a fixed delay interval (10, 30, or 100 s). Response rates decreased with increasing delay but remained higher than those produced by noncontingent reinforcement at equivalent intervals, confirming that delayed reinforcement maintained operant control. Comparisons of schedule types revealed higher response rates when responses during the delay did not postpone reinforcement. Pharmacological tests showed that methamphetamine increased responding proportionally more at lower baseline rates, while pentobarbital enhanced rates more at higher baselines, suggesting delay-dependent modulation of drug effects.

title: THE ACQUISITION AND EXTINCTION OF CONDITIONED SUPPRESSIONAS A FUNCTION OF THE BASE-LINE REINFORCER
abstract: This study examined how the type of base-line reinforcer (sweetened milk vs. water) influences the acquisition and extinction of conditioned suppression in rats. Using a tone–shock pairing procedure, rats lever-pressed for either milk or water on a VI 2-min schedule. Suppression (reduction in responding during the conditioned stimulus) developed more slowly with milk reinforcement, suggesting stronger operant behavior under a more potent reinforcer. During extinction (shock omitted), suppression dissipated faster with milk than with water reinforcement. When reinforcers were switched between phases, suppression partially transferred, indicating that conditioned suppression was partly tied to the reinforcement context.

title: REPEATED CONDITIONING-EXTINCTION SESSIONS AS A FUNCTIONOF THE REINFORCEMENT SCHEDULE
static-equation: Response Units=Responses per ReinforcementTotal Extinction Responses​
abstract: This study examined how different reinforcement schedules affect extinction performance in pigeons across repeated conditioning–extinction sessions. Three birds were trained on a fixed ratio (FR 10) and three on a fixed interval (FI 26 sec) schedule, each followed by a 60-minute extinction period. The number of extinction responses was converted to “response units” (responses per reinforcement) to compare schedules. Results showed that extinction responding stabilized within a narrow range, similar to findings in rats under comparable conditions (Weissman, 1959). Final extinction sessions were characterized by an initial “priming run” followed by pauses and occasional bursts, suggesting schedule-specific temporal control during extinction.

title: OPERANT DISCRIMINATION REVERSALS: COMPARA TIVE DA TA
process: Discrimination reversal learning and stimulus control
abstract: This comparative study examined discrimination reversal learning in a rat and a fish (Tilapia macrocephala) using a free-operant procedure with successive SD and SΔ presentations. Both species were trained to respond under a 30-second DRL contingency where illumination served as the discriminative stimulus. Over five reversals, both subjects showed decreasing SΔ responses, but the rat achieved reversal efficiency by the third reversal while the fish was still improving by the fifth. Further testing revealed that the rat’s responding came under control of the illumination change itself, rather than absolute intensity, indicating a shift in functional stimulus control across reversals.

title: SOME NOTES ON THE CONTROL OF CONDITIONED SUPPRESSION
abstract: Carlton and Didamo examined whether the rate or total number of reinforcements controls the degree of conditioned suppression in rats. Two subjects were trained to lever-press on a VI 2-minute schedule while periodic tones were paired with shock. The relative duration of tone (on-time/off-time) was varied across phases, with later phases delivering a constant total number of reinforcements. Suppression decreased as the proportion of tone time increased, despite total reinforcement being held constant—supporting the view that reinforcement rate, rather than total reinforcements missed, governs suppression. The authors proposed a discriminable “Weber–Fechner” type relationship between changes in reinforcement rate and suppression strength.

title: INTERMITTENT REINFORCEMENT OF MA TCHING TO SAMPLEIN THE PIGEON
process: Stimulus control and schedule-controlled matching behavior
abstract: Ferster extended the operant matching-to-sample paradigm to intermittent reinforcement schedules. Two pigeons were trained to peck a center key (sample) and then select one of two side keys (comparison) whose color either matched (SD) or did not match (SA) the sample. Matching responses produced brief conditioned reinforcers, while nonmatching responses produced short time-outs. The contingencies were arranged under fixed-ratio (FR), fixed-interval (FI), variable-interval (VI), and multiple FI–FR schedules. Matching accuracy increased with moderate ratio sizes (peaking at FR 15–20) and declined when ratios became too large (“ratio strain”). SA (nonmatching) responses were higher under interval schedules than ratio schedules. The findings demonstrated that complex chained discriminations could display the same schedule-controlled response patterns as simple operants, supporting the generality of reinforcement-schedule principles.

title: TEACHING MA CHINES: AN APPLICATION OF PRINCIPLESFROM THE LABORATORY
process: Programmed instruction and operant shaping of verbal behavior
abstract: Holland outlines the conceptual foundation of teaching machines as an application of operant conditioning principles rather than a mechanical innovation. He emphasizes that the essential advance lies in using reinforcement-based behavioral engineering to shape learning systematically, not in the hardware itself. Teaching machines automate the presentation of finely sequenced tasks and immediate feedback to ensure steady progress and individualized pacing. Data from revisions of a Harvard psychology program demonstrated that improvements in programmed material—based on student performance analyses—halved error rates and completion times. Holland concludes that genuine progress in education depends on applying experimentally derived behavioral principles, not mere automation.

title: NOTE ON RESPONSE DIFFERENTIATION DURINGA SIMPLE DISCRIMINATION
process: ["Operant discrimination learning with response-force differentiation as a secondary topography under stimulus control."
abstract:
Notterman and Block (1960) analyzed how response force differentiates during the acquisition of a simple light–dark discrimination in rats. Using a strain gauge to measure lever-press force, they found that while response rate increased under the reinforced light (

title: GENERALIZATION OF AUDITORY INTENSITY FOLLOWINGDISCRIMINATION TRAINING
process: Stimulus generalization of auditory intensity following operant discrimination training
abstract:
Pierrel and Sherman (1960) examined stimulus generalization of auditory intensity in rats following discrimination training on a 4 kHz tone. Two groups were trained to discriminate between tones differing by 20 dB, with

title: WA TER-DEPRIVA TION-PRODUCED SIGN REVERSAL OF ACONDITIONED REINFORCER BASED UPON DRY FOOD'
abstract: Pliskoff and Tolliver (1960) demonstrated that a conditioned reinforcer established through correlation with dry-food reinforcement can reverse its behavioral function under water deprivation. Rats performed on a fixed-ratio food lever and a timeout (TO) lever that produced a 5-minute suspension of food availability signaled by light and tone. Under normal conditions, TOs were rare, but during water deprivation, rats increasingly initiated TOs, suggesting that the stimuli previously associated with food (the TO complex) acquired aversive properties. When the TO contingency was placed on extinction, responding extinguished, confirming that the TO served as a negative reinforcer under thirst.

title: SOME ELECTRONIC CONTROL UNITS FOR OPERANT BEHAVIOR STUDIES:III. A TIMER AND COUNTER WITH PRINT-OUT
authors: Schoenfiled, W.N.; Cumming, W.W.; Hearst, E.; Waine, M.; Snapper, A.G.; Haas, P.
process: Measurement and control technology in operant conditioning
abstract: Schoenfeld, Cumming, Snapper, and Haas (1960) describe an electronic timing and counting apparatus designed for operant behavior research. The device measures response durations, interresponse times, and event frequencies over programmable intervals, with automated printout via a Hewlett-Packard digital recorder. It can resolve inter-event intervals as brief as 10 microseconds and durations down to 1 millisecond, offering adjustable accuracy through selectable tone frequencies (1 kHz–10 kHz). The unit integrates counting, timing, and signal coding functions to support diverse experimental contingencies in operant analysis.

title: SINGLE AND MULTIPLE SCHEDULES OF REINFORCEMENT INDEVELOPMENTALLY RETARDED CHILDREN'
abstract: Orlando and Bijou (1960) examined operant performance under single and multiple schedules of reinforcement in 46 institutionalized developmentally retarded children. Using candy reinforcers and free-operant lever pressing, the study analyzed response patterns under variable interval (VI), fixed interval (FI), variable ratio (VR), and fixed ratio (FR) schedules, as well as multiple VR–extinction and CRF–extinction schedules. The findings showed that the children’s response rates, pause distributions, and discrimination behaviors followed the same general patterns as those reported for nonhuman subjects, demonstrating reliable schedule control and stable responding under both simple and multiple contingencies.

title: SOME SCHEDULES INVOLVING A VERSIVE CONTROL"
process: Aversive control and stimulus control in operant discrimination
abstract: Appel (1960) studied monkeys on aversive multiple and mixed schedules combining Sidman avoidance (response postpones shock) with punishment (response produces shock) or extinction. Using discriminative stimuli (lights/noise) and reversals, he showed monkeys reliably discriminate avoidance vs. punishment (high rates in avoidance, near-zero in punishment), but discrimination between avoidance and extinction required distinct external stimuli. Adding unavoidable “free” shocks during extinction elevated response rates in both components.

title: SCHEDULES OF CONDITIONED REINFORCEMENT DURINGEXPERIMENTAL EXTINCTION
process: Conditioned reinforcement and schedule control of operant behavior during extinction.
abstract: Kelleher (1960) examined the effects of conditioned reinforcement during extinction by using the sound of a food magazine as a reinforcer for pigeons previously trained on an FI 5-minute food schedule. During extinction, food was withheld but the magazine sound was delivered on differential reinforcement of pausing (DRP), fixed interval (FI), or fixed ratio (FR) schedules. Distinct response patterns emerged under each condition: low rates under DRP, scalloped FI patterns under FI schedules, and high steady rates under FR schedules—demonstrating that the magazine sound functioned as a conditioned reinforcer capable of maintaining schedule-typical behavior in the absence of primary reinforcement.

title: RAPID DEVELOPMENT OF MULTIPLE-SCHEDULE PERFORMANCESWITH RETARDED CHILDREN
process: Acquisition of discriminated operant behavior and multiple-schedule performance through shaping and reinforcement
abstract: Bijou and Orlando (1960) outlined an efficient method for establishing multiple-schedule performance in institutionalized developmentally retarded children. The study described a systematic shaping procedure—using brief, simple instructions and edible reinforcers—to transition children from initial lever-pressing behavior to discriminated responding under alternating reinforcement and extinction components. The technique allowed subjects to progress at their own pace and demonstrated that complex schedule control (e.g., mult FR 25 ext, mult FI 1 ext, mult CRF VI 0.5) could be achieved within a few sessions rather than weeks, offering a rapid approach for studying discrimination and individual variability in human operant conditioning.

title: A VOIDANCE LEARNING IN DOGS WITHOUT AWARNING STIMULUS'
process: avoidance learning and maintenance; effects of punishment and psychostimulant
abstract: Black and Morse (1960) demonstrated that dogs can acquire and maintain avoidance responding in the absence of a warning stimulus using a shuttle-box jumping operant. Acquisition was highly sensitive to the temporal parameters of shock delivery: dogs learned rapidly at short shock–shock (S–S) intervals (0–4 s) but failed to acquire avoidance at longer intervals (15 s). Once established, the behavior was stable and maintained with minimal shock. Subsequent manipulations showed that punishment initially increased responding before producing suppression, with the duration of this effect depending on prior training. Amphetamine produced dose-dependent rate increases and disrupted punishment discrimination at high doses.

title: THE CONTROL OF THE CONTENT OF CONVERSATIONTHROUGH REINFORCEMENT
authors: Azrin, N.H.; Holz, W.; Ulrich, R.; Goldiamond, I.
abstract: Azrin, Holz, Ulrich, and Goldiamond (1960) attempted to replicate Verplanck’s (1955) finding that conversation content could be modified by reinforcement—specifically, that agreement with opinions increases their frequency. Across several classroom replications with graduate and undergraduate students, the authors found apparent effects of reinforcement, but also major procedural inconsistencies, poor reliability in identifying “opinions,” and even fabricated data. A final replication by trained psychologists failed entirely: when reinforcement (agreement/disagreement) was systematically limited, subjects quickly terminated the conversation. The study highlights methodological challenges in extending operant conditioning to uncontrolled verbal behavior and stresses the necessity of objective recording and response definition.

title: THE SHAPE OF SOME WA VELENGTH GENERA LIZA TION GRA DIEN T
abstract: Blough (1961) examined the shape and shifts of wavelength generalization gradients in pigeons trained to peck a key illuminated by specific monochromatic lights (530, 550, or 570 mµ). Following reinforcement at one wavelength and testing in extinction across a range (490–610 mµ), pigeons produced reproducible, wavelength-centered gradients that shifted appropriately when training wavelength changed. Gradient forms varied across birds and wavelengths—some symmetrical, others asymmetrical—suggesting spectral regions where generalization occurs more readily (e.g., around 570 mµ). The findings challenge assumptions linking gradient steepness with discriminability and indicate that generalization patterns may reveal aspects of avian color perception.

title: AMPLITUDE-INDUCTION GRADIENT OF A SMALL HUMAN OPERANTIN A N ESCA PE-A VOIDA NCE SITUA TION
abstract: Hefferline and Keenan (1960) analyzed the amplitude-induction gradient of a small electromyographically recorded thumb-contraction operant in a human escape–avoidance setting. The subject, unaware of the specific response, learned to terminate or postpone an aversive noise by producing microvolt-level muscle contractions. Reinforcement of a specific amplitude range induced increased responding in adjacent amplitude subclasses, forming an induction gradient analogous to a stimulus generalization gradient. Larger amplitudes appeared briefly then declined, while responses near the reinforced amplitude persisted until extinction. The findings demonstrate fine-grained response differentiation and operant induction in human microbehavior.

title: PUNISHMENT OF SA RESPONDING IN MATCHING TO SAMPLEBY TIME OUT FROM POSITIVE REINFORCEMENT'"
process: Punishment by time out from positive reinforcement
abstract: Ferster and Appel (1961) examined how time out (TO) from positive reinforcement functions as punishment for incorrect (SΔ) responses in a pigeon matching-to-sample task. Two pigeons were trained under continuous (CRF) and variable-interval (VI 3-min) reinforcement schedules while TO durations ranged from 0.5 to 600 seconds. Under CRF, long TOs (up to 10 minutes) produced little suppression, but under VI 3-min schedules, moderate TO durations (10–60 s) maximally increased matching accuracy by selectively suppressing SΔ responding without broadly reducing overall response rate. Very long TOs (≥120 s) disrupted performance entirely. The results show that the punishing effectiveness of time out depends on both its duration and the reinforcement schedule maintaining the behavior.

title: BEHA VIORAL CONTRAST
abstract: Reynolds (1961) investigated behavioral contrast—the change in response rate to one stimulus when the reinforcement schedule associated with another stimulus is altered. Pigeons responded on a multiple schedule in which key color (e.g., red vs. green) signaled different reinforcement contingencies. When reinforcement during one component was replaced by extinction or time-out, response rates during the unchanged component increased (positive contrast). However, when non-responding during the alternate component was itself reinforced (DRO), the contrast effect disappeared, showing that contrast depends on relative reinforcement frequency rather than response suppression alone. The findings supported a relativistic interpretation of contrast: the rate of responding in a given condition depends on the relative frequency of reinforcement across all concurrently alternating conditions.

title: CHARACTERISTICS OF RESPONDING UNDER A TEMPORALLY DEFINEDREINFORCEMENT SCHEDULE OF LONG CYCLE LENGTH
process: Temporal discrimination and response pacing under a time-correlated reinforcement schedule
abstract:
Cumming and Schoenfeld (1960) examined pigeon key-pecking under a time-correlated reinforcement schedule with a long total cycle (30 minutes). Only the first response in the “reinforcement-eligible” period (

Distributions of post-reinforcement pauses indicated distinct transition points between early and late responding, highlighting temporal discrimination and periodic organization across long cycles.

title: CONDITIONING OF MENTAL-HOSPITAL PATIENTS TO FIXED-RATIOSCHEDULES OF REINFORCEMENT'
abstract: Hutchinson and Azrin (1960) studied the acquisition and maintenance of operant responding under fixed-ratio (FR) reinforcement schedules in five long-term hospitalized patients diagnosed with schizophrenia. Using cigarettes or candy as reinforcers, patients pulled a manipulandum to earn rewards under progressively increasing FR requirements. Response rates began extremely low (≈5/min) but gradually increased over sessions, reaching >100/min at higher ratios (up to FR 300). Performances showed the characteristic pause-run pattern typical of FR schedules, with pauses localized after reinforcement. When ratio requirements were increased too rapidly (“ratio strain”), performance temporarily deteriorated but recovered with lower ratios. Extinction produced alternating bouts of high-rate responding and pausing until responding ceased. Conditioned reinforcers (light + buzzer) reinstated responding after primary reinforcement was withdrawn. Despite psychiatric diagnosis, the behavioral patterns closely resembled those of nonclinical human and animal subjects.

title: SOME TEMPORAL AND SEQUENTIAL PROPERTIES OF BEHA VIORDURING CONDITIONING AND EXTINCTION
process: ["Extinction effects on temporal structure and sequencing of an operant chain"
abstract: "A 10-year-old horse was conditioned to nudge a lever with its snout to receive small grain reinforcers. After shaping under continuous reinforcement, the animal was exposed to fixed-ratio (FR 3–11) and fixed-interval (FI 1–3 min) schedules. The horse qui

title: AN ANALYSIS OF INTERACTIONS IN A MULTIPLE SCHEDULE
abstract: Reynolds (1961) analyzed how components of two-stimulus multiple schedules interact in pigeons. In daily 3-h sessions (30 cycles of 3-min components), birds experienced sequences combining VI 3-min or VR 150:1 with EXT. Rates in a component held on reinforcement increased when the alternated component switched to EXT (behavioral contrast) and decreased when it switched to reinforcement. In an EXT component, switching the other component to EXT reduced responding (negative induction) whereas switching it to reinforcement produced a transient increase (positive induction). Extinction in one component was slower when alternated with VI (but not VR), consistent with VI-generated pausing functioning as a discriminative stimulus. During discrimination formation after EXT-EXT, induced responding depended on prior history: reversal (VI→EXT) > formation (VI→VI) > re-formation (EXT→VI).

title: VISUAL EXPLORATION AS REINFORCEMENT OF CONDITIONEDBAR-PRESSING RESPONSES OF MONKEYS
abstract: "Moon (1960) demonstrated that visual exploration—allowing monkeys to view their surroundings through a briefly opened window—can serve as an effective reinforcer for bar-pressing behavior. Thirteen rhesus monkeys were tested under FR, VI, and DRL schedules. The animals maintained high and stable response rates without the need for food or water deprivation, and exhibited minimal satiation across sessions. When permitted to control the window duration, monkeys spent roughly 50–60% of the session exploring visually. The study proposed visual exploration as a practical and ethologically relevant reinforcer, especially useful in psychopharmacological contexts."]

title: A MO UNT OF REINFORCEMENT A ND FREE-OPERA NT RESPONDING
abstract: Keesey and Kling (1961) investigated how varying the amount of food reinforcement affects free-operant responding in pigeons. Across variable-interval schedules, pigeons were exposed to different food quantities (¼, 1, 2, 3, and 4 peas) each paired with a unique key color. Once stable performance was achieved, response rates did not systematically differ by reinforcement amount. However, transient differences appeared early in sessions, during extinction, or immediately following procedural changes, suggesting that response rate sensitivity to reinforcement amount emerges when steady-state behavior is disrupted. The authors proposed that extensive exposure leads to a generalized conditioned reinforcement value that overrides differences in amount.

title: RESISTANCE-TO-EXTINCTION FUNCTIONS IN THE SINGLE ORGANISM
process: Operant extinction and resistance to extinction
abstract: Hearst (1961) measured resistance to extinction within individual pigeons by pairing four key colors with different reinforcement histories, then testing all colors in extinction. In Experiment I, total extinction responses and response probability increased (negatively accelerated at the group level) with the number of prior reinforcements, though individuals showed idiosyncratic gradients. In Experiment II, the extinction responding was an increasing function of the conditioning variable ratio (VR 1, 4, 10), reproducing the partial-reinforcement effect within subjects. The method yields both individual and group resistance-to-extinction functions without separate groups.

title: STIMULUS GENERALIZATION IN AN AVOIDANCE SITUATION
process: Stimulus generalization and discrimination in free-operant (Sidman) avoidance.
abstract: Sidman measured stimulus generalization of free-operant avoidance in two rhesus monkeys. After discrimination training (avoidance with 2 Hz clicks vs. extinction with 6 Hz), he probed a series of click rates (≈2–6 Hz). Generalization gradients (percent of total responding per stimulus) were orderly and usually centered near the training S+; switching the “anchor” stimulus and prolonged testing could sharpen the gradient. Manipulating response–shock intervals and shock duration scaled overall response rates but left the gradient’s shape largely unchanged. A “generalization surface” showed a roughly linear relation between per-stimulus rate and session-wide rate, with slopes decreasing as stimuli diverged from S+.

title: OPERANT CONTROL OF VOCALIZING IN THE CHICKEN
abstract: Lane demonstrated operant control of vocal behavior in Bantam chicks. Chirping was reinforced under fixed-ratio, fixed-interval, differential-reinforcement-of-other-behavior (DRO), and multiple schedules. Rates of vocalizing conformed to standard operant patterns (e.g., post-reinforcement pauses, scalloping, extinction decline). Discriminative control developed under multiple FI–EXT schedules, and differential responding persisted after reversal. Concurrent and conjunctive schedules involving chirping and pecking revealed both functional independence and interactions between the two operants. Overall, the study confirmed that vocalization in chickens can be governed by contingencies of reinforcement like other operant responses.

title: RELATIVITY OF RESPONSE RATE AND REINFORCEMENTFREQUENCY IN A MULTIPLE SCHEDULE
process: ["Behavioral contrast and relative rate of responding as a function of relative reinforcement frequency (precursor to the Matching Law)"
abstract: Reynolds examined how pigeons’ response rates in one component of a multiple schedule depend on the relative frequency of reinforcement across components. Using VI–FR multiple schedules, he showed that as the relative reinforcement frequency for a constant component increased, the response rate in that component rose proportionally—even when its absolute reinforcement rate was unchanged. Across both VI–FR and FR–VI arrangements, the relative response rate closely approximated a linear function of the relative reinforcement frequency, with slope ≈ 0.8 and intercept ≈ 0.13. The study refined the concept of behavioral contrast as part of a broader law of relative reinforcement.

title: SOME OBSERVATIONS CONCERNING PREPOTENCY AND PROBABILITY OFPOSTPONING SHOCK WITH A TWO-LEVER AVOIDANCE PROCEDURE
process: Free-operant avoidance with concurrent options; choice/response allocation under aversive control.
abstract: Verhave used a two-lever free-operant avoidance setup in rats to study how response allocation (“prepotency”) depends on the probability that a lever press postpones shock (P). With both levers equally effective (PA = PB = 100%), animals often developed dominance on one lever but also exhibited abrupt within- and between-session switching. When probabilities were made unequal (e.g., PA < PB), responding shifted—sometimes gradually, sometimes abruptly—toward the lever with the higher P. After stabilization, rats tended to “play it safe,” responding almost exclusively on the higher-probability lever; the ratio of lever rates did not track the ratio of P values.

title: SEPARATION OF TWO- CHANNEL RECORDINGS FORVOCAL BEHAVIOR STUDIES
process: Technological facilitation of verbal behavior measurement
abstract: Starkweather and Langsley described a two-channel switching system designed to eliminate crosstalk in simultaneous recordings of two people’s voices during social or clinical interactions. Using voice-activated keys (modified Heathkit VX-1 circuits) that alternately silence the opposite channel through diode-modulated relay circuits, each person’s voice could be recorded cleanly without interference from the other. The apparatus permitted later playback for automatic voice-duration and rate analyses and could generate clean separated tapes suitable for linguistic or behavioral research.

title: A METHOD FOR THE ANALYSIS AND CONTROL OFSPEECH RATE'
authors: Shearn, D.; Sprague, R.; Rosenzweig, S.
process: Operant conditioning of verbal response rate
abstract: Shearn, Sprague, and Rosenzweig (1961) developed an operant method for quantifying and controlling speech rate using an electronic voice-control manipulandum (a modified Heathkit VX-1 voice key). College students produced vocal responses reinforced on fixed-ratio (FR 30) schedules, while a psychiatric patient received contingent reinforcement on differential-reinforcement-of-low-rate (DRL) schedules. The apparatus transformed speech bursts into discrete measurable responses, allowing automated contingency control. Under FR schedules, speech rate increased and showed extinction patterns; under DRL schedules, the patient’s speech rate progressively decreased as interval requirements lengthened, demonstrating operant control of verbal response pacing.

title: A TTENTION IN THE PIGEON
abstract: Reynolds (1961) studied selective attention in pigeons using compound visual stimuli. In Experiment 1, pecks were reinforced to a triangle-on-red and extinguished to a circle-on-green; when elements were tested separately, one pigeon responded to shape (triangle) while the other responded to color (red), showing control by only one component of the compound. In Experiment 2, a side-lamp signaled which element would be reinforced (color vs. shape). Despite wavelength changes, behavior tracked the presence/intensity of the side-lamp rather than its hue, indicating stimulus control by intensity and not the nominal signaling dimension. Overall, the work demonstrates that pigeons may attend to a single effective aspect of a compound discriminative stimulus.

title: THE USE OF A BEHA VIORA L STIMULANT INTHE STUDY OF STIMULUS GENERALIZATION
abstract: Hanson and Guttman (1961) examined the effects of the behavioral stimulant pipradrol on stimulus generalization gradients in pigeons trained to peck a key illuminated at 550 nm. After VI training, birds received extinction generalization tests with wavelengths ranging from 490–640 nm. Experimental pigeons received 8 mg/kg pipradrol prior to testing, controls received saline. The stimulant markedly reduced extinction (sustaining response rate across tests) but did not alter the shape or spread of generalization gradients. Thus, pipradrol increased general activity while leaving the underlying stimulus control by wavelength unchanged.

title: SEPTAL STIMULATION AS A REINFORCER OF AND AN ALTERNATIVETO CONSUMA TORY BEHA VIOR
process: Intracranial self-stimulation as operant reinforcement and its interaction/competition with consummatory (drinking) behavior.
abstract: Falk (1961) investigated whether septal-area intracranial stimulation (ICS) could serve as a reinforcer for or alternative to water consumption in rats. Two female rats with electrodes in the medial septal nucleus were tested under various conditions combining lever-press–elicited ICS and drinkometer-activated water access. While ICS effectively reinforced lever pressing, it did not increase water intake when made contingent on licking. At higher stimulation voltages, rats even chose ICS over drinking despite prolonged water deprivation, resuming drinking only after severe dehydration. Thus, septal stimulation acted as a potent competing reinforcer rather than one that potentiated consummatory behavior.

title: SPACED RESPONDING IN RATS AS A FUNCTION OF SOME TEMPORAL VARIABLES
process: Schedule control of temporal spacing (DRL timing/temporal discrimination) via reinforcement-rate consequences.
abstract: Zimmerman compared two discrete-trial DRL arrangements that differed in how response timing affected reinforcement rate. In the recycling DRL, each response (reinforced or not) produced a 6-s SΔ and immediately started a new trial, so tighter spacing after reinforcement setup could raise reinforcement rate. In the fixed-trial DRL, the trial length was fixed; spacing after setup could not change reinforcement rate. With DRL 18-s LH 12 and DRL 6-s LH 24, recycling yielded shorter, more peaked SD→response (SD-R) distributions than fixed-trial; with DRL 6-s LH 2 (short limited hold), the schedules produced similar timing. Conclusion: reinforcement-rate consequences strongly shape spaced responding, though an additional factor promotes clustering near the first reinforced bin even when rate consequences are removed.

title: DISCRIMINATIVE PROPERTIES OF PUNISHMENT
process: Discriminative control (stimulus control) via punishment contingencies; interaction between aversive and discriminative functions of stimuli.
abstract: Holz and Azrin (1961) examined how punishment can acquire discriminative control over responding in pigeons. Pigeons pecked under variable-interval reinforcement schedules in which electric shock (punishment) was paired either with reinforcement or with extinction. When shock was consistently paired with reinforcement, punishment increased responding—even in extinction. Conversely, when a mild, nonaversive shock was paired with extinction, its later presentation decreased responding. Thus, punishment’s effect depended on its differential association with reinforcement or extinction, revealing that punishment can function as a discriminative stimulus rather than solely as an aversive consequence.

title: A N OPERA NT-DISCRIMINA TION A PPA RA TUS FOR INFANTS
process: ["Operant discrimination and reinforcement learning in human infants."
abstract: Simmons and Lipsitt (1961) designed an automated operant discrimination apparatus for infants, enabling the study of learning and sensory discrimination before the onset of speech. Infants interacted with two manipulanda (panels) that could be lit in different colors and accompanied by auditory reinforcement (e.g., chimes). By alternately pairing one panel with reinforcement and the other without, the authors demonstrated that 10- to 12-month-old infants could learn simple color–sound discriminations and adjust responding when contingencies were reversed. The apparatus allows quantitative analysis of early operant and discriminative behavior.

title: GENERALIZATION OF AUDITORY INTENSITY AS A FUNCTION OFAMOUNT OF DISCRIMINATION TRAINING
process: Stimulus generalization and discrimination learning (operant, auditory intensity).
abstract: "Sherman & Pierrel (1961) trained rats on an auditory intensity discrimination (SD vs. a louder SA) for different durations (6, 13, 27 days), then tested generalization across multiple attenuated intensities. Longer prior discrimination produced sharper, smoother generalization gradients with lower variability; gradients steepened further with continued multi-SA training, approaching asymptote within ~10 days.

title: INFLUENCE OF REINFORCEMENT SCHEDULES ON RESPONSE DURATION'
process: ["Operant temporal topography (response duration) under schedule control; schedule effects (FI
and probability-based contingencies) on response variability."
abstract: Millenson, Hurwitz, & Nixon (1961) examined how reinforcement schedules shape lever-press response duration in rats. Compared to CRF, fixed-interval (FI) schedules produced longer, more variable, positively skewed duration distributions; extended exposure to FI yielded modest, inconsistent trends. Under contingencies approximating uniform reinforcement probability (T = proportion of time reinforcement available), both median duration and dispersion peaked around T ≈ 0.11. With FR 5, durations were short with narrow dispersion and asymmetric distributions, and showed no consistent within-ratio (1st–5th press) differences.

title: SOME BEHAVIORAL EFFECTS OF A CONCURRENTLY POSITIVEAND NEGATIVE STIMULUS
abstract: Hearst & Sidman studied rats pressing one lever that produced both food (VI) and shock (FR)—a concurrently positive–negative (reward–punishment) situation—with a second “escape” lever that switched to a neutral timeout (no food, no shock). About half the rats learned reliable escape only when both reward and punishment co-occurred; removing either contingency greatly reduced escape. Others instead adopted low, steady rates on the food/shock lever and rarely escaped. In a variant where escape also reset the shock counter, escape responding increased sharply. Results suggest it’s the combination of appetitive and aversive consequences—not either alone—that drives escape from the discriminative context.

title: SIMPLE TRANSDUCERS TO DETECT OR RECORD OPERANT AMPLITUDE'
authors: Heferline, R.F.; Birch, J.D.; Gentry, T.
abstract: "Bijou and Orlando (1960) outlined an efficient method for establishing multiple-schedule performance in institutionalized developmentally retarded children. The study described a systematic shaping procedure—using brief, simple instructions and edible re

title: BEHAVIORAL INTERACTION UNDER CONCURRENT SPACED-RESPONDING,VARIABLE-INTER VAL SCHEDULES OF REINFORCEMENT
process: Schedule interaction and induction between timing (DRL) and variable-interval responding, emphasizing temporal discrimination and mediation.
abstract: "Azrin, Holz, Ulrich, and Goldiamond (1960) attempted to replicate Verplanck’s (1955) finding that conversation content could be modified by reinforcement—specifically, that agreement with opinions increases their frequency. Across several classroom repli

title: RELATIVE AND ABSOLUTE. STRENGTH OF RESPONSE AS A FUNCTIONOF FREQUENCY OF REINFORCEMENT
process: Concurrent schedule performance — matching law and response strength as functions of reinforcement frequency.
abstract: "Bijou and Orlando (1960) outlined an efficient method for establishing multiple-schedule performance in institutionalized developmentally retarded children. The study described a systematic shaping procedure—using brief, simple instructions and edible re

title: A WIDE-RANGE, CONSTANT-CURRENT SHOCK STIMULATOR
process: Apparatus/methods for aversive stimulation—constant-current electric shock delivery (hardware note; not a behavioral process per se)
abstract: "Bijou and Orlando (1960) outlined an efficient method for establishing multiple-schedule performance in institutionalized developmentally retarded children. The study described a systematic shaping procedure—using brief, simple instructions and edible re

title: FREE-OPERANT A VOIDANCE CONDITIONING IN HUMAN SUBJECTS
process: Free-operant (Sidman) avoidance conditioning — maintenance of behavior by postponement of an aversive event without external warning stimuli.
abstract: "Bijou and Orlando (1960) outlined an efficient method for establishing multiple-schedule performance in institutionalized developmentally retarded children. The study described a systematic shaping procedure—using brief, simple instructions and edible re

title: DISCRETE-TRIALS DRL
abstract: "Logan (1961) examined discrete-trials differential reinforcement of low rate (DRL) schedules to compare bar-pressing performance in an operant chamber with alley-running behavior under negatively correlated reinforcement. Four rats were trained to delay bar pressing following a light signal to receive food. The procedure produced unimodal response-time distributions similar to alley performance, with few short latencies but more excessively long ones. The study demonstrated that discrete-trials DRL eliminated rapid responses common in free-operant DRL and suggested that spatial orientation at trial onset influences timing precision."]

title: SOME DATA ON MATCHING BEHAVIOR IN THE PIGEON
process: Conditional discrimination (matching-to-sample behavior) and stimulus control acquisition.
abstract: Cumming and Berryman (1961) studied color matching behavior in pigeons using a three-key apparatus. Each trial began with illumination of a center key (red, green, or blue) followed by two comparison keys—one matching and one nonmatching in hue. A peck on the matching key produced reinforcement, while the nonmatching choice produced a blackout. Pigeons rapidly acquired accurate matching performance after several sessions, progressing from position-based responding to color-controlled matching. When a novel yellow stimulus was introduced, pigeons reverted to position preferences, indicating the absence of generalized “matching-to-sample” concept formation.

title: SOME EFFECTS OF VARYING THE DURATION OF AN EXTEROCEPTIVEWARNING STIMULUS ON INTERCURRENT AVOIDANCE ANDA PPETITIVE RESPONSES
process: Avoidance conditioning — specifically, reconditioning effects and stimulus generalization involving variations in the conditioned stimulus duration.
abstract: Halasz (1961) investigated how changes in the duration of a conditioned warning stimulus (CS) affect intercurrent avoidance and appetitive behavior in a cat trained to both press a lever for milk and deflect a pendulum to avoid shock. After stable avoidance and food-maintained responding were established, the CS was shortened and terminated by the experimenter, weakening avoidance. When the original long CS duration was restored (without shock), the avoidance response reappeared strongly, producing renewed after-suppression of appetitive responding. The findings suggest second-order reconditioning of avoidance via CS duration and highlight stimulus generalization and induction processes in avoidance maintenance.

title: CONTRAST, GENERALIZATION, AND THE PROCESSOF DISCRIMINATION
process: Stimulus control & generalization under discrimination training; behavioral contrast; peak-shift phenomena.
abstract: "Blough (1961) examined the shape and shifts of wavelength generalization gradients in pigeons trained to peck a key illuminated by specific monochromatic lights (530, 550, or 570 mµ). Following reinforcement at one wavelength and testing in extinction ac

title: EXTINCTION OF BAR PRESSING FOLLOWING TRAININGUNDER A MULTIPLE VI VI SCHEDULE
process: Multiple-schedule performance and extinction dynamics (differential response persistence across variable-interval components).
abstract: Four albino rats were trained to press a bar under a multiple VI 15 s / VI 75 s schedule with alternating noise stimuli. Two rats were later extinguished under identical stimulus durations, and two under equalized durations. For the “same” (S) group, response rates remained higher under the VI 15 s condition, showing an accentuated rate ratio during extinction. For the “different” (D) group, equalized stimulus durations produced either no change or a slight decrease in rate ratio. Results suggest that interaction effects between VI components contribute to differential extinction patterns beyond simple duration effects.

title: A SIMPLE NOISE SOURCE
abstract: This paper describes a simple electronic circuit for generating audio noise with random spectral characteristics. The design uses only four components and produces output with approximately equal energy per octave across the audio spectrum. The circuit can function either as a low-level noise source for microphone preamplifiers or, with an added amplifier stage, as a higher-output source suitable for driving audio amplifier inputs.

title: THE DIFFERENTIATION OF HUMAN TIME ESTIMATIONS'
authors: Taber, J.I.; Homme, L.E.; Csanyi, A.
process: Temporal differentiation (operant timing / differential reinforcement of response duration).
abstract: This study examined how humans learn to estimate time intervals through differential reinforcement of a “holding” response, analogous to operant timing in animals. Using a mechanical ergograph and feedback lights, subjects learned to hold a lever for a specified interval (e.g., 1 second) to receive reinforcement. In Experiment I, untrained human estimations became highly precise after differential reinforcement, with variability reduced sixfold. In Experiment II, time intervals ranging from 0.5 to 5 seconds were tested, showing that relative variability decreased as the interval length increased, approximating Weber’s law. The findings suggest that human timing behavior, though initially variable, can be systematically shaped through reinforcement.

title: THE INFLUENCE OF REPETITION OF INCORRECTLY ANSWEREDITEMS IN A TEACHING-MACHINE PROGRAM1
process: Programmed instruction / differential reinforcement via immediate feedback and required correct responding (error correction)
abstract: Graduate students worked through a programmed instruction course either with item-by-item review (missed items re-presented until correct) or without review (each item seen once). On three immediate tests, the review group made fewer errors at all difficulty levels (≈4–13 percentage-point advantage). On an unannounced retest 6 months later, both groups showed forgetting, larger for harder items, but the review group remained superior. Review cost ~1.3 extra minutes per 29-item set (~1.3 hours total across 60 sets). Authors argue repetition of missed items materially improves acquisition and long-term retention when error rates are nontrivial.

title: DISCRIMINATED BAR-PRESS AVOIDANCE
authors: Hoffman, H.S.; Fleshler, M.; Chorny, H.
abstract: Rats learned signaled (tone) bar-press avoidance of shock. Over ~50 sessions, some reached ≥90% avoidance, while others persistently showed within-session “warm-up” (poor early, good late performance). Follow-ups varied shock intensity, removed the warning tone for initial trials, and blocked bar access during unsignaled shocks. Raising shock intensity didn’t fix deficits; eliminating the tone or preventing the response still abolished warm-up. Authors conclude warm-up reflects a shock-induced motivational/emotional process rather than sensitivity to shock, relearning of tone–shock, or response execution per se.

title: RESPONSE DURATION IN OPERANT LEVEL,REGULAR REIANFORCEMENT, AND EXTINCTION
process: Operant conditioning with continuous reinforcement and subsequent extinction
abstract: Rats’ bar-press duration was measured across operant level (baseline), continuous reinforcement, and extinction. Mean duration was long at baseline, shortened and stabilized under regular reinforcement (more short presses; narrower range), then lengthened again through extinction (with within-session increases and spontaneous recovery). Duration distributions late in conditioning resembled early-extinction distributions; late-extinction resembled operant level. More conditioning reinforcers produced a higher fraction of short-duration presses in both conditioning and extinction, and greater resistance to extinction.

title: THE DEVELOPMENT OF AN INSTRUMENTAL SKILL SEQUENCEIN THE CHIMPANZEE
process: Acquisition and maintenance of a discrimination-based instrumental chain under fixed-ratio reinforcement
abstract: A chimpanzee was trained to press one of three levers corresponding to the “odd” visual stimulus (circle, triangle, or square). Over repeated sessions, accuracy increased from chance to ~99%. Once stable, the schedule was shifted from continuous reinforcement (CRF) to fixed ratio (FR), progressing up to FR-19. Both discrimination accuracy and response rate increased with higher ratios. The chimp eventually appeared to perform the 18-set discrimination sequence as a learned serial chain, suggesting acquisition of a complex instrumental skill.

title: TIMING BEHAVIOR DURING PROLONGED TREATMENT WITH dl-AMPHETAMINE
abstract: Rats trained on a differential reinforcement of low rates (DRL 17.5 s) schedule received chronic intraperitoneal injections of dl-amphetamine (0.75–1.5 mg/kg). Initially, amphetamine produced typical stimulant effects—shortened interresponse times (IRTs), increased response rates, and reduced reinforcement. Over repeated sessions, however, performance gradually returned toward baseline despite continued drug exposure, indicating behavioral tolerance specific to the operant task. In contrast, general locomotor activity remained elevated throughout, suggesting differential tolerance across behavioral systems.

title: BEHAVIORAL CONTRAST IN A MULTIPLE AND CONCURRENTSCHEDULE OF REINFORCEMENT
abstract: Pigeons pecked two keys under combined multiple–concurrent schedules. When a VI component (green) on one “multiple” key alternated with extinction (yellow), response rate in the VI component increased—behavioral contrast. Crucially, if the overall reinforcement rate during the extinction components was restored (by arranging reinforcement on the other, concurrent key while the multiple key was in extinction), the contrast vanished. Across manipulations, the magnitude of contrast scaled with the relative drop in reinforcement rate during extinction. Local performance analyses showed higher overall VI rates emerged largely from shorter cycles (reduced changeover and red-key run times), with only minor changes in within-run rates on the VI key.

title: PUNISHMENT DURING FIXED-INTERVAL REINFORCEMENT
abstract: Two pigeons pecked under FI 5-min food reinforcement while every response was punished with brief tail shock (30–180 V). Punishment always reduced overall responding; mild intensities showed partial recovery across days, stronger intensities produced sustained suppression. The within-interval pattern (low post-reinforcement rate rising to a high terminal rate) was proportionally depressed across the entire FI, leaving the temporal pattern essentially unchanged (confirmed via Quarter-life and Curvature indices). During extinction with punishment still present, higher shock intensities yielded fewer responses and much faster cessation.

title: COLLATERAL BEHAVIOR IN HUMANS'
process: Collateral behavior and conditioned reinforcement processes emerging under DRL schedules
abstract: Four high school students pressed telegraph keys under a DRL (Differential Reinforcement of Low Rates) schedule, where reinforcement occurred only if the interval between presses on one designated key was between 8.2 s and 10.25 s. The other three keys had no programmed consequence but allowed observation of collateral behavior. Each participant developed stable, idiosyncratic sequences of presses on the nonreinforced keys that filled the waiting interval before reinforcement. During extinction, these organized chains disappeared. Subjects often believed reinforcement depended on particular key sequences rather than timing.

title: EFFECTS OF CHRONICALLY ADMINISTERED CHLORPROMAZINEON MULTIPLE-SCHEDULE PERFORMANCE1
process: Drug effects on discriminative control under multiple schedules
abstract: Two beagle dogs were trained on multiple schedules (FI 3-min, FR 50, and SΔ 25-min components) and given chronic daily oral doses of chlorpromazine in sustained-release (spansule) form (1–24 mg/kg). Low doses increased responding in both interval and ratio components, suggesting reduced stimulus control and increased induction between components. Higher doses produced rate depression, erratic responding, or intermittent bursts, yet substantial behavior persisted even at 24 mg/kg. Subsequent experiments showed that removing the ratio component reduced induction and clarified that high-dose responding was partly due to cross-component control. Chronic dosing produced tolerance to chlorpromazine, and abrupt withdrawal led to temporary behavioral disruptions resembling a withdrawal syndrome.

title: SALIVARY CONDITIONING IN DOGS DURING FIXED-INTERVALREINFORCEMENT CONTINGENT UPON LEVER PRESSING
process: Interaction between operant and respondent conditioning—specifically, temporal (fixed-interval) respondent conditioning embedded within an operant contingency.
abstract: Four dogs were trained to press a lever for food under an FI 2-minute schedule while salivary responses were continuously recorded via implanted parotid duct tubing. Respondent salivation increased prior to food delivery, showing a temporal pattern similar to the operant lever-pressing curve (low immediately post-reinforcement, rising as reinforcement time approached). Salivary conditioning extinguished when reinforcement ceased and covaried positively with operant rate. A negative correlation between conditioned (CR) and unconditioned (UR) salivation suggested reciprocal modulation between anticipatory and consummatory responding.

title: IMPAIRMENT OF PERFORMANCE W7HEN A DISCRIMINATIVESTIMULUS IS CORRELATED WVITH A REINFORCEMENT CONTINGENCY
process: Discriminative control interacting with temporal schedule performance
abstract:
Five pigeons were trained on temporally defined reinforcement schedules in which the duration of the reinforcement-available period (
𝑡
𝐷
t
D
	​

) was systematically shortened within a 90-second cycle. When 
𝑡
𝐷
t
D
	​

 was paired with a discriminative stimulus (Sᴰ), strong stimulus control developed at long durations but led to extinction when 
𝑡
𝐷
t
D
	​

 was reduced below 1 second. Without the discriminative cue, pigeons maintained responding even at 0.5-second 
𝑡
𝐷
t
D
	​

, showing ratio-like performance. When discriminative control was weak, high response rates persisted despite brief Sᴰ durations, resembling fixed-interval and fixed-ratio performance. Thus, excessive discriminative control under brief Sᴰ durations impaired adaptive responding.

title: STIMULUS FACTORS IN A VERSIVE CONTROLS:THE GENERALIZATION OF CONDITIONED SUPPRESSION
process: Conditioned suppression (CER) and stimulus generalization under aversive control; modulation by motivational operations.
abstract:
White Carneaux pigeons key-pecked for food on VI 2-min while 48-s tones (300–3400 Hz, 80 dB SPL) periodically occurred; the last 8 s of a 1000-Hz tone co-terminated with response-independent shock during training. After stable, complete suppression to the CS, shock was removed and generalization tests (varied tone frequencies) were run. Initial suppression generalized broadly and then sharpened systematically across sessions as suppression extinguished for nontraining tones, with a slight bias toward lower frequencies. A second experiment showed deprivation effects: at 70% body weight (higher motivation), gradients were very sharp (little generalization); after raising to 80%, gradients broadened. Thus, conditioned suppression generalizes and then narrows in extinction, and the breadth of generalization is inversely related to motivation for the ongoing appetitive behavior."Five pigeons were trained on temporally defined reinforcement schedules in which the duration of the reinforcement-available period (

title: THE INTERACTING EFFECTS OF DEPRIVATIONAND REIANFORCEMENT SCHEDULE
process: ["Conditioned suppression (aversive Pavlovian control over operant responding) and its stimulus generalization/gradient sharpening during extinction; modulation by motivation (deprivation) of ongoing operant strength."
abstract: Pigeons on VI food schedules received 48-s tones ending with 8-s shock. After complete suppression to the training tone, nonshocked generalization tests showed initially broad suppression that narrowed across extinction, with slightly greater generalization to lower frequencies. A second study varied deprivation: stronger motivation (lower body weight) yielded sharper, less-generalized suppression gradients.

title: RATE-CHANGE EFFECTS DURING A PRE-SCHEDULE-CHANGE STIMULUS
process: Behavioral contrast and anticipatory rate modulation under multiple variable-interval schedules with discriminative (pre-schedule-change) stimuli.
abstract: Four pigeons were trained on a multiple VI 1 min / VI 10 min schedule where a 3-minute warning light preceded each schedule change. Response rate consistently decreased during the warning that preceded transition to the richer (VI 1) component and increased during the warning that preceded transition to the leaner (VI 10) component. These rate-change effects were interpreted in terms of reinforcement properties of schedule transitions, schedule contrast, or emotional/motivational processes.

title: BEHA VIORAL CONTRAST WITH FIXED-INTER VAL ANDLOW-RATE REINFORCEMENT'
abstract: Two experiments demonstrated behavioral contrast under fixed-interval (FI) and differential-reinforcement-of-low-rate (DRL) schedules. Pigeons showed higher response rates on FI and DRL components when alternated with extinction than when alternated with reinforcement. On FI, contrast effects were strongest early in the interval; on DRL, short interresponse times (IRTs) increased most. The findings indicate that behavioral contrast does not depend solely on differential reinforcement or punishment of specific IRTs.

title: A VOIDANCE CONDITIONING IN THE CHIMPANZEE
abstract: Clark (1961) demonstrated that chimpanzees can acquire and maintain avoidance behavior under the Sidman (free-operant) avoidance procedure. Six young male chimpanzees learned to postpone periodic shocks by pressing a lever, showing characteristic acquisition patterns similar to those observed in other species—initial bursts of responding followed by stabilized, well-spaced avoidance responses. Extended training produced reliable avoidance without general behavioral deterioration, suggesting that avoidance conditioning in chimpanzees follows principles consistent with operant processes across species.

title: OBSERVING RESPONSES IN PIGEONS
authors: Kelleher, Roger, T.; Riddle, W.C.; Cook, L.
process: stimulus-producing (observing) responses maintained by conditioned reinforcement under variable-ratio (VR) and extinction (EXT) conditions.
abstract: Pigeons were trained on an observing-response procedure in which periods of VR 100 and EXT alternated unpredictably during a white light (mixed stimulus). During VR 100, responses on a food-producing key were intermittently reinforced. Responses on an observing key produced a green light (positive stimulus) when VR 100 was in effect and a red light (negative stimulus) during EXT. The birds did not respond on either key during the negative stimulus but did respond on the food-producing key when the positive stimulus appeared. Observing responses were maintained under FR schedules up to a maximum ratio value, after which only food-producing responses occurred. When observing responses no longer produced stimuli, rates fell to zero. Prolonged exposure to FR 20 led to higher observing rates during EXT than VR 100. Chlorpromazine decreased overall responding but markedly increased observing-response rates, except during observing-response extinction sessions.

title: ON THE FORM OF LEARNING CURVES
process: ["the quantitative relation between cumulative performance and practice
modeled as a power law of learning describing response acceleration and asymptotic behavior in operant
abstract:
Across diverse learning experiments, cumulative performance measures (e.g., correct responses, time on target) consistently follow a power function of practice time or number of trials:

𝑛

Deviations from the power law occur mainly at early stages (reflecting prior skill or transfer). Changes in training conditions often alter the parameters
