title: THE CONDITIONING OF TEXTUAL RESPONSESUSING "EXTRINSIC" REINFORCERS
authors: Staats, A.W.; Staats, C.K.; Schutz, R.E.; Wolf, M.
abstract: Six 4-year-olds completed a programmed-text reading task that built from single words to sentences and short “stories.” In No-Reinforcement phases (social praise only), children quickly disengaged and text acquisition stalled. Introducing extrinsic reinforcement (edibles, small trinkets, and tokens exchangeable for prizes) restored engagement and produced steady gains in the number of new texts acquired per 45-min session, yielding learning curves analogous to operant conditioning records. Results suggest textual behavior in early reading can be effectively established and maintained with tangible/token reinforcement, with procedures (picture prompts, discrimination “loops”) that minimize guessing and bring responding under textual stimulus control.

title: VARIABLE-INTERVAL ESCAPE FROM STIMULIACCOMPANIED BY SHOCKS
process: Negative reinforcement via escape/avoidance maintained by conditioned aversive stimuli,
abstract: Three rats pressed a bar to terminate irregularly spaced shock series; a successful press produced a fixed “time-out” with no shocks. In experimental sessions, a tone or light accompanied the shock series; in control sessions, no exteroceptive signal was present though shock contingencies were identical. Stable escape responding was maintained by conditioned aversive stimulation: response rates were higher with signals than without, decreased as the mean inter-shock interval increased, and fell when time-outs were shorter than ~60 s. A reliable session “warm-up” (initial delay then acceleration) indicated that some shock exposure was required to establish the supporting state. Discrimination between shock vs. no-shock periods (relative responding) depended on the ratio of inter-shock interval to time-out duration.

title: EXTEROCEPTIVE CONTROL OF FIXED-INTERVALRESPONDING
process: Stimulus control and temporal discrimination under fixed-interval schedules
abstract: Two pigeons were studied under fixed-interval (FI) schedules of food reinforcement to examine how exteroceptive “clock” stimuli affect temporal control of responding. The visual stimuli (colored lights changing in fixed order) provided discrete temporal cues across the interval. When light changes were response-independent, the stimuli came to exert nearly complete control over responding—pigeons pecked almost exclusively during the final light period. When light changes were response-dependent, responding reflected both the discriminative and reinforcing effects of light changes, showing intermediate control. Results suggest strong stimulus control of temporal responding by exteroceptive cues, with evidence of additional temporal discrimination at longer intervals.

title: SOME OBSERVATIONS ON THE ADVENTITIOUSREINFORCEMENT OF DRINKING UNDER FOODREINFORCEMENT
abstract: Clark (1962) investigated the adventitious reinforcement of drinking in rats responding under variable-interval (VI) food schedules. Strong post-reinforcement drinking emerged rapidly when short VI intervals and close proximity between lever and water tube were present. Attempts to disrupt the behavior using conjunctive inter-response-time contingencies or fixed-ratio schedules were largely ineffective; the behavior returned under VI conditions. However, increasing the lever–water distance or reducing short intervals prevented its emergence. The study demonstrated that superstitious drinking, once established, is highly resistant to change and is governed by the temporal proximity of reinforcement and collateral responses.

title: GENERALIZATION AND DISCRIMINATION AS AFUNCTION OF THE SD-SA INTENSITY DIFFERENCE
process: ["Stimulus generalization and discrimination along an auditory intensity continuum"
abstract: Pierrel and Sherman (1962) examined auditory-intensity discrimination and generalization in rats by varying the difference between SD and SA intensities (10 dB, 20 dB, and 30 dB). Smaller SD–SA differences required longer training to achieve stable discrimination. During generalization tests with multiple SA intensities, response gradients initially peaked away from the SD and SA but later stabilized with maxima at the SD intensity. Gradients declined more steeply on the SA side, indicating asymmetrical stimulus control. The study demonstrated that the precision and shape of generalization gradients are inversely related to SD–SA separation magnitude.

title: THE EFFECT OF DRUGS ON A FIXED-RATIOPERFORMANCE SUPPRESSED BY A PRE-TIME-OUTSTIMULUSi1
authors: Ferster, C.B.; Appel, J.B.; Hiss, R.A.
abstract: Ferster, Appel, and Hiss (1962) studied how several drugs affected pigeons’ fixed-ratio (FR) responding when a pre-time-out stimulus (a red light) suppressed performance. During the red light, responses produced time-outs; withholding responses allowed uninterrupted completion of the FR schedule. Larger FRs produced stronger suppression (“FR strain”). Administration of amobarbital, pentobarbital, chlorpromazine, and d-amphetamine markedly increased responding under these strained conditions, restoring more typical FR patterns. High doses reduced rates only briefly post-injection. The results suggest these drugs disrupt suppressive control by the pre-time-out stimulus rather than create new behavior, effectively normalizing disrupted FR performance.

title: DISTRIBUTIONAL PROPERTIES OF OPERANT-LEVELLOCOMOTION IN THE RAT
process: Operant-level locomotion; effects of access limitation (motivating operation) on response patterning—primarily reducing interburst intervals (IBIs).
abstract: Four rats had continuous access to activity wheels first, then access for 1 hr per day, and, subsequently, continuous access. Limiting S's access to the wheel substantially increased the total frequency of running. A distributional analysis of response duration, burst duration, and interburst interval showed that the increased frequency arose almost entirely from a shortening of the interval between successive bursts. In contrast, speed of the individual response and number of responses per burst changed only negligibly. If S were running, the probability that it would either stop or continue did not differ appreciably for the conditions of continuous or limited access to the wheel. But if S were not running, the probability that it would start running was appreciably greater for limited than for continuous access.

title: CLASSICAL AVOIDANCE WITHOUT A WARNINGSTIMULUS
abstract: White rats were scheduled to be shocked every 15 sec; but they were given a limited time interval between shocks when they could prevent the next scheduled shock from occurring if they pressed a lever. The duration of this limited avoidance period was varied, as was its location within the interval between scheduled shocks. Response rate, shock frequency, and the temporal distribution of lever presses were examined. Conditions were generated in which the formation of a temporal discrimination prevented the animals from maintaining successful avoidance behavior.

title: EFFECTS OF dl-AMPHETAMINE UNDER CONCURRENTVI DRL REINFORCEMENT
abstract: Three adult, food-deprived rats were given IP injections of dl-amphetamine sulfate under DRL and concurrent VI DRL reinforcement schedules. The drug results were as follows: (1) The IRT distributions of DRL responses shifted to the left, but some temporal discrimination remained. (2) The IRT distributions of VI responses shifted slightly to the left. (3) The distinguishing characteristics of VI and DRL IRT distributions were preserved. (4) The frequency distribution of number of VI responses between two consecutive DRL responses was relatively unaffected. (5) Over-all response rates on the two components of the concurrent schedules increased more or less proportionately. These findings imply that the primary behavioral effect of dl-amphetamine was a motor excitatory one. The drug’s disruption of timing behavior was not due to a derangement of internal timing mechanisms, nor to interference with the topography or pattern of behavior. Rather, it might be a secondary result of the accelerated emission of overt behavior patterns mediating the temporal spacing of DRL bar presses.

title: STIMULUS FUNCTIONS IN CHAINED FIXED-INTERVALSCHEDULES
abstract: This study examined how different stimulus arrangements affect responding under a three-component chained fixed-interval (FI) schedule with pigeons. When a single stimulus was correlated with all components (a tandem schedule), response rates accelerated positively across intervals. When distinct stimuli were correlated with each component in a fixed order, birds paused for long durations in the first component, responded slowly in the second, and accelerated responding in the latter components. However, when the sequence of stimuli varied across trials, acceleration occurred in all components. Because the reinforcement contingencies were constant across procedures, the performance differences were attributed solely to the discriminative and conditioned reinforcing functions of the stimuli and their sequencing.

title: INDEPENDENCE OF CONCURRENT RESPONDINGMAINTAINED BY INTERVAL SCHEDULES OFREINFORCEMENT
process: Concurrent schedules of reinforcement and response independence.
abstract: Catania investigated how pigeons respond when two independent interval schedules operate concurrently on separate keys, comparing performance with and without a changeover delay (COD). Under conc VI 3 (mult VI 3 EXT) and conc VI 3 FI 6 arrangements, the COD prevented immediate reinforcement following a switch between keys. With the COD, each key maintained response patterns similar to when its schedule was programmed alone: steady responding under VI, high responding during the VI component of the multiple schedule, and scalloping under FI. Without the COD, behavior on one key often came under the control of reinforcers programmed for the other key, producing interaction and loss of characteristic interval performance. The study demonstrated that the COD promotes independence between concurrent responses and clarified relations among local response patterns, overall rate, and reinforcement frequency.

title: BEHAVIOR IN A NOVEL SITUATION AS INFLUENCED BYTHE IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING ENVIRONMENT'
process: Behavioral organization in novelty / effects of environmental history (stimulus complexity) on exploratory and self-maintenance responses.
abstract: Woods examined how rats’ behavior in a novel closed field (30″ square) depends on the immediately preceding 24-hr environment: free/complex, restricted, or standard lab cage. Using 5-sec time sampling over 24 minutes with exhaustive categories (sniffing, locomotion, grooming, freezing, lying/sitting, miscellaneous), he found large group differences. Sniffing generally declined over time, with a faster drop for animals coming from the free/complex environment; grooming increased over time, was more frequent in free-reared rats, and often showed cyclic waxing–waning. Notably, only free-reared animals sometimes lay down/slept in the final 9 minutes. Restricted and lab-cage rats showed briefer, more frequent behavioral shifts (shorter runs), whereas free-reared rats produced longer bouts of non-sniffing behavior later in the session. The study highlights immediate environmental history (especially stimulus complexity) as a determinant of behavioral organization in novelty and advocates time-sampling for profiling whole-organism behavior.

title: A FACILITATIVE EFFECT OF PUNISHMENTON UNPUNISHED BEHAVIOR
abstract: Two pigeons pecked under a multiple schedule with alternating 3-min red and green components; both components arranged VI 3-min food. During several phases, every peck in red produced electric shock (≈20 ms AC; intensities 1.35, 2.5, 3.6 mA). Punishment in red reliably decreased punished responding and produced behavioral contrast: the unpunished VI rate in green increased, with larger increases at higher shock intensities. When punishment was removed, red responding showed a transient overshoot and green rates declined, then recovered depending on conditions. Replacing red VI with extinction while removing punishment yielded a decrease then increase in green responding, separating effects of shock from reinforcement frequency. Local patterns showed high green rates early in the component with later within-component suppression.

title: SOME EFFECTS OF RESPONSE COST UPONHUMAN OPERANT BEHAVIOR
process: Effects of response cost/punishment on schedule-controlled performance; induction of FI temporal patterning and behavioral contrast-like adjustments via negative contingencies.
abstract: Across three experiments with human observers detecting brief visual signals, Weiner added a per-response point loss (“cost”) to VI and FI schedules that delivered 100 points per correct detection. Under VI (1, 3, 9 min), cost reliably reduced response rates while largely preserving the VI-typical constancy; effects held regardless of whether cost or no-cost came first. Under FI 1-min, FI scalloping emerged only with cost; without cost, responding was approximately constant within intervals (VI-like). Cost effects were reversible: removing cost restored prior no-cost baselines. By alternately scheduling cost/no-cost within FI intervals (e.g., 30-s or 15-s segments), the study produced classic FI irregularities (bites, knees, double scallops, run-throughs). Overall, response cost is a potent determinant of human schedule performance.

title: INTERLOCKING SCHED ULES OF REINFORCEMENT
abstract: Four male pigmented rats were exposed to a procedure designed to investigate the relation between several performance measures and a schedule continuum ranging from FR 36 to FI 2 through four intermediate interlocking schedules. On all schedules, each subject developed a stable, generally break-and-run performance. Analysis of cumulative records, post-reinforcement pauses, and running rates showed a continuum of performance related to the schedule continuum.

title: DELAYED ALTERNATION IN THE PIGEON
abstract: Pigeons were studied in a delayed-response task requiring alternation of key pecks on two response keys, with blackout delays of 1–10 seconds between choices. Accuracy remained well above chance at all delays, though it was generally lowest at 1 and 10 seconds. Postural orientation during delays appeared to mediate accurate responding. The shape of the delay–accuracy function was discussed in terms of potential confounds involving stimulus traces and aversive time-out effects.

title: INTERACTIONS BETWEEN THE DISCRIMINA TIVEAND AVERSIVE PROPERTIES OF PUNISHMENT
process: Discriminative control and aversive control of punishment on FI schedules
abstract: On FI schedules, the authors arranged response-contingent electric shock in specific quarters of each interval to disentangle punishment’s discriminative vs. aversive functions. Mild shocks, when paired with extinction portions, acted as S^Δ and suppressed responding; when paired with reinforcement portions, they acted as S^D and elevated responding. As shock intensity increased, aversiveness amplified suppression in extinction-paired periods and attenuated (but did not abolish) enhancement in reinforcement-paired periods. Termination of S^Δ punishment produced compensatory post-S^Δ response bursts, unlike a neutral S^Δ (green light). Thus, punishment’s effect depends on both its discriminative history and aversive magnitude.

title: FOOD SATIATION IN THE PIGEON
process: Motivational control of operant responding by food deprivation and satiation (satiation functions and deprivation effects).
abstract: Eight pigeons were permitted to eat to satiation after systematically increasing deprivation intervals (5–191 hr), and two others were tested at prescribed percentages of free-feeding weight. Across 110 sessions, most satiation curves were linear with abrupt stops (≈64%), fewer were linear with gradual stops, and none were fully curvilinear as in Skinner’s rat data. Pecking rates were constant until eating ceased abruptly at about 85% of free-feeding weight. Weight data showed predictable losses with deprivation and partial recovery after each satiation, with a consistent separation between pre- and post-feeding weights.

title: REDUCTION OF SHOCK FREQUENCY ASREINFORCEMENT FOR A VOIDANCE BEHA VIOR
process: ["Free-operant avoidance maintained by negative reinforcement via shock-frequency reduction"
abstract: Rats had access to two levers, each controlling its own free-operant avoidance schedule (independent shock–shock and response–shock timers). Many animals initially pressed one lever predominantly, which already cut overall shock density roughly in half; alternation across levers further reduced shock frequency. Conditioning occurred rapidly when the shock–shock interval was short (e.g., 5 s), and changing the response–shock interval on one lever shifted responding to the other, with effects reversing when schedules were restored. Inter-response-time functions showed temporal discrimination (low short-pause responding, rising with time). Findings support “reduction of shock frequency” as the primary reinforcer in avoidance.

title: EFFECTS OF CHLORPROMAZINE ON APPETITIVE ANDAVERSIVE COMPONENTS OF A MULTIPLE SCHEDULE'
process: Multiple schedule performance under concurrent appetitive
abstract: A multiple schedule with both appetitive (food) and avoidance (shock) components was maintained in two dogs to study the effects of chlorpromazine (CPZ). Both soluble and sustained-release (“Spansule”) forms of CPZ produced comparable dose–response functions despite differing dosing procedures. Low doses slightly increased response rates on food-reinforced components while leaving avoidance responding largely unchanged. Higher doses depressed responding on both components approximately equally. The study discusses interpretive issues in comparing drug effects across schedules and between appetitive and aversive contingencies.

title: THE QUAIL, Coturnix coturnix, AS A LABORATORY ANIMAL
process: ["Acquisition and maintenance of operant behavior (pecking) under food reinforcement
abstract: The quail Coturnix coturnix is described as an ideal laboratory bird: small, hardy, docile, inexpensive, and easy to maintain. Reese and Reese demonstrated that the pecking operant can be conditioned rapidly with minimal deprivation and that quail learn visual and temporal discriminations with remarkable speed. They adapted well to laboratory conditions, showed stable responding despite distractions, and could perform under continuous, fixed-ratio, and fixed-interval schedules. Their rapid maturation and tolerance for short deprivation periods make them suitable for operant conditioning research, comparative studies, and teaching demonstrations.

title: AN ADJUSTING AVOIDANCE SCHEDULE'
process: Avoidance conditioning under variable response–shock contingencies
abstract: Sidman developed an “adjusting avoidance” schedule in which each lever press postponed shock by 5 s, allowing rats to accumulate up to 50 s of safety. This design eliminated differential reinforcement for long pauses, resulting in response probabilities largely independent of elapsed time since the last press. When a warning stimulus signaled proximity to shock (≤ 5 s), rats increased responding near the warning period and spent more time closer to shock, demonstrating control by temporal cues. The procedure produced rapid conditioning and revealed that shock-frequency reduction, rather than temporal discrimination alone, governs avoidance behavior.

title: PROGRAMMING STIMULI IN MATCHING TO SAMPLE
process: discrimination learning and transfer of stimulus control
abstract: Preschool and first-grade children learned matching-to-sample using a teaching-machine program that introduced a sequence of gradually harder discriminations. Training with these successive approximations was far more efficient than training on the final (criterion) discrimination alone. Error rates depended jointly on the “step size” between consecutive discriminations and on how long children trained at each step. The work highlights how programming stimulus control (and when to advance steps) governs errors and efficiency, with implications for both theory (continuity/discontinuity, stimulus control) and instructional design.

title: RESPONSE LATENCY AS A FUNCTION OFREINFORCEMENT SCHEDULE1
abstract: "Four rats were trained to press and hold a key during a light and release it upon onset of a tone, with water reinforcement following correct releases. Reinforcement schedules were systematically varied (CRF, intermittent, extinction). Median response latency and its variability were inversely related to reinforcement frequency: lower reinforcement probabilities produced longer and more variable reaction times. Extinction produced the greatest latency increases, suggesting a graded relationship between reinforcement density and response speed stability."]

title: RESPONSE LATENCY AS A FUNCTION OF AMOUNTOF REINFORCEMENT
abstract: Four food-deprived rats were trained to press and hold a telegraph key in the presence of a light and to release it upon hearing a tone, which produced a sucrose solution as reinforcement. After establishing a stable baseline with 20% sucrose, concentrations were reduced to 5% and 0%. Decreases in sucrose concentration produced systematic increases in both the median and variability of response latency, with the greatest change at 0%. These results indicate that response latency is inversely related to the amount of reinforcement, paralleling effects previously observed for reinforcement frequency.

title: THE OPERANT CONDITIONING OF CONVERSATION
process: Social operant conditioning of sequential behavior
abstract: Four experiments tested whether the order of speakers in small-group conversation can be operantly conditioned. Triads (and one tetrad) discussed tasks requiring unanimous choices; “correct” feedback (reinforcement) was delivered contingent on specific conversational sequences (e.g., last two speakers in clockwise order, or ABC among three). Reinforcement increased target sequences above chance; in later studies with improved procedures (tones for feedback, one-way screen, colored-light guessing task), a single two-person subsequence (e.g., B→C) typically stabilized and dominated, suggesting shaping of a specific social response rather than a general positional rule. Observer reliability improved with higher reinforcement density. Results support that conversational turn order is a modifiable operant under group reinforcement.

title: EFFECTS OF OPERANT REINFORCEMENT ON THE GSR1
authors: Mandler, G.; Preven, D.W.; Kuhlman, C.K.
process: Attempted operant conditioning of an autonomic respondent (GSR); observed effect aligns more with stimulus-controlled arousal/activation than response-contingent reinforcement.
abstract: Nine undergraduates completed 11 daily sessions measuring GSR (≥500-ohm drops in ≤2 s). Sessions 2–9 included a 10-min no-reinforcement control followed by 20-min periods in which each qualifying GSR turned on a red light (putative operant reinforcer). Sessions 10–11 were extinction. Across subjects, there was no orderly acquisition indicating operant conditioning of the GSR. However, GSR rates were reliably higher during reinforcement than control for participants with low baseline (operant) GSR rates (“stabiles”), but not for those with high baselines (“labiles”). Conductance (arousal) rose during control, spiked at reinforcement onset, then remained elevated, and rose further during extinction, suggesting the reinforcement period functioned as a contextual activator rather than a contingent reinforcer of GSR.

title: A TRACKING PROCEDURE FOR DETERMININGTHE CAT'S FREQUENCY DISCRIMINATION
authors: Elliot, D.N.; Frazier, L.; Riach, W.
process: Operant discrimination learning under a feedback (tracking
abstract: Cats were trained to discriminate between a steady 1000 Hz tone and a 3 Hz frequency-modulated (“warbling”) tone using a feedback (tracking) operant conditioning procedure. Pressing one of two bars during the steady tone produced food on a variable-ratio (VR) schedule; pressing the other during the warble changed the tone back to steady. Early training showed poor auditory stimulus control because of rapid, indiscriminate responding. Introducing inter-press time-outs (TOs) and light cues signaling the correct bar and TO periods markedly improved discrimination. Although the method required many sessions, it minimized the “experimental neurosis” seen in avoidance conditioning and may be superior for long-term auditory threshold studies.

title: FI LENGTH AND PERFORMANCE ON AN FIFR CHAIN SCHEDULE OF REINFORCEMENT
authors: Hanson, H.M.; Cambell, E.H.; Witoslawski, J.J.
process: Fixed-interval (FI) temporal control within a chained schedule of reinforcement.
abstract: In a chained FI–FR schedule, manipulating the length of the FI component produced changes confined almost entirely to the FI performance; increasing the interval length increased the total number of responses emitted per reinforcement. The configuration of the fixed-interval scallop was clearly modified as the interval length was increased, with the larger intervals becoming flatter (i.e., a larger proportion of the total responses earlier in an interval). Measurement of the postreinforcement pause is suggested as a possible indicator of fixed-interval scalloping.

title: PRODUCING EITHER POSITIVE OR NEGATIVETENDENCIES TO A STIMULUS ASSOCIATED WITH SHOCK
abstract: A technique is described in which rats are pretrained with reinforcement to bar press. Each bar press was associated with a tone. This tone was later paired with one of two aspects of an electric shock—either at the onset or at the end of the shock—in a situation in which the shock was inescapable. These animals were retested in the operant situation under extinction, but with the tone present as a conditioned reinforcer. Animals for which the tone was associated with shock onset extinguished quickly, whereas those for which the tone was paired with shock termination extinguished more slowly.

title: CONTROL OF THE BEHAVIOR OF SCHIZOPHRENICPATIENTS BY FOOD'
process: ["Operant conditioning with food reinforcement: extinction of socially maintained problem behavior
and establishment of cooperative responding via programmed contingencies."
abstract: Ayllon & Haughton (1962) used operant procedures to control behavior of 45 chronic psychiatric inpatients on a closed ward by making food access contingent on specific responses. First, they withdrew all social attention for “refusal to eat,” which rapidly eliminated eating problems. They then added response requirements: a motor act (deposit a coin to enter the dining room) and later a social/cooperative act (two patients simultaneously press buttons to earn coins). With a 5-min access window, patients reliably acquired and maintained the chains without spoon/tube feeding or coercion; weights and health were largely stable. The study demonstrates that inadvertent social reinforcement can maintain “refusal to eat,” and that food functions as a powerful reinforcer to shape motor and social behavior in this population.

title: QUANTITA TIVE RELA TIONS BETWEEN A VOIDANCEBEHAVIOR AND PITUITARY-ADRENALCORTICAL ACTIVITY1
authors: Sidman, M.; Mason, J.W.; Brady, J.V.; Thach, J.
process: with parametric control via response–shock intervals and free-shock probes."
abstract: Sidman, Mason, Brady & Thach (1962) studied rhesus monkeys avoiding shock via lever pressing while measuring plasma 17-hydroxycorticosteroids (indexing pituitary–adrenal activity). Manipulating avoidance parameters showed steroid elevations covaried with both behavior and shock exposure. Shorter response–shock intervals increased avoidance rates and typically raised steroids; adding warning stimuli (discriminated avoidance) lowered response rates and steroid rises. Superimposing unavoidable “free” shocks increased both response rate and steroids; removing them reduced both. In extinction with constant free shocks (every 2 min), steroids declined as responding ceased, but surged when shock rate was briefly increased (every 20 s). Reinstating high-rate discriminated avoidance without shocks again elevated steroids. Thus, adrenal activation depends jointly on avoidance response rate and shock frequency.

title: MACHINE DEFINITION OF ONGOING SILENT ANDORAL READING RATE'
process: Observing/monitoring responses (a form of operant-controlled attending to textual stimuli).
abstract: Goldiamond (1962) introduced an apparatus that operationalizes ongoing silent and oral reading as a sequence of “monitoring responses.” A projected page is masked by an opaque loop with a moving transparent slit; each press of a microswitch advances the slit to reveal successive text segments (≈half-line frames). The device thereby defines reading rate as presses per time and brings it under experimental control. With this method, reading/monitoring rates proved highly stable and were sensitive to manipulations including item difficulty, signal-to-noise (defocus), schedule-based payoffs (e.g., DRL contingencies), age, and practice. The same framework generalizes to other visual monitoring tasks (e.g., complex displays), enabling training of selective attention to different regions of a stimulus.
